ctw.intro: Online shopping has dramatically changed the way ordinary families buy almost everything they need.
ctw.text: With a few taps, customers can {com:pare} prices, read reviews, and {or:der} goods from distant warehouses. Purchases arrive at the front {do:or} within days, saving shoppers time and {ef:fort}. Retailers collect detailed data to {predi:ct} what each buyer might want {ne:xt}. However, the convenience tempts many people to {over:spend} on things they never truly {ne:eded}. Analysts also worry about the environmental cost of packaging and rapid {deli:very} across the whole {indu:stry}.

ctw.title: Museums Go Virtual
ctw.intro: Many famous museums now invite visitors to explore their galleries without ever leaving home.
ctw.text: Using a computer or phone, anyone can {wan:der} through detailed virtual {gal:leries} at any time. High-resolution images let viewers {zo:om} in on tiny brushstrokes that are hard to see in {per:son}. Curators add audio guides to {expl:ain} the history behind each {tre:asure}. These digital tours make world-famous art {access:ible} to students who could never {aff:ord} to travel abroad. Some experts worry, though, that screens can never fully {cap:ture} the magic of a real {gal:lery}.

ctw.title: Giving Back
ctw.intro: Universities increasingly expect their students to spend time volunteering in the surrounding community.
ctw.text: Young people {tut:or} struggling children, serve meals at {shel:ters}, and help clean local {par:ks}. Such service teaches practical skills while building genuine {empa:thy} for others. Volunteers often {disco:ver} social problems they had never {noti:ced} before. Employers value this experience because it shows {commit:ment} and the ability to {coope:rate} with very different people. Many graduates say the friendships and lessons gained through service {sha:pe} their values for the rest of their {li:ves}.

daily_life.title: Study Group Tonight
daily_life.body:
Maya: Hey! Still on for the study group tonight?
Jordan: Ugh, I totally forgot. I have a shift til 8 :(
Maya: No worries. We're starting at 8:30 anyway.
Jordan: Perfect. Can u send me the notes from Tuesday?
Maya: Sure. Also we're meeting at the coffee shop, NOT the library this time.
Jordan: Got it. Thx! I'll grab a table.
Maya: Cool, see u there. Don't be late lol

questions.q: Why does Jordan say 'I totally forgot'?
options: He does not want to join the study group
He had not remembered the study group was tonight
He forgot to bring his notes
He forgot where the coffee shop is
questions.why: 由「Still on for tonight?」後回「I totally forgot」可知他忘了今晚有讀書會。

questions.q: Where will they meet tonight?
questions.why: Maya 強調「at the coffee shop, NOT the library this time」。

questions.q: What does 'Thx' most likely mean?
questions.why: 「Thx」為 Thanks 的縮寫,回應 Maya 幫忙傳筆記。

To: BIO 210 Students

Hi everyone,

Prof. Delgado

Because the website was not working

academic.title: The Birth of Standard Time
academic.passage: Before the 1880s, towns across the United States kept their own local time, setting clocks by the position of the sun. When it was noon in one city, it might be 12:12 in a town just a short distance to the east. For centuries this caused little trouble, since people rarely traveled far or fast. The spread of railroads, however, made the confusion impossible to ignore. Each railroad company followed its own clock, and a single station might display several different times at once. Passengers missed trains, and schedules became a puzzle that few could solve. In 1883, the major railroad companies agreed on a solution. They divided the country into four zones, each one hour apart, and reset their clocks to match. The public did not vote on the change; the railroads simply adopted it, and most towns quickly followed because doing business required it. Some critics complained that companies had no right to decide something as basic as time. Yet the convenience was so great that resistance faded within a few years. The federal government did not officially confirm the zones until 1918, long after they had already become part of everyday American life.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did towns set their clocks before the 1880s?
options: By following the nearest railroad company's clock
By using the position of the sun
By waiting for a federal government signal
By copying the time in the largest city
questions.why: 第一句「setting clocks by the position of the sun」直接說明。

questions.q: The word 'confusion' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: 由多家鐵路各自計時導致的混亂,confusion 最接近 disorder。

questions.q: What can be inferred about travel before the spread of railroads?
options: It was mostly done for business reasons
It required knowing several different clocks
It was slow enough that time differences rarely mattered
It was more common than travel today
questions.why: 「people rarely traveled far or fast」故時差影響不大,可推知旅行慢到時差無妨。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that 'a single station might display several different times at once'?
options: To show how confusing the old system had become
To praise the railroads for offering choices
To explain how sunlight was measured
To prove that trains were usually late
questions.why: 此例用來說明舊制混亂到難以忽視,呼應前句 confusion。

questions.q: What is the author's main purpose in the passage?
options: To argue that the government should control time
To describe how and why standard time zones were adopted
To compare train travel in different countries
To explain how clocks measure the position of the sun
questions.why: 全文敘述時區制度出現的原因與過程,屬說明性目的。

academic.title: The Discovery of Perspective
academic.passage: For most of human history, painters struggled to make flat surfaces appear deep. Medieval European artists often placed important figures in the center and drew them larger than everything else, regardless of where they stood. A king might tower over a distant mountain simply because he mattered more. These paintings could be beautiful, but they did not look like the world the eye actually sees. During the early 1400s in Florence, an architect named Filippo Brunelleschi worked out a mathematical method now called linear perspective. The idea was simple but powerful: parallel lines, such as the edges of a road, appear to meet at a single point on the horizon. Objects shrink in a regular, predictable way as they move farther from the viewer. By following these rules, a painter could create a convincing illusion of depth on a flat wall or panel. The technique spread rapidly among Italian artists, who treated it almost as a science. Perspective did more than improve realism; it changed how people thought about space itself. A painting was no longer a flat decoration but a window opening onto an imagined world. Later artists would sometimes break these rules on purpose, but only because Brunelleschi had first shown how to follow them.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did medieval European artists often decide the size of a figure?
options: By how far away the figure stood
By how important the figure was
By the mathematical rules of perspective
By the color the figure wore
questions.why: 「drew them larger ... because he mattered more」表示依重要性決定大小。

questions.q: The word 'convincing' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: convincing illusion 指令人信服的深度錯覺,最接近 believable。

questions.q: What can be inferred about paintings made before linear perspective?
options: They were painted only by architects
They always placed figures near the horizon
They did not show depth in a realistic way
questions.why: 文中說舊畫「did not look like the world the eye actually sees」,可推知缺乏寫實深度。

questions.q: Why does the author mention the edges of a road in the second part of the passage?
options: To give an example of parallel lines meeting at one point
To show that roads were a popular subject for painters
To explain why Brunelleschi was an architect
To prove that perspective was difficult to learn
questions.why: 道路邊緣是「parallel lines ... meet at a single point」的具體例子。

questions.q: Why does the author state that later artists broke the rules 'only because Brunelleschi had first shown how to follow them'?
options: To criticize later artists for being lazy
To suggest that perspective was eventually forgotten
To emphasize how important Brunelleschi's method was
To argue that rules in art should never be broken
questions.why: 此句強調後人破格也建立在他先確立規則之上,凸顯其重要性。

academic.title: The Loss Leader
academic.passage: Walk into almost any large supermarket and you will find a few items priced surprisingly low - a gallon of milk, perhaps, or a roast chicken sold for less than it cost the store to make. At first this seems like a mistake. Why would a business deliberately lose money on the things it sells? The answer lies in a strategy known as the loss leader. A loss leader is a product priced below cost in order to attract customers into the store. The store does not expect to profit from that single item. Instead, it counts on shoppers buying many other products once they are inside. A customer who comes for cheap milk may leave with bread, coffee, and a magazine, all sold at a normal markup. The small loss on the milk is easily covered by the profit on everything else. This strategy works best when the discounted item is something people buy often and can easily compare across stores. Shoppers rarely remember the price of every product, but they do notice when milk or eggs are cheaper than at a rival. Placement matters too. Stores usually put loss leaders at the back, forcing customers to walk past shelves of tempting, full-priced goods before they reach the bargain.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is a loss leader?
options: A product priced below cost to attract customers
A manager who leads the sales team
An item that a store has failed to sell
The most profitable product in a store
questions.why: 文中定義「priced below cost in order to attract customers」。

questions.q: The word 'markup' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: 「sold at a normal markup」指正常加價,即高於成本的加成。

questions.q: Why do stores place loss leaders at the back of the store?
options: Because those items are hard to keep fresh
So customers must pass many full-priced goods first
Because customers prefer shopping at the back
To hide the low prices from rival stores
questions.why: 末句說此舉「forcing customers to walk past ... full-priced goods」,可推知目的。

questions.q: Why does the author describe a customer leaving with 'bread, coffee, and a magazine'?
options: To show the store lost money on those items too
To prove that milk is the most popular product
To illustrate how the store makes back its loss on other goods
To suggest customers forget what they came to buy
questions.why: 此例說明顧客購買其他正常加價商品彌補牛奶虧損。

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the passage?
options: To warn shoppers against buying cheap milk
To explain why some stores sell certain items below cost
To compare supermarkets with other kinds of stores
To describe how milk and eggs are produced
questions.why: 全文旨在解釋「賠本商品」策略及其運作原因。

academic.title: How Birds Find Their Way
academic.passage: Every autumn, billions of birds leave their northern homes and fly thousands of kilometers to warmer regions. Many travel at night, crossing oceans and deserts where there are no landmarks to guide them. How these animals find their way has puzzled scientists for generations, and the answer appears to involve not one tool but several. Some birds rely on the sun, using its position to hold a steady direction during the day. Others watch the pattern of stars at night, much as early human sailors once did. Perhaps the most surprising ability is the detection of Earth's magnetic field. Tiny particles in a bird's body seem to respond to this field, giving the animal a kind of built-in compass that works even under a cloudy sky. Young birds making their first journey are especially remarkable. In many species, they travel without any experienced adult to follow, yet still reach a destination they have never seen. This suggests that at least part of the route is inherited rather than learned. Scientists continue to study how these separate senses are combined, but it is already clear that migration depends on a flexible system, one that allows a bird to switch methods when clouds, weather, or landscape hide its usual signs.

questions.q: According to the passage, which of the following do some birds use to navigate at night?
questions.why: 「Others watch the pattern of stars at night」明說夜間靠星象。

questions.q: The word 'detection' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: detection of Earth's magnetic field 指察覺磁場,最接近 sensing。

questions.q: What does the behavior of young birds suggest about migration?
options: It must be taught by older birds
Part of the route is inherited, not learned
It only works in clear weather
It is the same for every species
questions.why: 幼鳥無成鳥帶領仍能抵達,文中說「inherited rather than learned」。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that a bird's compass 'works even under a cloudy sky'?
options: To show a limitation of using the stars and sun
To prove that birds prefer cloudy weather
To explain why birds travel only at night
To argue that magnetic fields are weak
questions.why: 此句對比日月星辰在陰天失效,凸顯磁場感知的優勢。

questions.q: What is the author's main point in the passage?
options: Birds migrate only because of cold weather
Bird migration relies on several combined methods, not just one
Young birds are better navigators than adults
Scientists have fully explained how birds navigate
questions.why: 全文論證候鳥導航「involve not one tool but several」的彈性系統。

academic.title: Why Ice Floats
academic.passage: Most substances become denser as they cool, so that their solid form sinks in their own liquid. Water breaks this rule in a way that has enormous consequences for life on Earth. As liquid water cools, it grows denser - but only down to about four degrees Celsius. Below that temperature, it begins to expand again, and when it finally freezes, the resulting ice is lighter than the water beneath it. That is why ice cubes float and why a frozen pond has a solid lid rather than a solid bottom. The reason lies in the shape of the water molecule and the way its molecules link together. As water freezes, each molecule bonds with its neighbors in an open, six-sided pattern. This arrangement holds the molecules slightly farther apart than they are in liquid water, so the same number of molecules takes up more space. More space for the same mass means lower density. This unusual behavior protects underwater life through the winter. Because ice forms on top, it acts as a blanket, slowing the loss of heat from the water below. Fish and other organisms can survive in the liquid water that remains beneath the frozen surface. If ice sank instead, lakes would freeze solid from the bottom up, and many would never fully thaw.

questions.q: According to the passage, at what temperature does water stop growing denser as it cools?
questions.why: 「denser - but only down to about four degrees Celsius」明確指出。

questions.q: The word 'enormous' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: enormous consequences 指極大的影響,最接近 very great。

questions.q: What can be inferred about most substances other than water?
options: Their solid form floats in their liquid form
Their solid form sinks in their liquid form
They expand when they are cooled
questions.why: 首句說多數物質冷卻變密、固態下沉,水才是例外。

questions.q: Why does the author compare ice to a 'blanket'?
options: To show that ice is soft and light
To suggest that ice keeps fish warm by touching them
To explain that ice slows heat loss from the water below
To describe the color of a frozen pond
questions.why: blanket 比喻冰層「slowing the loss of heat」,保住下方液態水。

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the passage?
options: To explain why water and its ice behave unusually and why it matters
To describe how fish breathe underwater in winter
To compare the density of many different liquids
To argue that lakes should not be allowed to freeze
questions.why: 全文解釋水結冰反常的原因及其對生命的重要性。

academic.title: The Unreliable Witness
academic.passage: People often imagine that human memory works like a video camera, faithfully recording events and playing them back on demand. Research in psychology, however, suggests that memory is far more active and far less reliable than this image implies. Rather than storing a perfect copy of an event, the mind reconstructs it each time we remember, filling in gaps with expectations, assumptions, and information gathered after the fact. This has serious consequences in courtrooms, where eyewitness testimony can send a person to prison. In a well-known series of experiments, participants watched a film of a car accident and were later asked questions about it. When researchers asked how fast the cars were going when they 'smashed' into each other, people gave higher speed estimates than when the milder word 'hit' was used. A single word had reshaped their memory of the same event. Even more striking, some participants later 'remembered' seeing broken glass at the scene, although there had been none in the film. Their memories had absorbed the suggestion contained in the question itself. Findings like these do not mean witnesses are dishonest. They mean that honest, confident people can remember things that never happened - a fact that legal systems around the world are still learning to take seriously.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happened when researchers used the word 'smashed' instead of 'hit'?
questions.why: 「'smashed' ... gave higher speed estimates than ... 'hit'」直接對比。

questions.q: The word 'reconstructs' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: reconstructs 指每次回憶都重新建構記憶,最接近 rebuilds。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the fact that some participants 'remembered' broken glass?
options: The film actually contained broken glass
A question can add false details to a memory
The participants were trying to lie
Broken glass is common in car accidents
questions.why: 影片並無碎玻璃卻被記得,可推知問題本身植入了假細節。

questions.q: Why does the author compare memory to a video camera at the beginning?
options: To prove that cameras are more accurate than people
To present a common belief the passage then challenges
To explain how films of accidents are made
To argue that people should record important events
questions.why: 攝影機比喻是先立「常見迷思」,後文再加以推翻。

questions.q: What is the author's main purpose in the passage?
options: To show that eyewitnesses usually lie in court
To explain that memory is reconstructive and can be unreliable
To describe how car accidents are studied
To argue that courtrooms should ban all testimony
questions.why: 全文旨在說明記憶會重構且可能失真,而非攝影機式重播。

academic.title: How Leaves Capture Sunlight
academic.passage:
Plants may appear still and passive, but inside their leaves an extraordinary chemical process is constantly at work. This process, called photosynthesis, allows plants to convert sunlight into usable energy. Within the leaf are tiny structures known as chloroplasts, which contain a green pigment called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll absorbs light most strongly in the red and blue parts of the spectrum, while reflecting green light, which is why leaves appear green to our eyes.

When sunlight strikes the chlorophyll, its energy is used to split water molecules drawn up from the roots. This reaction releases oxygen, which escapes into the air through small openings on the leaf surface. At the same time, the plant absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Using the captured energy, it combines carbon dioxide and hydrogen to build sugars, which store energy in their chemical bonds.

These sugars fuel nearly every activity the plant performs, from growing new stems to producing flowers. Just as importantly, the oxygen released during photosynthesis sustains almost all animal life on Earth. In this way, a quiet leaf becomes a bridge between the energy of the Sun and the living systems that depend on it. Without this hidden machinery, the planet would look utterly different.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do most leaves appear green?
options: Chlorophyll reflects green light while absorbing other colors
Chloroplasts turn green only when sunlight is strongest
Green light carries more energy than red or blue light
The sugars made in the leaf slowly stain it green
questions.why: 文中說葉綠素反射綠光、吸收其他顏色的光，故葉子看似綠色。

questions.q: The word "sustains" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: sustains 意為「維持、支撐」，與 supports 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a plant kept in complete darkness would
options: release much more oxygen than usual
absorb carbon dioxide far more quickly
be unable to build the sugars it needs
reflect red and blue light instead of green
questions.why: 沒有光就無法行光合作用，故無法製造所需的糖分。

questions.q: Which statement best describes the relationship the passage draws between sunlight and sugars?
options: Sugars are produced first and then give off sunlight
Sunlight destroys the sugars stored in the roots
Sunlight and sugar production are unrelated events
Sunlight supplies the energy used to build sugars
questions.why: 文中說陽光提供能量以合成糖，兩者是能量來源與產物的關係。

questions.q: Why does the author call a leaf "a bridge between the energy of the Sun and the living systems that depend on it"?
options: To stress that photosynthesis links sunlight to life
To argue that leaves are stronger than most bridges
To show that sunlight passes through leaves unchanged
To suggest that animals could survive without plants
questions.why: 作者用「橋樑」比喻，強調光合作用把陽光與地球生命連結起來。

academic.title: How a Rainbow Forms
academic.passage:
A rainbow is one of the most familiar sights in nature, yet its formation depends on precise physical laws. Rainbows appear when sunlight passes through raindrops still floating in the air, usually after a shower when the Sun is low in the sky. Although sunlight looks white, it is actually a mixture of many colors, each traveling as a wave of a slightly different length.

When a ray of sunlight enters a raindrop, it slows down and bends, a process called refraction. Because each color bends by a slightly different amount, the colors begin to spread apart inside the drop. The light then reflects off the back of the raindrop and bends once more as it leaves. By the time it exits, the single beam has been separated into a band of colors, from red on the outer edge to violet on the inner edge.

We see a rainbow only when the Sun is behind us and the rain is in front. Each raindrop sends a slightly different color toward our eyes, so the rainbow we observe is actually built from countless drops working together. This is also why no two people ever see exactly the same rainbow: the precise angle depends entirely on where the observer happens to stand.

questions.q: The word "precise" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: precise 意為「精確的」，與 exact 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that two people standing in different places will
options: see rainbows of completely different colors
see the very same rainbow at the same angle
each see a slightly different rainbow
be unable to see any rainbow at all
questions.why: 彩虹角度取決於觀察者位置，故不同地點的人看到略為不同的彩虹。

questions.q: According to the passage, the colors of sunlight spread apart because
options: raindrops remove some colors from the light
the Sun sits low in the sky after a shower
white light holds no colors until it rains
each color bends by a slightly different amount
questions.why: 文中說各色光彎折的角度略有不同，因而分散開來。

questions.q: The author mentions that "no two people ever see exactly the same rainbow" mainly to
options: show that a rainbow depends on the observer's position
prove that rainbows are extremely rare events
explain why rainbows fade so quickly after rain
suggest that some colors are invisible to certain eyes
questions.why: 此句用以說明彩虹取決於觀察者所站的位置。

questions.q: According to the passage, where must the Sun be for us to see a rainbow?
questions.why: 文中說太陽須在觀察者背後，才看得到彩虹。

academic.title: How Birds Find Their Way
academic.passage:
Every autumn, millions of birds leave their breeding grounds and travel thousands of kilometers to warmer regions, only to return the following spring. How these travelers find their way across oceans and deserts, often at night, has puzzled scientists for centuries. Research now suggests that migrating birds rely on several different cues at once.

During the day, many species use the position of the Sun as a kind of compass, adjusting for the fact that it moves across the sky. On clear nights, some birds appear to read the patterns of the stars instead. Perhaps most remarkably, certain birds can sense the Earth's magnetic field, which acts as an invisible guide pointing toward the poles. Special cells in their eyes or beaks may allow them to detect this field directly.

Young birds making the journey for the first time seem to inherit a rough sense of direction and distance. Yet experience still matters: older birds that have traveled before make fewer mistakes and can correct their course when blown off track by storms. By combining inherited instinct with learned experience, migrating birds accomplish one of the most demanding feats in the natural world, guided by signals most humans cannot perceive at all.

questions.q: It can be inferred that a young bird migrating for the first time
options: cannot travel unless an older bird leads it
depends only on the stars to find its way
already has some built-in sense of direction
never makes any navigational mistakes at all
questions.why: 文中說幼鳥天生就繼承了粗略的方向與距離感。

questions.q: The passage suggests that older birds make fewer mistakes because they
options: are simply much stronger than younger birds
no longer rely on the Earth's magnetic field
travel only on clear nights with bright stars
can use past experience to correct their course
questions.why: 老鳥能憑過往經驗，在被風吹偏時修正路線。

questions.q: The author lists the Sun, the stars, and the magnetic field mainly to
options: show that birds combine several cues to navigate
argue that one cue is far better than the others
prove that birds cannot navigate on cloudy nights
compare bird navigation with human navigation tools
questions.why: 列舉太陽、星辰與磁場，是為說明鳥同時利用多種線索導航。

questions.q: According to the passage, how might some birds sense the Earth's magnetic field?
options: Through the feathers on their wings
Through special cells in their eyes or beaks
By measuring changes in the air temperature
By following the daily movement of the Sun
questions.why: 文中說某些鳥可能透過眼睛或喙中的特殊細胞感應磁場。

questions.q: The word "perceive" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: perceive 意為「察覺、感知」，與 detect 最接近。

academic.title: The Moving Plates of the Earth
academic.passage:
The ground beneath our feet feels perfectly solid, but on the scale of the whole planet, the Earth's outer shell is broken into enormous pieces called tectonic plates. These plates float on a layer of hot, slowly flowing rock, drifting no faster than a fingernail grows. Over millions of years, however, even such tiny movements reshape the surface of the planet.

Most of the world's earthquakes occur where two plates meet. In some places the plates grind past each other; in others, one plate is forced beneath its neighbor. As the rocks lock together, pressure builds up along the boundary. Eventually the strain becomes too great, the rocks suddenly slip, and energy is released as waves that shake the ground.

Because the same boundaries move again and again, earthquakes tend to strike the same regions repeatedly. Scientists cannot yet predict exactly when a particular quake will happen, but by mapping plate boundaries they can identify which areas face the greatest danger. This knowledge allows engineers to design stronger buildings and helps communities prepare. Understanding that the solid ground is in fact slowly moving has transformed the way we see our restless planet.

questions.q: According to the passage, earthquakes tend to strike the same regions repeatedly because
options: the buildings there are usually too weak
scientists choose to study those areas most
the plates in those areas have stopped moving
the same plate boundaries move again and again
questions.why: 因為相同的板塊邊界一再移動，故同一地區反覆發生地震。

questions.q: Why does the author compare plate movement to the growth of a fingernail?
options: To stress how extremely slowly the plates move
To suggest plates and nails share a material
To show that the plates grow larger over time
To argue that plate movement cannot be measured
questions.why: 以指甲生長作比，是為強調板塊移動極為緩慢。

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens just before an earthquake?
options: Two plates permanently join into one
Pressure builds up along a plate boundary
The rock beneath the plates cools down
Buildings begin to shake for no reason
questions.why: 文中說地震前，壓力沿板塊邊界逐漸累積。

questions.q: The word "restless" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: restless 意為「不停活動的」，與 always in motion 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that mapping plate boundaries is useful mainly because it helps people
options: predict the exact moment of a quake
slow down the movement of the plates
learn which areas are most at risk
questions.why: 繪製板塊邊界，有助判斷哪些地區風險最高。

academic.title: The Hidden Partners of Coral Reefs
academic.passage:
A coral reef looks like a colorful garden of rock, but it is in fact built by tiny living animals called coral polyps. Each polyp is soft-bodied and no larger than a grain of rice, yet together they construct some of the largest structures ever made by living things. The secret to their success lies in a remarkable partnership.

Inside the tissues of most reef-building corals live microscopic algae. The algae capture sunlight and, through photosynthesis, produce sugars that feed the coral. In return, the coral gives the algae a safe place to live and supplies the raw materials they need. Thanks to this exchange, corals can thrive even in warm, clear waters where food is otherwise scarce.

This partnership, however, is delicate. When the surrounding water becomes too warm, the corals expel the algae living inside them and turn a ghostly white, an event known as bleaching. Without their algal partners, the corals lose their main source of food and may soon starve. Because reefs shelter a quarter of all marine species, their health matters far beyond the reef itself. Protecting these fragile partnerships has therefore become one of the central concerns of ocean science.

questions.q: The author describes a coral reef as "a colorful garden of rock" mainly to
options: highlight the contrast between its look and its builders
explain why coral reefs are often found near gardens
argue that reefs grow in the same way plants do
suggest that reefs are prettier than real gardens
questions.why: 此比喻凸顯珊瑚礁外表如岩石、實為活體生物所建的反差。

questions.q: According to the passage, what do the algae give to the coral?
options: A hard outer shell for protection
A bright white color when stressed
questions.why: 文中說藻類透過光合作用製造糖分，供給珊瑚。

questions.q: The word "scarce" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: scarce 意為「稀少的」，與 in short supply 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that bleaching harms corals because it
options: makes the corals grow far too quickly
turns the surrounding water milky white
draws in too many marine species
removes the corals' main source of food
questions.why: 白化使珊瑚失去主要食物來源，故對其有害。

questions.q: The relationship between corals and algae is best described as one in which
options: both partners benefit from the exchange
only the algae gain any real advantage
the coral slowly harms the algae
neither partner depends on the other
questions.why: 珊瑚與藻類互利共生，雙方都從交換中獲益。

academic.title: The Journey of Water
academic.passage:
Water is one of the few substances on Earth that naturally exists as a solid, a liquid, and a gas. This ability to change form drives the water cycle, the endless journey that moves water between the oceans, the air, and the land. Although the total amount of water on the planet stays almost the same, it is constantly on the move.

The cycle is powered by the Sun. Heat causes water at the surface of oceans and lakes to evaporate, turning into an invisible gas called water vapor that rises into the atmosphere. As the vapor climbs higher, it cools and condenses into tiny droplets, forming the clouds we see overhead. When these droplets grow heavy enough, they fall back to the ground as rain or snow.

Some of this water soaks into the soil and is taken up by plants, while some flows into rivers that carry it back toward the sea. From there, the whole process begins again. Because the cycle connects distant places, pollution released in one region can eventually travel to another. Understanding how water circulates is therefore essential not only for predicting weather but also for managing this shared and limited resource.

questions.q: According to the passage, what causes water at the ocean surface to evaporate?
questions.why: 文中說太陽的熱使海面的水蒸發。

questions.q: The word "circulates" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: circulates 意為「循環流動」，與 moves around 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that the total amount of water on Earth
options: depends mainly on the number of clouds
stays roughly the same as it cycles
questions.why: 文中說水的總量幾乎不變，故隨循環大致維持恆定。

questions.q: According to the passage, clouds form when water vapor
options: cools and condenses into tiny droplets
soaks into the soil beside rivers
is heated strongly by the Sun
is taken up by growing plants
questions.why: 雲是水蒸氣冷卻凝結成小水滴而形成。

questions.q: The author mentions that pollution can travel between regions mainly to
options: prove that all rainwater is now unsafe
show why the cycle matters to everyone
explain how clouds form in the sky
argue that rivers should avoid the oceans
questions.why: 提到污染會跨區傳播，是為說明理解水循環對所有人都重要。

academic.title: The Dance of the Honeybee
academic.passage:
When a honeybee discovers a rich source of food, it faces a curious problem: how to tell the rest of the hive where the food is without being able to lead them there one by one. The bee's solution is a form of communication so precise that scientists once found it hard to believe. Returning to the dark hive, the bee performs a special movement across the honeycomb known as the waggle dance.

During the dance, the bee walks in a straight line while rapidly shaking its body, then circles back to repeat the pattern. The direction of the straight run tells the other bees the direction of the food relative to the Sun. The length of the run, and how long the waggling lasts, indicates how far away the food lies. Nearby bees follow closely, sensing the movements and vibrations in the darkness.

Remarkably, the dancing bee adjusts its signal as the Sun moves across the sky, so the instructions stay accurate throughout the day. Through this elegant code, a single bee can send dozens of others straight to a flower patch far from the hive. The waggle dance remains one of the clearest examples of detailed communication found outside human language.

questions.q: The word "indicates" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: indicates 意為「顯示、指出」，與 shows 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the waggle dance must change during the day because
options: the dancing bees slowly grow tired
the food source keeps moving away
the hive grows darker toward evening
the position of the Sun keeps shifting
questions.why: 因太陽位置不斷改變，故舞蹈訊號須隨之調整。

questions.q: According to the passage, the length of the straight run tells other bees
options: how far away the food lies
which way the Sun is moving
how rich the food will be
questions.why: 文中說直線奔跑的長度，告訴其他蜜蜂食物有多遠。

questions.q: The author notes that scientists "once found it hard to believe" mainly to
options: suggest that early scientists were careless
stress how surprisingly precise the dance is
prove that bees cannot really communicate
explain why the hive is kept in darkness
questions.why: 「難以置信」用以強調此舞蹈的精確令人驚訝。

questions.q: According to the passage, where does the bee perform the waggle dance?
options: On a flower far from the hive
In the open air near the Sun
On the honeycomb inside the hive
At the front entrance of the hive
questions.why: 文中說蜜蜂在蜂巢內的蜂巢面上跳舞。

academic.title: Why Volcanoes Erupt
academic.passage:
Deep beneath the Earth's surface lies a layer of intensely hot rock. In certain places, some of this rock melts, forming a thick, fiery liquid called magma. Because magma is lighter than the solid rock around it, it slowly rises toward the surface, collecting in underground chambers. A volcano is essentially an opening through which this molten material can escape.

Not all volcanoes behave in the same way. When magma is thin and runny, gases trapped inside it can bubble out gently, and the lava flows in slow, glowing rivers. When the magma is thick and sticky, however, gases become trapped and pressure builds until the volcano erupts with tremendous force, hurling ash and rock high into the sky. The most violent eruptions can affect the weather across an entire region for months.

Although eruptions can be destructive, volcanoes also play a creative role. Over long periods, the material they release forms new land and enriches the surrounding soil, making it unusually fertile. Many islands owe their very existence to ancient underwater eruptions. By studying the gases and small tremors that often come before an eruption, scientists hope to give people living nearby enough warning to reach safety.

questions.q: It can be inferred that a volcano with thick, sticky magma is more likely to
options: cool down before reaching the top
questions.why: 濃稠黏滯的岩漿易困住氣體，故更可能猛烈噴發。

questions.q: According to the passage, magma rises toward the surface because
options: it is lighter than the rock around it
gases push it down into the chambers
the surface rock pulls it upward
it is heavier than the solid rock
questions.why: 岩漿比周圍岩石輕，故會向上升。

questions.q: The author mentions that many islands were formed by underwater eruptions mainly to
options: warn readers about the dangers of the sea
show that volcanoes can create as well as destroy
explain why islands are so hard to reach
prove that every volcano is found underwater
questions.why: 提到島嶼由噴發形成，是為說明火山也有創造的一面。

questions.q: According to the passage, what is magma?
options: A gas let out before an eruption
A layer of cooled volcanic ash
Hot rock that has melted into liquid
The solid rock found near the surface
questions.why: 文中說岩漿是熔化成液態的高溫岩石。

questions.q: The word "fertile" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: fertile 意為「肥沃的」，與 good for growing plants 最接近。

academic.title: The Body's Line of Defense
academic.passage:
The human body is constantly surrounded by bacteria, viruses, and other microbes, many of which could cause disease. To defend itself, the body relies on the immune system, a network of cells and organs that works much like an internal security force. Its task is to tell the difference between the body's own cells and dangerous intruders, and then to destroy the invaders.

The first line of defense is general. Skin acts as a physical barrier, and certain cells patrol the blood, swallowing any foreign particles they encounter. If an invader slips past these defenses, a more targeted response begins. The body produces proteins called antibodies, each shaped to lock onto one specific type of intruder and mark it for destruction.

What makes the system truly powerful is its memory. After defeating a particular microbe, the body keeps a record of it. Should the same intruder return, the immune system recognizes it at once and responds far more quickly than before, often stopping the disease before any symptoms appear. This is why we rarely catch certain illnesses twice. Vaccines take advantage of this memory, training the body to recognize a dangerous microbe safely, long before a real infection ever occurs.

questions.q: According to the passage, the immune system's most basic task is to
options: tell the body's own cells from intruders
produce as many antibodies as it can
remember every microbe it has ever met
questions.why: 免疫系統最基本的任務，是分辨自身細胞與入侵者。

questions.q: The author compares the immune system to "an internal security force" mainly to
options: suggest that the body is always in danger
help readers picture how it defends the body
prove that guards work just like antibodies
argue that the skin is our only defense
questions.why: 以「保安部隊」作比，是為幫助讀者理解其防禦功能。

questions.q: According to the passage, what do antibodies do?
options: Form a physical barrier on the skin
Swallow all foreign particles in the blood
Lock onto one specific type of intruder
Store a lasting record of every microbe
questions.why: 文中說抗體會鎖定某一特定入侵者，並標記之。

questions.q: The word "encounter" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: encounter 意為「遇到」，與 come across 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that vaccines work by
options: preparing the immune system's memory in advance
replacing the body's antibodies with new ones
weakening the skin so microbes can be studied
erasing the body's record of past infections
questions.why: 疫苗預先訓練免疫系統的記憶，以防真正的感染。

academic.title: The Moon and the Tides
academic.passage:
Anyone who spends a day at the seaside may notice that the level of the water rises and falls in a regular rhythm. These movements, known as tides, are caused mainly by the pull of the Moon's gravity on the Earth's oceans. Although the Moon is far away, its gravitational force is strong enough to tug gently on the vast body of water covering the planet.

The Moon pulls hardest on the ocean nearest to it, drawing the water into a bulge. Curiously, a second bulge forms on the opposite side of the Earth at the same time. As the planet rotates, different coastlines pass through these bulges, so most places experience two high tides and two low tides each day. The Sun's gravity adds to the effect, though its influence is weaker despite its enormous size, because it lies so much farther away.

When the Sun and Moon line up, their combined pull produces especially high tides. When they sit at right angles, the tides are smaller. Sailors and fishing communities have tracked these patterns for thousands of years, and today accurate tide tables allow ships to enter harbors and cross shallow waters in complete safety.

questions.q: The author mentions sailors and fishing communities mainly to
options: prove that tides were once impossible to predict
show that understanding tides has long been useful
argue that only sailors truly grasp the Moon
explain why harbors are built in shallow water
questions.why: 提到水手與漁民，是為說明理解潮汐長久以來都很有用。

questions.q: According to the passage, what mainly causes ocean tides?
options: The rotation of the Earth alone
The heat of the Sun on the water
The pull of the Moon's gravity
questions.why: 文中說潮汐主要由月球的引力造成。

questions.q: The word "tug" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: tug 意為「拉、拽」，與 pull 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the Sun affects the tides less than the Moon because the Sun
options: lies much farther away from the Earth
is far smaller than the Moon is
has no gravity of its own at all
only shines during the daytime hours
questions.why: 太陽雖大，但距離遠得多，故對潮汐的影響較小。

questions.q: According to the passage, especially high tides occur when
options: the Moon drifts closer to the Sun
the Sun and Moon line up together
the Sun and Moon sit at right angles
the Earth stops rotating for a while
questions.why: 當太陽與月球連成一線時，會產生特別高的潮。

academic.title: Light in the Deep Sea
academic.passage:
Sunlight reaches only the upper layers of the ocean. Below about a thousand meters, the sea is plunged into total, permanent darkness. Yet this hidden world is far from lifeless, and it is not entirely dark either. Many of the creatures living there produce their own light through a chemical reaction, an ability known as bioluminescence.

The glow is created when a special molecule reacts with oxygen inside the animal's body, releasing energy as light rather than heat. Because almost none of the energy is wasted, this living light is remarkably efficient. Different animals use it in different ways. Some flash brightly to startle a predator and gain a moment to escape. Others dangle a glowing lure to draw curious prey within reach. Still others use patterns of light to recognize members of their own species in the darkness.

In the deep ocean, where finding food or a mate is a constant challenge, the ability to make light offers a powerful advantage. Scientists studying these animals have even borrowed the chemistry behind the glow for use in medical research, where it helps them track processes inside living cells. The dark depths, it turns out, still have a great deal to teach us.

questions.q: According to the passage, how is bioluminescent light produced?
options: By taking in sunlight during the day
By reflecting light from other animals
By a molecule reacting with oxygen
By heat rising from the deep ocean
questions.why: 文中說發光是分子與氧反應而產生。

questions.q: The word "startle" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: startle 意為「使驚嚇」，與 frighten 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the light is called "efficient" because it
options: produces light while wasting little energy
lets the animals see sunlight underwater
works only when the animal is in danger
needs a large amount of heat to appear
questions.why: 因幾乎不浪費能量，故這種生物光被稱為高效。

questions.q: According to the passage, an animal that dangles a glowing lure is most likely trying to
options: scare away a much larger predator
draw prey close enough to catch
signal to others of its own kind
light up its path through the dark
questions.why: 垂下發光誘餌，是為把獵物引到可捕捉的範圍內。

questions.q: The author mentions medical research mainly to
options: warn that studying the deep sea is risky
prove that deep-sea animals are hard to find
show that deep-sea discoveries can have wider uses
explain how living cells make their own light
questions.why: 提到醫學研究，是為說明深海發現可有更廣泛的用途。

academic.title: How Crystals Grow
academic.passage:
A crystal is any solid whose atoms are arranged in a highly ordered, repeating pattern. This hidden order is what gives many crystals their smooth, flat faces and regular geometric shapes. From a grain of salt to a diamond, the same basic principle applies: the atoms settle into a fixed arrangement and repeat it over and over in every direction.

Most crystals form as a hot liquid or a dissolved substance slowly cools or dries. As the temperature drops, atoms that were once free to move begin to lock together in the most stable pattern available. The slower this process happens, the larger and more perfect the resulting crystals tend to be. Deep underground, where cooling can take thousands of years, enormous crystals sometimes grow. When cooling is rapid, only tiny crystals have time to form.

The particular pattern the atoms adopt depends on which elements are present and the conditions under which they gather. This is why each mineral tends to produce crystals of a characteristic shape and color, allowing geologists to identify them. By examining a crystal's structure, scientists can even work out the conditions under which it formed, reading it almost like a record of the distant past.

questions.q: The word "characteristic" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: characteristic 意為「典型的、特有的」，與 typical 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a crystal that cooled very quickly would probably be
options: smaller than one that cooled slowly
larger than one that cooled slowly
unable to form any pattern at all
questions.why: 冷卻愈快晶體愈小，故快速冷卻者較小。

questions.q: According to the passage, the size of a crystal depends largely on
options: the color of the mineral present
how deep the crystal is buried
the number of faces it develops
questions.why: 文中說晶體大小主要取決於冷卻的快慢。

questions.q: The author compares a crystal to "a record of the distant past" mainly to
options: suggest that crystals are extremely old
prove that crystals cannot form quickly
show that a crystal reveals how it formed
argue that geologists prefer large crystals
questions.why: 以「過去的紀錄」作比，是為說明晶體能透露其形成過程。

questions.q: According to the passage, what makes something a crystal?
options: Cooling from a hot liquid very fast
Having atoms in a repeating pattern
questions.why: 晶體的定義，是原子排列成重複的規律圖案。

academic.title: How Plants Spread Their Seeds
academic.passage:
A plant that drops all of its seeds directly beneath itself faces a serious difficulty. The young seedlings would be crowded together, forced to compete with one another and with the parent plant for light, water, and space. Over millions of years, plants have therefore evolved a remarkable variety of methods for scattering their seeds far and wide.

Some plants rely on the wind. Their seeds are light and may carry tiny wings or tufts of hair that let them drift long distances through the air. Others depend on water, producing seeds that can float down rivers or across the sea before washing ashore. Many plants, however, enlist the help of animals. They wrap their seeds inside sweet, nourishing fruit; an animal eats the fruit, travels some distance, and later deposits the seeds, unharmed, in a new location.

A few plants take a more forceful approach, building up tension in their seed pods until they burst open and fling the seeds outward. Whatever the method, the goal is the same: to give the next generation a chance to grow in fresh ground, away from crowded competition. This drive to spread has helped plants colonize almost every corner of the living world.

questions.q: It can be inferred that seeds falling directly below the parent are at a disadvantage because they would
options: have to compete for limited resources
be eaten at once by passing animals
float away before they could grow
lack any wings or tufts of hair
questions.why: 落在母株下的種子須爭奪有限資源，故處於劣勢。

questions.q: According to the passage, seeds with tiny wings or tufts of hair are best suited for being spread by
questions.why: 帶翅或絨毛的種子，最適合藉風力傳播。

questions.q: The author describes seeds wrapped in sweet fruit mainly to
options: explain why some fruit tastes better than others
prove that animals prefer fruit to other foods
give an example of using animals to spread seeds
warn that eating seeds can harm the animals
questions.why: 描述包在甜果中的種子，是為舉例說明利用動物散播種子。

questions.q: According to the passage, how do some plants fling their seeds outward?
options: By growing tiny wings on each seed
By floating them down a flowing river
By hiding them inside sweet, soft fruit
By building up tension in their pods
questions.why: 文中說某些植物靠在莢中累積張力，把種子彈出。

questions.q: The word "nourishing" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: nourishing 意為「有營養的」，與 nutritious 最接近。

academic.title: Lightning and Thunder
academic.passage:
A flash of lightning is one of the most dramatic displays in nature, but it begins with something invisible. Inside a storm cloud, powerful currents of air toss water droplets and ice crystals up and down, causing them to collide again and again. These collisions strip away tiny electric charges, and gradually the top of the cloud becomes positively charged while the bottom becomes negatively charged.

When the difference in charge grows large enough, it overcomes the resistance of the air, and a huge spark leaps between the cloud and the ground, or between two clouds. This is lightning. The spark heats the surrounding air almost instantly to a temperature far greater than the surface of the Sun. The air expands so violently that it produces a shock wave, which we hear as a crash of thunder.

Because light travels far faster than sound, we always see the flash before we hear the thunder. By counting the seconds between them, an observer can roughly estimate how far away the storm is: the longer the gap, the greater the distance. This simple method, known for generations, turns a frightening spectacle into a rough but useful measuring tool.

questions.q: According to the passage, lightning occurs when
options: light starts to travel faster than sound
the charge difference overcomes the air's resistance
thunder heats the air inside the cloud
water droplets freeze into ice crystals
questions.why: 當電荷差大到克服空氣阻力時，就產生閃電。

questions.q: The author explains how to count the seconds between flash and thunder mainly to
options: warn readers to stay away from all storms
prove that thunder is louder than lightning
show a practical use of the science described
argue that lightning is worse than thunder
questions.why: 教人數秒數，是為展示所述科學的實際用途。

questions.q: According to the passage, what causes the sound of thunder?
options: Ice crystals colliding inside the cloud
The spark leaping between two clouds
Charges building at the cloud's top
questions.why: 雷聲是空氣被瞬間加熱後劇烈膨脹所致。

questions.q: The word "spectacle" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: spectacle 意為「壯觀景象」，與 impressive sight 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that if the gap between a flash and its thunder grows longer, the storm is
questions.why: 閃電與雷聲的間隔愈長，代表暴風雨離得愈遠。

academic.title: Sleeping Through the Winter
academic.passage:
For many animals living in cold climates, winter brings a harsh challenge: food becomes scarce just as the body needs more energy to stay warm. Rather than struggle through these lean months, some species escape them altogether by entering a deep, sleep-like state called hibernation. During this period, the animal's body slows down to a remarkable degree.

A hibernating ground squirrel, for example, may lower its heart rate from hundreds of beats per minute to only a few, while its body temperature drops close to that of its surroundings. In this slowed condition, the animal burns very little energy and can survive for months on the fat it stored during the autumn. Its breathing becomes so faint that the animal may appear, at first glance, to be lifeless.

Hibernation is not simply a long sleep, however. The animal must prepare carefully, eating heavily beforehand to build up reserves of fat. It must also choose a sheltered den where the temperature will stay stable. Waking up too often would waste precious energy and could prove fatal. By shutting down almost completely, hibernating animals turn the deadly season of winter into a period they can quietly wait out.

questions.q: The author mentions the ground squirrel's heart rate mainly to
options: prove that squirrels sleep more than others
warn that hibernation harms the heart
give a concrete example of the body slowing down
explain why squirrels store fat in autumn
questions.why: 舉地松鼠心跳為例，是為具體說明身體如何減緩。

questions.q: According to the passage, what does a hibernating animal live on during winter?
options: Food it digs up under the snow
Energy drawn from the warm den
questions.why: 文中說冬眠動物靠秋天儲存的脂肪維生。

questions.q: The word "reserves" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: reserves 意為「儲備」，與 stores 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that waking up often during hibernation is harmful because it
options: makes the den too warm for sleep
uses up energy the animal cannot spare
lowers the body temperature even further
draws predators toward the sheltered den
questions.why: 頻繁醒來會耗掉動物無法承受的能量。

questions.q: According to the passage, an animal that failed to eat enough before winter would most likely
options: hibernate for a far longer period
keep a much higher body temperature
run out of energy before spring
wake up far less often than usual
questions.why: 冬前吃得不夠的動物，很可能在春天前耗盡能量。

academic.title: How Sound Travels
academic.passage:
Sound is a form of energy that travels as a wave, but unlike light, it cannot move through empty space. It needs a material to travel through, such as air, water, or even solid rock. When an object vibrates, it pushes against the particles around it, and these particles bump into their neighbors, passing the disturbance along until it reaches our ears.

Because sound depends on a material, it moves at different speeds through different substances. Surprisingly, it travels faster through water than through air, and faster still through solids, where the particles are packed closely together. When a sound wave meets a hard surface, some of it bounces back. If the reflected wave returns after a noticeable delay, we hear it as a separate sound, an echo.

Many animals make clever use of reflected sound. Bats and dolphins, for instance, send out bursts of sound and listen for the echoes that return. From the time each echo takes to come back, they can judge how far away an object is, and even its shape. This natural skill, called echolocation, lets them hunt and navigate in darkness or murky water, where ordinary sight would be of little use.

questions.q: According to the passage, sound travels fastest through
questions.why: 文中說聲音在固體中傳播最快。

questions.q: The word "murky" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: murky 意為「混濁的」，與 cloudy 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that sound cannot travel through empty space because empty space
options: is far too cold for any wave
holds no particles to pass it along
soaks up sound faster than solids
questions.why: 真空中沒有粒子傳遞，故聲音無法通過。

questions.q: According to the passage, an echo is heard when a sound wave
options: passes through a solid without slowing
is first created by a vibrating object
bounces back after a noticeable delay
travels faster in water than in air
questions.why: 聲波在明顯延遲後反彈回來時，就聽成回聲。

questions.q: The author describes bats and dolphins mainly to
options: prove that animals hear better than people
warn readers that murky water is dangerous
explain why sound cannot travel in space
give examples of animals that use echoes
questions.why: 舉蝙蝠與海豚為例，是為說明利用回聲的動物。

academic.title: The Art of Disguise
academic.passage:
In the struggle to survive, many animals depend not on speed or strength but on deception. One of the most widespread strategies is camouflage, in which an animal's color or pattern blends into its surroundings, making it hard to see. A moth with bark-colored wings resting on a tree, or a flat fish speckled like the seabed, may pass unnoticed by both predators and prey.

A related trick is mimicry, in which one animal comes to resemble another. Some harmless insects, for example, have evolved the bright colors of a dangerous, stinging species. Predators that have learned to avoid the dangerous model also avoid the harmless copy, which enjoys protection without possessing any real defense of its own. In other cases, a predator may imitate something harmless in order to approach its prey unnoticed.

These disguises are not the result of any single animal's effort or intention. Instead, they develop gradually over many generations. Individuals that happen to blend in or resemble a protected species are slightly more likely to survive and reproduce, passing on the features that helped them. Across long stretches of time, this steady advantage sharpens the disguise until it can become astonishingly convincing.

questions.q: The word "resemble" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: resemble 意為「相似、像」，與 look like 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a harmless insect with bright warning colors survives better because predators
options: mistake it for a dangerous species
are strongly drawn to its pattern
questions.why: 掠食者把無害昆蟲誤認為危險物種，故牠較易存活。

questions.q: According to the passage, camouflage and mimicry are alike in that both
options: rely on an animal's speed to work
help an animal survive through deception
require the animal to sting its enemies
questions.why: 偽裝與擬態的共同點，是都以欺騙幫助動物生存。

questions.q: The author states that these disguises are "not the result of any single animal's effort" mainly to
options: suggest that animals never notice predators
prove that camouflage rarely works well
argue that mimicry beats plain camouflage
stress that they develop slowly over generations
questions.why: 此句強調這些偽裝是經多代逐漸演化而成。

questions.q: According to the passage, how does a predator sometimes use mimicry?
options: By imitating something harmless to approach prey
By copying the exact colors of the seabed
By stinging any animal that comes too near
By resting motionless upon the tree bark
questions.why: 文中說掠食者有時模仿無害之物，以接近獵物。

academic.title: Rivers of Ice
academic.passage:
A glacier is a vast, slowly moving mass of ice that forms where more snow falls each year than melts away. As fresh snow piles up, its weight presses the layers beneath into dense ice. Once the ice grows thick enough, gravity sets the whole mass creeping downhill, sometimes only a few centimeters a day, yet with enormous force.

As a glacier moves, it acts like a giant, slow river of ice, reshaping the land beneath it. Rocks frozen into the base of the glacier scrape against the ground, carving deep valleys and grinding solid stone into fine powder. Material picked up along the way is carried great distances and dumped far from its origin when the ice finally melts. Many of the world's broad valleys and lakes were shaped by glaciers during past ice ages.

Today, glaciers act as vital stores of fresh water. In many regions, their gradual summer melting feeds rivers on which millions of people depend for drinking and farming. As the climate warms and glaciers shrink, scientists worry that these natural reservoirs may one day fail, threatening the water supply of entire communities long after the ice itself has gone.

questions.q: It can be inferred that a glacier forms only in places where
options: rivers flow quickly down the slopes
more snow falls than melts away
the ground is made of soft rock
the yearly rainfall is very heavy
questions.why: 冰川只在降雪多於融化的地方形成。

questions.q: According to the passage, what sets a glacier moving downhill?
options: The melting of the ice in summer
The flow of the nearby rivers
The pull of gravity on its weight
The scraping of rocks at its base
questions.why: 是重力作用於冰的重量，使冰川向下移動。

questions.q: The author mentions broad valleys and lakes mainly to
options: warn that glaciers are dangerous to visit
prove that glaciers move very quickly
explain why the ice ages came to an end
show the lasting marks glaciers leave on land
questions.why: 提到寬谷與湖泊，是為說明冰川對地貌的長遠影響。

questions.q: According to the passage, how do glaciers carve deep valleys?
options: Rocks in the ice scrape against the ground
Meltwater slowly dissolves the solid stone
The freezing ice cracks the rock apart
Rivers cut through the ice each summer
questions.why: 冰中的岩石刮擦地面，而鑿出深谷。

questions.q: The word "vital" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: vital 意為「至關重要的」，與 essential 最接近。

academic.title: The Wonder of Spider Silk
academic.passage:
Spider silk is one of the most impressive materials found in nature. Though finer than a human hair, it is remarkably strong, and it can stretch considerably before it breaks. Weight for weight, some spider silk is tougher than steel, yet a spider produces it at ordinary temperatures using only simple raw materials from its own body.

The silk begins as a liquid protein stored inside the spider. As the spider draws it out through tiny openings, the protein molecules line up and lock together, and the liquid hardens almost instantly into a solid thread. A single spider can make several kinds of silk, each suited to a different task. One type forms the strong frame of a web, another the sticky spiral that traps insects, and yet another wraps captured prey or protects the spider's eggs.

Scientists have long dreamed of copying this material, which is light, strong, and harmless to the body. Such silk could be used for anything from tougher ropes to fine threads for repairing human tissue. So far, reproducing it in the laboratory has proved difficult, but researchers continue to study the spider's elegant method, hoping to learn a chemistry that nature perfected long ago.

questions.q: According to the passage, spider silk hardens into a solid thread when
options: it is warmed to a high temperature
the spider wraps it around its prey
its protein molecules line up and lock
it is stored as a liquid in the spider
questions.why: 蛋白質分子排列鎖合時，絲便硬化成固態線。

questions.q: The author compares spider silk to steel mainly to
options: prove that steel is heavier than silk
suggest that spiders could replace factories
explain how spiders build their round webs
stress how strong the silk is for its weight
questions.why: 以鋼作比，是為強調蛛絲相對其重量極為堅韌。

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the sticky spiral of a web used for?
options: Trapping the insects that land on it
Forming the strong frame of the web
Anchoring the web to a nearby branch
questions.why: 文中說黏性螺旋用來捕捉落網的昆蟲。

questions.q: The word "considerably" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: considerably 意為「大幅地」，與 greatly 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that artificial spider silk is not yet widely used because
options: natural spider silk is too weak to copy
the silk is harmful to human tissue
it remains hard to reproduce in the lab
spiders already supply all that is needed
questions.why: 因目前仍難以在實驗室重現，故人造蛛絲尚未廣泛使用。

academic.title: The Hanseatic League
academic.passage: In the twelfth century, merchants scattered across the ports of northern Europe faced a common problem: piracy, unpredictable tariffs, and rulers who offered little protection to foreign traders. Their response was the Hanseatic League, a loose confederation of trading cities that eventually linked more than a hundred towns from London to Novgorod. Unlike a modern nation, the League had no permanent army, no fixed capital, and no written constitution. Its power rested instead on the collective agreement of member cities to trade on shared terms and to punish those who violated them. A town that cheated Hanseatic merchants might find itself cut off from Baltic grain, Flemish cloth, or Russian furs, an economic penalty more persuasive than any threat of war. The League established fortified trading posts, called kontore, in foreign cities, where German merchants lived under their own laws. Delegates met irregularly at assemblies known as Hansetage, though attendance was voluntary and decisions were difficult to enforce. This very looseness was both a strength and a weakness. It allowed the League to expand without the cost of governing territory, but it also meant that as strong monarchies emerged in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, the Hanseatic cities could not unite quickly enough to resist them, and the confederation slowly faded.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did member cities of the Hanseatic League agree to do?
options: Trade on shared terms and punish those who broke them
Elect a single monarch to rule all towns
Build a fixed capital city at Novgorod
questions.why: 短文指出成員城市同意以共同條件貿易並懲罰違規者。

questions.q: The word 'persuasive' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: persuasive 意為「有說服力的」,與 convincing 同義。

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that the kontore allowed German merchants to:
options: avoid trading in foreign cities altogether
pay higher tariffs than local residents
operate under familiar rules while living abroad
recruit soldiers for the League's army
questions.why: kontore 讓德意志商人依自己的法律生活,可推論他們在海外仍依熟悉規則運作。

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why the League eventually declined?
options: It exhausted the entire supply of Baltic grain
Its merchants abandoned sea trade for farming
Piracy in the Baltic increased sharply
Its loose structure prevented quick united resistance to strong monarchies
questions.why: 短文結尾說鬆散結構使各城市無法及時聯合抵抗新興王權,導致衰落。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that the League had 'no permanent army, no fixed capital, and no written constitution'?
options: To emphasize how it differed from a modern nation
To argue that the League was poorly managed
To explain why merchants distrusted it
To describe the geography of northern Europe
questions.why: 作者藉此對比,強調漢薩同盟與現代國家的不同之處。

academic.title: Ukiyo-e: Pictures of the Floating World
academic.passage: During Japan's Edo period, a distinctive art form flourished in the crowded pleasure districts of cities such as Edo and Osaka. Known as ukiyo-e, or 'pictures of the floating world,' these woodblock prints depicted kabuki actors, famous courtesans, sumo wrestlers, and, later, sweeping landscapes. What made ukiyo-e remarkable was not only its subject matter but its method of production. A single print was the product of a collaboration among four specialists: the artist who drew the design, the carver who cut it into blocks of cherry wood, the printer who applied the inks, and the publisher who financed and marketed the whole enterprise. Because prints could be reproduced in the hundreds, they were affordable to ordinary townspeople rather than reserved for wealthy patrons. This commercial character shaped the art itself. Publishers, sensitive to public taste, encouraged images of current fashions, popular entertainers, and celebrated travel destinations. A single design might require a dozen separate blocks, one for each colour, all carefully aligned so that the layers registered precisely. When Japanese ports reopened to trade in the nineteenth century, these prints reached Europe, where their flat planes of colour and bold, asymmetrical compositions astonished painters accustomed to modelling and perspective, and helped inspire a new direction in Western art.

questions.q: According to the passage, how many specialists collaborated to produce a single ukiyo-e print?
questions.why: 短文明確指出由四位專家分工合作:繪師、雕師、刷師與出版商。

questions.q: The word 'registered' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: 此處 registered 指各色層精確對齊。

questions.q: It can be inferred that ukiyo-e prints were affordable to ordinary townspeople because:
options: they could be reproduced in large numbers
they used cheap paper instead of wood
the artists worked without any payment
questions.why: 因版畫可大量複製,故價格親民,一般市民也買得起。

questions.q: The passage suggests that the commercial nature of ukiyo-e most directly influenced:
options: the durability of the cherry wood
questions.why: 商業性質使出版商迎合大眾口味,影響了描繪的題材。

questions.q: Why does the author mention European painters at the end of the passage?
options: To explain why Japanese ports had closed
To compare ink prices across countries
To show that ukiyo-e influenced Western art
questions.why: 作者藉此說明浮世繪對西方藝術產生了影響。

academic.title: Polynesian Wayfinding
academic.passage: Long before the magnetic compass reached the Pacific, Polynesian navigators were crossing thousands of kilometres of open ocean to settle islands separated by vast stretches of empty water. They carried no instruments, yet they routinely found tiny targets, single islands only a few kilometres wide, in a sea that covers a third of the planet. Their method, known today as wayfinding, depended on the disciplined observation of nature. Navigators memorized the rising and setting points of dozens of stars, using them as a kind of compass that turned slowly through the night. When clouds hid the sky, they read the direction and rhythm of ocean swells, which held their pattern for days after the winds that created them had died. The behaviour of birds offered further clues: certain species fly out to sea at dawn to feed and return to land at dusk, betraying the direction of an unseen island. Even the colour of the sky and the shape of distant clouds, which tend to gather over land, were pressed into service. This knowledge was not written down but passed from expert to apprentice through years of training and memorized chant. When European explorers arrived, they were astonished that people without charts or instruments had colonized an ocean larger than all the continents combined.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did navigators use to find direction when clouds hid the stars?
options: The direction and rhythm of ocean swells
Written charts kept in their canoes
The changing position of the moon
questions.why: 短文指出雲遮蔽星空時,他們依海浪湧的方向與節奏辨向。

questions.q: The word 'betraying' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: betraying 此處意為「洩露、顯示」島嶼的方向。

questions.q: It can be inferred that ocean swells were useful for navigation because they:
options: kept a steady pattern even after the wind stopped
questions.why: 短文說湧浪在生成它的風停止後仍維持數日,故可穩定辨向。

questions.q: Which detail best supports the idea that wayfinding was a demanding skill?
options: Islands were only a few kilometres wide
The Pacific covers a third of the planet
Certain birds fly out to sea at dawn
Navigators memorized dozens of stars over years of training
questions.why: 需背誦數十顆星並經多年訓練,顯示這是艱難的技藝。

questions.q: Why does the author mention European explorers at the end of the passage?
options: To emphasize how impressive the Polynesian achievement was
To explain how the compass was later invented
To argue that Europeans were the better sailors
To describe the size of Polynesian canoes
questions.why: 藉歐洲探險者的驚訝,凸顯玻里尼西亞航海成就之非凡。

academic.title: The Indonesian Gamelan
academic.passage: A gamelan is not a single instrument but an entire orchestra, most often associated with the islands of Java and Bali. It consists largely of tuned percussion: bronze gongs of many sizes, metal keys struck with mallets, drums, and occasionally bamboo flutes or a bowed string. What strikes newcomers most is that a gamelan is treated as a single, indivisible set. The instruments are tuned to one another rather than to any universal standard, so instruments from one gamelan cannot simply be swapped into another. Each set has its own slightly different tuning, giving it a distinct voice that villagers often describe in almost personal terms. The music itself is built in layers. A central melody, played on certain metal-keyed instruments, is elaborated by faster-moving parts above it and punctuated by deep gongs below that mark the boundaries of musical phrases. No single musician dominates; the interlocking parts fit together so tightly that a rapid passage may be shared between two players, each striking alternate notes so quickly that the ear hears one continuous line. Traditionally, the music was learned entirely by ear and memory, without notation, through long participation in a community ensemble. This emphasis on collective interlocking rather than individual virtuosity reflects social values that many observers see mirrored in the music.

questions.q: According to the passage, why can instruments from one gamelan not be swapped into another?
options: They are made of different metals
Each set is tuned to itself rather than a universal standard
They are too heavy to move between villages
Each village forbids lending them out
questions.why: 短文指出每套甘美朗自成一體調音,而非依通用標準,故不能互換。

questions.q: The word 'punctuated' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: punctuated 此處指低音鑼在樂句邊界處間隔標示。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a rapid passage is shared between two players in order to:
options: let one player rest between phrases
reduce the number of instruments needed
produce a fast line that sounds continuous
make the music easier to write down
questions.why: 兩人交替快速擊音,讓耳朵聽成一條連續且快速的旋律線。

questions.q: The passage suggests that gamelan music most clearly reflects:
options: a preference for individual star performers
the need for written musical notation
questions.why: 強調互鎖合作而非個人炫技,反映重視集體協作的社會價值。

questions.q: Why does the author describe the gamelan as 'not a single instrument but an entire orchestra'?
options: To correct a likely misunderstanding of the term
To argue that gamelans are too expensive
To explain why the gongs are made of bronze
To compare the islands of Java and Bali
questions.why: 作者藉此澄清讀者可能誤以為甘美朗是單一樂器。

academic.title: The Persian Qanat
academic.passage: The dry plateau of ancient Persia posed a stubborn challenge: rain was scarce, rivers were few, and much of the available water lay trapped beneath distant hills. The solution, developed more than two and a half thousand years ago, was the qanat, an ingenious system of underground channels that carried water for tens of kilometres without pumps of any kind. A qanat begins with a mother well dug into a water-bearing layer at the foot of the highlands. From there, a gently sloping tunnel is cut toward the settlement, angled just steeply enough for water to flow by gravity but not so steeply that it erodes the channel. Because the tunnel runs underground, very little water is lost to evaporation under the harsh sun, a crucial advantage in such a climate. A line of vertical shafts, spaced at regular intervals, connects the tunnel to the surface. These shafts provided air and removed excavated soil during construction, and afterward allowed workers to enter for cleaning and repair. Building a qanat demanded great skill and years of labour, and the knowledge was guarded by specialists whose craft passed from father to son. Remarkably, many qanats dug in antiquity still deliver water today, and the technology spread far beyond Persia, reaching North Africa, Spain, and even parts of Asia.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the main advantage of running the qanat tunnel underground?
options: It is cheaper to build than surface canals
It reduces the water lost to evaporation
It allows the use of powerful pumps
It keeps the water pleasantly warm
questions.why: 短文指出地下渠道使水在烈日下蒸發流失極少,是關鍵優勢。

questions.q: The word 'stubborn' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: stubborn 此處形容難以克服的挑戰。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the slope of the tunnel had to be carefully controlled because:
options: workers could not dig steep tunnels at all
the water would freeze if it flowed too slowly
too steep a slope would erode the channel
the mother well was always very shallow
questions.why: 坡度過陡會侵蝕渠道,故須謹慎控制傾斜角度。

questions.q: Which of the following functions of the vertical shafts is NOT mentioned in the passage?
options: Allowing entry for cleaning and repair
questions.why: 短文提到通風、排土與進入維修,但未提及為磨坊發電。

questions.q: Why does the author state that many ancient qanats still deliver water today?
options: To show the durability and success of the technology
To argue that modern pumps are entirely unnecessary
To explain why Persia became so wealthy
To describe how underground rivers form
questions.why: 藉古坎兒井至今仍供水,凸顯此技術的耐久與成功。

academic.title: The Discovery of Linear Perspective
academic.passage: For much of the Middle Ages, European painters represented space in ways that look, to modern eyes, curiously flat. Figures were sized according to their spiritual importance rather than their distance from the viewer, and buildings were often shown from several angles at once. In early fifteenth-century Florence, this began to change with the working out of linear perspective, a geometric method for depicting three-dimensional space on a flat surface. The key idea was the vanishing point: a single point on the horizon toward which all parallel lines receding from the viewer appear to converge. Objects were drawn smaller as they approached this point, so that a tiled floor or a row of columns seemed to shrink convincingly into the distance. The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is credited with the first practical demonstrations, and the principles were soon set down in writing by the scholar Leon Battista Alberti, who described the picture surface as a kind of window through which the viewer looks. Perspective did more than create an illusion of depth; it imposed a single, fixed viewpoint, placing the observer in a precise relationship to the scene. Within a few decades the technique had spread across Italy and beyond, transforming not only how painters worked but how educated viewers expected a convincing picture of the world to look.
