questions.q: According to the passage, what is the vanishing point?
options: The brightest area of a painting
The point where parallel lines appear to converge
The exact centre of a tiled floor
The decorative frame around a picture
questions.why: 短文定義消失點為所有平行線看似匯聚的地平線上一點。

questions.q: The word 'converge' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: converge 意為「匯聚、聚合於一點」。

questions.q: It can be inferred that medieval paintings looked flat partly because:
options: figures were sized by importance, not distance
the paintings were made very small
questions.why: 人物依精神重要性而非遠近定大小,故畫面顯得平面。

questions.q: Alberti's description of the picture surface as a 'window' most directly supports the idea that perspective:
options: places the viewer at a single fixed viewpoint
questions.why: 把畫面比作窗戶,呼應透視法將觀者置於單一固定視點。

questions.q: Why does the author describe medieval painting at the beginning of the passage?
options: To praise medieval artists over Renaissance ones
To explain how pigments were manufactured
To provide a contrast that highlights the change perspective brought
To argue that flat images are more accurate
questions.why: 先描述中世紀平面畫法是為對比,凸顯透視法帶來的變革。

academic.title: The Inca Quipu
academic.passage: The Inca Empire, which stretched along the spine of the Andes in the fifteenth century, governed millions of people and coordinated vast movements of goods, labour, and food, yet it left behind no writing system of the familiar kind. Instead, its administrators relied on the quipu, a device made of a main cord from which many knotted strings hung down. Information was encoded in the colour of the strings, the type and position of the knots, and the way subsidiary cords were attached. Scholars are confident that quipus recorded numbers: the knots follow a decimal pattern, with the position of a cluster of knots along a string indicating units, tens, or hundreds. Such records allowed officials to track census figures, tribute owed, and the contents of storehouses scattered across the empire. Whether quipus also recorded narrative information, such as histories or songs, remains debated, and much of their meaning was lost after the Spanish conquest, when the specialists trained to read them died or were suppressed. What is clear is that a complex state was administered without paper or ink, using a portable, durable medium that a trained official could interpret by both sight and touch. Modern researchers, aided by computers, continue to study surviving quipus, hoping to recover more of the logic that once held an empire together.

questions.q: According to the passage, what are scholars confident that quipus recorded?
questions.why: 短文明確指出學者確信奇普記錄了數字。

questions.q: The word 'suppressed' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: suppressed 此處指專家遭到壓制、禁絕。

questions.q: It can be inferred that much of the quipus' meaning was lost because:
options: the knots were too small to see
the people who could read them were gone after the conquest
questions.why: 征服後能解讀者死亡或遭壓制,故大部分意義失傳。

questions.q: Which detail best supports the claim that quipus encoded numbers in a decimal pattern?
options: The main cord was thicker than the others
The strings came in many colours
The position of a knot cluster indicated units, tens, or hundreds
questions.why: 結節群在繩上的位置代表個、十、百,支持其為十進位系統。

questions.q: Why does the author mention modern researchers and computers at the end of the passage?
options: To argue that quipus are useless today
To show that efforts to decode quipus continue
To explain how the Inca themselves used computers
questions.why: 說明現代學者仍藉電腦持續解讀奇普。

academic.title: The Glow of Byzantine Mosaic
academic.passage: Walk into one of the great churches of the Byzantine world and you enter a space that seems to glow from within. The source of that light is mosaic: images assembled from thousands of small cubes, called tesserae, of coloured glass and stone pressed into wet plaster. Byzantine craftsmen developed the medium to an extraordinary level, above all through their use of gold. To make a golden tessera, a thin sheet of gold leaf was sandwiched between two layers of glass, producing a cube that gleamed without tarnishing. Vast fields of these gold cubes formed the backgrounds of religious images, dissolving any sense of ordinary space and suggesting instead a timeless, heavenly realm. Crucially, the artisans did not set the tesserae perfectly flat. They tilted each cube at a slightly different angle, so that as a worshipper moved or as candlelight flickered, the surface shimmered and seemed alive. The images themselves followed established conventions: solemn frontal figures, elongated proportions, and large eyes intended to convey spiritual rather than physical presence. Because mosaic is durable and resists fading, many of these works survive in near-original brilliance after more than a thousand years, offering us a vivid sense of how overwhelming such interiors were meant to feel to those who first stepped inside them.

questions.q: According to the passage, how was a golden tessera made?
options: By painting glass with yellow pigment
By polishing a solid cube of gold
By sandwiching gold leaf between two layers of glass
By dipping stone in molten gold
questions.why: 短文說金色鑲嵌塊是將金箔夾在兩層玻璃之間製成。

questions.q: The word 'dissolving' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: dissolving 此處指消解、去除一般空間感。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the tesserae were tilted at different angles in order to:
options: save on the amount of gold used
make the surface shimmer as light and viewers moved
make the images easier to carve
prevent the plaster from ever drying
questions.why: 傾斜角度使表面隨光影與觀者移動而閃爍生動。

questions.q: The large eyes and frontal figures are described as intended to convey:
questions.why: 短文指這些特徵旨在傳達精神性而非肉體的存在。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that many mosaics survive in near-original brilliance?
options: To explain why gold was so expensive
To argue that modern art fades much faster
To describe how the churches were built
To suggest that we can still experience their intended effect
questions.why: 因作品保存良好,今人仍能感受其原本的震撼效果。

academic.title: The Stone City of Great Zimbabwe
academic.passage: Rising from the grasslands of southern Africa are the stone ruins of Great Zimbabwe, a city that flourished between roughly the twelfth and fifteenth centuries. Its most striking feature is a set of massive curving walls, some more than nine metres high and built entirely without mortar. The builders shaped granite blocks into even courses and fitted them so precisely that the walls have stood for centuries under their own weight alone. At its height the city may have housed many thousands of people and served as the centre of a wide trading network. Archaeologists have recovered glass beads from the Indian Ocean coast, porcelain from China, and coins from distant ports, showing that Great Zimbabwe was linked, through intermediaries, to commerce spanning half the world. The wealth that supported it came largely from cattle and from gold, mined in the surrounding region and carried to the coast for export. For a long time, foreign observers refused to believe that local African societies had built such a place, and proposed far-fetched theories crediting outsiders, a view now thoroughly discredited by archaeological evidence. The city was gradually abandoned, perhaps because the surrounding land could no longer support its population or because trade routes shifted. Its very name, meaning 'houses of stone,' was later given to the modern nation that surrounds it.

questions.q: According to the passage, how were the walls of Great Zimbabwe constructed?
options: From granite blocks fitted together without mortar
From mud bricks baked in the sun
From blocks held together with iron bars
questions.why: 短文指城牆以花崗岩塊精確堆砌,完全不用灰漿。

questions.q: The word 'discredited' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: discredited 意為「被證明為錯誤、遭推翻」。

questions.q: The recovery of Chinese porcelain and Indian Ocean beads suggests that Great Zimbabwe:
options: took part in long-distance trade networks
was located directly on the coast
questions.why: 出土遠方器物顯示該城參與了長距離貿易網絡。

questions.q: Which of the following is given as a possible reason the city was abandoned?
options: The sudden collapse of its stone walls
The land could no longer support its population
questions.why: 短文推測周邊土地無法再供養人口,是可能的棄城原因之一。

questions.q: Why does the author mention the far-fetched theories crediting outsiders?
options: To show that such claims have been rejected by evidence
To prove that outsiders really built the city
To explain how the local gold was mined
To describe the Indian Ocean trade in detail
questions.why: 提及這些牽強說法,是為說明它們已被考古證據否定。

academic.title: The Kora and the Griot
academic.passage: Among the most distinctive instruments of West Africa is the kora, a harp-like instrument with twenty-one strings that has been played for centuries in the region once dominated by the Mali Empire. Its body is a large gourd cut in half and covered with cowhide, which acts as a resonator, while a long hardwood neck rises from it carrying two parallel rows of strings. The player sits facing the instrument, gripping two upright handposts and plucking the strings with the thumb and forefinger of each hand, producing flowing patterns that can sound like several instruments at once. The kora is traditionally the instrument of the jali, or griot, a hereditary class of musicians and oral historians. Far more than entertainers, griots were the living memory of their communities. They preserved genealogies, recounted the deeds of past rulers, mediated disputes, and were expected to advise the powerful, sometimes praising and sometimes gently criticizing them through song. A ruler without a griot risked being forgotten, for it was the griot who carried a family's reputation forward across generations. This role passed from parent to child along with the musical skill itself, so that mastery of the kora was inseparable from a deep knowledge of history, poetry, and the social obligations of the craft.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the body of the kora made from?
options: A large gourd covered with cowhide
questions.why: 短文指科拉琴的共鳴體是覆以牛皮的大葫蘆。

questions.q: The word 'hereditary' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: hereditary 意為「世襲的」,即在家族中代代相傳。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a ruler valued having a griot because the griot:
options: would preserve the ruler's reputation for later generations
questions.why: 格里奧能將統治者的聲名傳續後世,故受重視。

questions.q: The passage states that griots were 'far more than entertainers' mainly because they:
options: were paid much larger sums of money
served as historians, advisers, and mediators
questions.why: 他們兼任歷史記錄者、顧問與調解者,故不僅是娛人者。

questions.q: Why does the author state that mastery of the kora was 'inseparable from a deep knowledge of history'?
options: To emphasize that the griot's role combined music and learning
To explain how the gourd was grown
To argue that the kora is very hard to tune
To compare the kora with the European harp
questions.why: 強調格里奧的角色將音樂與歷史學識結合為一體。

academic.title: The Endurance of Roman Concrete
academic.passage: The Romans were not the first people to use a form of concrete, but they exploited it on a scale and with a durability that still astonishes engineers. Their most important discovery was a particular volcanic ash, found near the Bay of Naples, which when mixed with lime and water set into a hard, stone-like mass. Crucially, this mixture would harden even under water, allowing the Romans to build harbours, piers, and bridge foundations directly in the sea. With concrete they could pour massive structures into wooden moulds rather than laboriously cutting and stacking stone, and the material freed them to shape interiors in bold new ways, above all the sweeping domes and vaults that define Roman architecture. The vast dome of one temple in Rome, unreinforced by any steel, has stood for nearly nineteen centuries and remains among the largest of its kind. For a long time the recipe seemed almost magical, and modern engineers puzzled over why Roman marine concrete endured while much modern concrete crumbles within decades. Recent research suggests part of the answer: chemical reactions between the seawater and the volcanic ash actually continued over time, forming rare minerals that healed cracks and strengthened the material. Far from simply resisting the sea, Roman concrete in some ways grew tougher because of it.

questions.q: According to the passage, what unusual property allowed the Romans to build harbours and piers?
options: The concrete could harden even under water
The concrete never cracked at all
The concrete floated on the sea
questions.why: 短文指此混凝土能在水下硬化,故能建造港口與碼頭。

questions.q: The word 'exploited' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: exploited 此處意為「加以利用」。

questions.q: It can be inferred that pouring concrete into moulds was advantageous because it:
options: avoided the labour of cutting and stacking stone
questions.why: 灌入模具省去切割與堆疊石材的繁重工序,是其優勢。

questions.q: According to recent research, why did Roman marine concrete grow tougher over time?
options: It was reinforced with hidden steel
It was kept entirely away from water
Reactions with seawater formed minerals that healed cracks
questions.why: 海水與火山灰反應生成礦物,填補裂縫,使材料更堅固。

questions.q: Why does the author mention the ancient dome that has stood for nearly nineteen centuries?
options: To criticize modern engineers as careless
To explain how volcanic ash is formed
To provide evidence of the concrete's durability
To argue that steel is never needed in buildings
questions.why: 以屹立近十九世紀的穹頂為例,佐證羅馬混凝土的耐久。

academic.title: The Discipline of Fresco
academic.passage: A fresco is a wall painting made not on top of a dry surface but into wet plaster, and this simple-sounding difference demands remarkable discipline from the artist. In the technique known as true fresco, the painter spreads a layer of fresh, wet lime plaster over the area to be worked and applies pigments mixed only with water. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction binds the colour into the wall itself, so that the paint becomes part of the surface rather than a film lying on top of it. The result is exceptionally durable, which is why so many frescoes have survived for centuries. But the method is unforgiving. The painter can work only while the plaster remains damp, a window of a single day, and must therefore plan each section in advance, laying down only as much wet plaster as can be finished before it sets. The joints between these daily patches can sometimes still be detected by close observation. Mistakes are hard to correct, since the colour cannot simply be scraped off and repainted without cutting away the hardened plaster and beginning again. This combination of permanence and difficulty made fresco a demanding test of skill, rewarding careful preparation and confident execution while punishing hesitation, and it shaped the grand wall paintings of many cultures.

questions.q: According to the passage, what are pigments mixed with in true fresco?
questions.why: 短文指真濕壁畫的顏料僅與水調和。

questions.q: The word 'unforgiving' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: unforgiving 此處指幾乎不容出錯的技法。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the painter must plan each section in advance because:
options: the plaster can only be painted while it is damp
the walls are often very large
questions.why: 灰泥僅在濕潤時可上色,故須事先規劃每一區塊。

questions.q: Why are frescoes described as exceptionally durable?
options: They are painted on very thick stone walls
They are always protected behind glass
They are repainted on a regular schedule
The colour is chemically bound into the wall as the plaster dries
questions.why: 灰泥乾燥時顏色被化學結合進牆體,故格外耐久。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that the joints between daily patches can sometimes be detected?
options: To argue that frescoes are poorly made
To explain how plaster is manufactured
To show evidence of the day-by-day working method
To prove that frescoes fade quickly
questions.why: 每日接縫可見,佐證濕壁畫是逐日分區完成的工作方式。

academic.title: Movable Metal Type in Korea
academic.passage: The printing of books from movable type is often associated with fifteenth-century Europe, yet the world's oldest surviving book printed with movable metal type was produced in Korea in 1377, decades before Gutenberg's press. This work, a Buddhist text, was printed using individual characters cast in bronze, assembled into a page, inked, and pressed onto paper, then broken apart and reused. Korean printers had strong reasons to develop the method. The kingdom prized learning and Confucian scholarship, and its government wanted to circulate authoritative texts, but the demand for any single title was often too small to justify carving a whole set of fixed wooden blocks. Metal type, once cast, could be rearranged endlessly to print many different works. Yet the technology did not spread and transform society as it later did in Europe, and historians point to several reasons. The writing of the educated Korean class relied heavily on thousands of Chinese characters, so a font required an enormous number of separate pieces, unlike the couple of dozen letters of an alphabet. Printing therefore remained largely a state enterprise serving officials and scholars rather than a commercial industry. The invention is a striking reminder that a technology's impact depends not only on its cleverness but on the language, economy, and society into which it is introduced.

questions.q: According to the passage, in what year was the oldest surviving book printed with movable metal type produced?
questions.why: 短文明確指出該書於 1377 年在韓國印製。

questions.q: The word 'prized' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: prized 此處意為「高度重視、珍視」。

questions.q: It can be inferred that fixed wooden blocks were impractical for many titles because:
options: wooden blocks could not hold ink
carving a whole set was not worth it for small print runs
the government banned all wood carving
questions.why: 單一書籍需求小,雕整套固定木板不划算,故金屬活字更合適。

questions.q: According to the passage, why did movable type NOT transform Korean society as it did Europe's?
options: Korean paper was far too fragile
Bronze was too expensive to cast
The government deliberately destroyed the type
Korean writing needed thousands of characters, requiring a huge font
questions.why: 韓文書寫需數千漢字,字模數量龐大,限制了活字的普及。

questions.q: What is the author's main purpose in the final sentence?
options: To argue that Korea's invention was a failure
To make the general point that a technology's impact depends on its social context
To praise Gutenberg over the Korean printers
To explain in detail how bronze is cast
questions.why: 結尾旨在提出通則:技術的影響取決於它所處的社會脈絡。

academic.title: The Art of Persian Miniature
academic.passage: Persian miniature painting is an art of small scale and extraordinary refinement, developed above all to illustrate manuscripts of poetry and history. Rather than aiming for the illusion of depth that occupied European painters, these artists organized space according to a different logic. Figures and buildings were arranged across the page in bright, flat areas of colour, often tilted so that a garden or a courtyard is shown as if seen from above, letting the viewer take in the whole scene at once. Scale followed importance and composition rather than strict distance, and the eye is invited to wander from one exquisitely detailed passage to another. The paintings glow with pigments made from costly materials: brilliant blue from ground lapis lazuli, along with gold and silver, applied with brushes said to be made from just a few hairs. Because the images accompanied texts, they were meant to be held close and studied slowly, not viewed from across a room. A painting might be the work of a whole workshop, with different specialists preparing the paper, laying down gold, and painting faces or borders. Far from being mere decoration, the finest miniatures interpret the stories they illustrate, capturing a decisive moment of a poem or legend and rewarding the patient viewer with details that reveal themselves only on close and repeated looking.

questions.q: According to the passage, what was the brilliant blue pigment made from?
questions.why: 短文指鮮豔的藍色顏料由研磨的青金石製成。

questions.q: The word 'refinement' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: refinement 此處指精緻細膩、優雅講究。

questions.q: It can be inferred that Persian miniatures were meant to be viewed:
questions.why: 因隨文而作、細節繁密,故須近距離慢慢品賞。

questions.q: The tilted, bird's-eye arrangement of gardens allowed the artist to:
options: reduce the number of colours used
let the viewer take in the whole scene at once
questions.why: 俯視式的傾斜佈局讓觀者一眼盡覽整個場景。

questions.q: Why does the author state that the finest miniatures 'interpret the stories they illustrate'?
options: To argue that they are more than mere decoration
To explain how lapis lazuli is mined
To show that they copied European art
To describe the small size of the brushes
questions.why: 強調頂尖細密畫詮釋所繪故事,並非單純的裝飾。

academic.title: The Venetian Arsenal
academic.passage: At the height of its power, the maritime republic of Venice maintained a shipyard so vast and so efficient that it amazed visitors from across Europe. Known as the Arsenal, this walled complex employed thousands of workers and could, when pressed, assemble a fully equipped warship in a single day. It achieved this through a principle that would not become widespread in industry for centuries: standardized, interchangeable parts moved along an assembly line. A hull under construction was floated down a canal past a series of stations, and at each stop workers added a specific set of components, such as oars, ropes, weapons, and provisions, drawn from vast stores kept ready in advance. Because ropes, rudders, and fittings were made to uniform patterns, a part from the storehouse would fit any hull of the same class. This organization gave Venice a strategic advantage. A city with few natural resources and a small population could nonetheless keep a large navy at sea and replace losses with startling speed, allowing it to dominate Mediterranean trade for generations. The Arsenal was also a jealously guarded secret; its skilled workers enjoyed privileges but were discouraged from carrying their knowledge abroad. To later observers, this centuries-old operation looks remarkably like a preview of the industrial methods that would transform manufacturing only much later.

questions.q: According to the passage, how quickly could the Arsenal assemble a fully equipped warship when pressed?
questions.why: 短文指兵工廠在緊急時可於一天內組裝出一艘裝備齊全的戰艦。

questions.q: The word 'uniform' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: uniform 此處指樣式一致、統一規格。

questions.q: It can be inferred that interchangeable parts allowed the Arsenal to:
options: build ships more slowly but more cheaply
assemble and repair ships very quickly
employ fewer than a hundred workers
questions.why: 可互換零件使兵工廠能迅速組裝與修復船艦。

questions.q: Why was the Arsenal a strategic advantage for Venice despite its few resources?
options: It allowed Venice to avoid all naval battles
It let Venice trade only in luxury goods
It let Venice replace lost ships with startling speed
questions.why: 兵工廠使資源匱乏的威尼斯能快速補充損失的船艦,故具戰略優勢。

questions.q: Why does the author compare the Arsenal to later industrial methods?
options: To argue that Venice invented the modern factory
To highlight how far ahead of its time the Arsenal's organization was
To explain why Venice eventually declined
To describe the design of the warships
questions.why: 藉與後世工業方法相比,凸顯兵工廠的組織遠超其時代。

academic.title: The Untouched Instrument: The Theremin
academic.passage: Among the strangest musical instruments ever built is the theremin, invented in the 1920s by a Russian physicist, which is played without being touched at all. The device consists of a box fitted with two metal antennas, one rising vertically and one looping horizontally. When a performer moves a hand near the vertical antenna, the pitch of the sound rises or falls; moving the other hand toward the horizontal loop controls the volume. The instrument works by sensing the position of the player's hands, which slightly alter electric fields around the antennas. Because there are no keys, strings, or frets to guide the fingers, the performer must find every note in empty air, relying entirely on trained hearing and precise muscle memory. This makes the theremin notoriously difficult to play in tune, and its eerie, wavering voice, capable of gliding smoothly between pitches like a human singing, soon became a favourite for suggesting mystery or unease in film soundtracks. Its significance, however, reaches beyond its haunting sound. As one of the first electronic instruments, the theremin demonstrated that music could be produced entirely by electric circuits rather than by vibrating strings or columns of air. In doing so it pointed toward the vast world of electronic music that later generations of inventors and musicians would go on to explore.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does a performer change the pitch on a theremin?
options: By pressing a row of keys
By blowing into a narrow tube
By moving a hand near the vertical antenna
questions.why: 短文指演奏者將手靠近垂直天線來改變音高。

questions.q: The word 'eerie' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: eerie 此處形容怪異、令人不安的音色。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the theremin is difficult to play in tune because:
options: it is extremely heavy to hold
there are no keys or frets to guide the fingers
it can only ever play one note
questions.why: 沒有琴鍵或品格引導手指,須全憑聽覺與肌肉記憶,故難以準確。

questions.q: Why is the theremin described as historically significant beyond its sound?
options: It was the loudest instrument of its time
It required no electricity to operate
It was very easy for beginners to learn
It showed that music could be made entirely by electric circuits
questions.why: 它證明音樂可完全由電子電路產生,故意義超越其音色本身。

questions.q: Why does the author mention film soundtracks?
options: To argue that the theremin replaced whole orchestras
To illustrate how the instrument's eerie sound was used
To explain how the antennas are built
questions.why: 以電影配樂為例,說明其怪異音色的實際運用。

academic.title: Taming the Horse on the Steppe
academic.passage: The domestication of the horse was one of the most consequential events in human history, yet it long remained difficult to pin down, since the bones of an early tame horse look much like those of a wild one. The most likely setting was the grassy steppe of Central Asia, where the animals were once hunted for meat. Determining when herding gave way to riding has challenged archaeologists for decades. One promising line of evidence comes from the teeth of ancient horses. When a horse is controlled with a bit, the mouthpiece of a bridle, the metal or bone can leave a distinctive pattern of wear on certain teeth, a mark that would not appear in a wild animal. Traces of milk proteins and the remains of enclosures offer further clues that herds were being managed rather than merely hunted. The consequences of riding were enormous. Mounted herders could control far larger flocks and travel distances that would exhaust a person on foot, spreading languages, goods, and technologies across the vast interior of a continent. In time the horse would carry not only riders but wheeled vehicles and, much later, the mounted warriors who reshaped the map of Asia and Europe. Few relationships between humans and another species have so thoroughly transformed the course of events.

questions.q: According to the passage, where was the horse most likely first domesticated?
options: The grassy steppe of Central Asia
The high mountains of the Andes
questions.why: 短文指馬最可能於中亞的草原上被馴化。

questions.q: The word 'consequential' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: consequential 意為「影響深遠的、重大的」。

questions.q: It can be inferred that distinctive wear on a horse's teeth indicates that:
options: the horse ate only fresh grass
the horse was controlled with a bit and therefore ridden or driven
the horse had been hunted for meat
questions.why: 牙齒特有的磨損顯示馬曾被馬銜控制,故被騎乘或駕馭。

questions.q: Why is it difficult to identify early domesticated horses from bones alone?
options: Their bones decayed far too quickly
No horse bones have survived at all
Horses were never buried by people
The bones of tame horses look much like those of wild ones
questions.why: 早期馴馬的骨骼與野馬相似,故難以僅憑骨骼辨識。

questions.q: Why does the author discuss the spread of languages, goods, and technologies?
options: To explain why horses were first hunted
To describe the daily diet of steppe peoples
To explain how a horse's bit is made
To show the far-reaching consequences of horse riding
questions.why: 藉語言、貨物與技術的傳播,說明騎馬帶來的深遠影響。

academic.title: Mountain and Water: Chinese Landscape Painting
academic.passage: Chinese landscape painting, known as shan shui, or 'mountain-water,' is among the most esteemed traditions in the history of art, yet its aims differ sharply from those of Western landscape painting. A shan shui work was rarely a portrait of a particular place. Instead, the painter sought to capture the underlying spirit and order of nature, often combining remembered and imagined scenery into an ideal whole. Mountains rise through drifting mist, rivers wind into the distance, and tiny human figures, such as a fisherman or a scholar crossing a bridge, appear dwarfed by the immensity around them, expressing a philosophy in which people form a small part of a vast natural order. These paintings do not use a single fixed viewpoint. The eye is meant to travel through the scene as if on a journey, wandering along paths and up slopes, and a tall hanging scroll may lead the gaze steadily upward from foreground water to distant peaks. Much is left unpainted; areas of blank silk or paper suggest mist, water, or sky, and this emptiness is considered as expressive as the brushwork itself. Executed in ink, often with little or no colour, shan shui prizes the trained, spontaneous gesture of the brush, in which a few controlled strokes can suggest a whole range of mountains, rewarding contemplation rather than mere recognition.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did a shan shui painter mainly seek to capture?
options: The spirit and order of nature
An exact portrait of a real place
questions.why: 短文指山水畫家主要追求捕捉自然的精神與秩序。

questions.q: The word 'dwarfed' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: dwarfed 此處指在浩瀚景物襯托下顯得渺小。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the blank, unpainted areas of a shan shui painting are meant to:
options: show that the painter ran out of ink
mark where colour was later erased
questions.why: 留白處旨在暗示霧、水或天空,而非未完成。

questions.q: The absence of a single fixed viewpoint supports the idea that the viewer is meant to:
options: measure the height of the mountains
travel through the scene as if on a journey
questions.why: 沒有單一固定視點,呼應觀者應如遊歷般在畫中神遊。

questions.q: Why does the author contrast shan shui with Western landscape painting?
options: To argue that Western painting is superior
To explain how ink is manufactured
To claim that the two traditions are identical
To highlight the distinctive aims of shan shui
questions.why: 藉與西方風景畫對比,凸顯山水畫獨特的追求。

academic.title: Iceland's Althing
academic.passage: When Norse settlers colonized Iceland in the ninth and tenth centuries, they arrived at an island with no king and no existing government. Around the year 930 they established the Althing, a general assembly that met once a year and is often described as one of the oldest surviving parliaments in the world. Each summer, free men travelled from across the island to a dramatic open-air site, a broad plain flanked by rifted cliffs, where they camped for two weeks. There, an official known as the law-speaker recited aloud a portion of the laws from memory, since they were not yet written down, cycling through the whole body of law over three years. The assembly settled disputes, adjudicated cases, and adopted new laws, and it functioned without any central executive to enforce its rulings. This was its great weakness. A court might declare a man guilty, but carrying out the sentence was left to the wronged party and their supporters, so that justice ultimately depended on the balance of power among rival families. When powerful chieftains fell into prolonged feuds that the assembly could not contain, the system eventually broke down, and in the thirteenth century Iceland lost its independence to the Norwegian crown. Yet the Althing endured in some form and remains a source of national pride.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did the law-speaker do at the Althing?
options: Crown a new king each year
Recite a portion of the laws aloud from memory
questions.why: 短文指宣法人憑記憶朗誦部分法律。

questions.q: The word 'adjudicated' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: adjudicated 意為「裁決、審理案件」。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the laws were recited aloud because:
options: the settlers could not hear very well
reciting was faster than enforcing them
they had not yet been written down
questions.why: 因法律尚未成文,故須由宣法人口誦相傳。

questions.q: According to the passage, what was the main weakness of the Althing?
options: It met too rarely to pass any laws
It excluded all free men from attending
Its meeting site was difficult to reach
It had no central authority to enforce its rulings
questions.why: 議會缺乏中央權力來執行判決,是其根本弱點。

questions.q: Why does the author mention that the Althing 'remains a source of national pride'?
options: To argue that Iceland should have kept a king
To explain exactly how the laws were enforced
To note the assembly's lasting significance despite its breakdown
To describe the geography of the island
questions.why: 說明即使制度曾崩解,議會至今仍具長遠意義,是民族自豪的來源。

academic.title: The Power of Network Effects
academic.passage: A network effect occurs when the value of a product or service increases as more people use it. Consider a telephone system: a single telephone is useless, but each additional user makes the entire network more valuable to everyone connected to it. This dynamic, sometimes called demand-side economies of scale, distinguishes many modern digital platforms from traditional manufacturers. For a factory, producing more units eventually raises costs; for a social network, each new member potentially benefits all existing members at little additional expense. Economists distinguish between direct network effects, where users benefit from others using the same product, and indirect effects, where one group benefits from the growth of a complementary group. A video game console, for instance, becomes more attractive as developers create more titles for it, which in turn draws more players. Because early leadership can become self-reinforcing, markets governed by strong network effects often tend toward concentration, with one or two firms capturing most users. Critics warn that such dominance may entrench incumbents and stifle competition, since newcomers struggle to attract users away from an established network. Yet history shows that even entrenched platforms can be displaced when a rival offers sufficiently superior value.

questions.q: According to the passage, a network effect occurs when:
options: a firm lowers its production costs by making more units
the value of a product rises as more people use it
developers create titles for a game console
questions.why: 短文開頭明確定義網路效應為使用者增加時價值上升。

questions.q: The word 'entrench' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: entrench 意為鞏固、使根深蒂固,故最接近 firmly establish。

questions.q: It can be inferred that traditional manufacturers, unlike digital platforms:
options: rely entirely on indirect network effects
can add customers at no cost whatsoever
tend to face rising costs as they expand output
are completely immune to competition from rivals
questions.why: 短文對比工廠生產更多最終提高成本,可推論傳統製造業擴產時成本上升。

questions.q: Why does the author mention a video game console?
options: To prove that consoles are better than telephones
To illustrate how indirect network effects operate
To argue that gaming markets never concentrate
To show that network effects are declining
questions.why: 遊戲主機的例子用來說明間接網路效應的運作方式。

questions.q: The primary purpose of the passage is to:
options: explain network effects and their consequences for markets
criticize digital platforms for harming consumers
provide instructions for building a social network
compare the prices of consoles and telephones
questions.why: 全文旨在解釋網路效應概念及其對市場的影響。

academic.title: The Bystander Effect
academic.passage: In 1964, social psychologists began investigating a puzzling phenomenon: why individuals often fail to help a person in distress when others are present. Their research identified what became known as the bystander effect, the tendency for people to be less likely to offer assistance when they are part of a large group. Two mechanisms appear to drive this behavior. The first is diffusion of responsibility, whereby each witness assumes that someone else will intervene, so that the felt obligation to act is divided among all those present. The second is pluralistic ignorance, in which people look to others for cues about how to behave; if everyone remains calm, each observer concludes that the situation is not truly an emergency. Laboratory experiments confirmed these ideas. When participants believed they were the sole witness to an emergency, most responded quickly. When they thought others were also aware, response rates fell sharply and help arrived more slowly. Importantly, the effect does not indicate that people are callous. Rather, it reflects how social context shapes individual perception and action. Later studies suggested that the effect can be reduced when a bystander clearly identifies one person and directly requests help, thereby overriding the diffusion of responsibility that a crowd produces.

questions.q: According to the passage, the bystander effect refers to:
options: people being less likely to help when others are present
the tendency to help faster when in a large group
the calmness that emergencies naturally produce
a method for training emergency responders
questions.why: 短文定義旁觀者效應為群體在場時人們較不願伸出援手。

questions.q: The word 'callous' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: callous 指冷漠無情,最接近 unfeeling。

questions.q: It can be inferred that pluralistic ignorance depends on:
options: observers interpreting others' calm as a signal
the complete absence of any witnesses
questions.why: 多數無知源於旁觀者將他人的平靜解讀為情況不緊急的訊號。

questions.q: The mention of laboratory experiments serves to:
options: provide evidence supporting the two proposed mechanisms
contradict the idea of diffusion of responsibility
show that the bystander effect does not exist
describe how emergencies are staged for films
questions.why: 實驗被用來提供支持兩種機制的證據。

questions.q: What is the author's main purpose?
options: to blame witnesses for their moral failings
to explain the bystander effect and its causes
to argue that groups always respond faster
to recount the entire history of psychology
questions.why: 作者主旨在解釋旁觀者效應及其成因。

academic.title: The Trap of Sunk Costs
academic.passage: Economists define a sunk cost as an expense that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered, regardless of future decisions. Rational analysis holds that such costs should be irrelevant to choices about the future, because no action can undo them. Only prospective costs and benefits ought to guide a decision. Yet people and organizations frequently violate this principle, a pattern known as the sunk cost fallacy. Having invested substantial money, time, or effort into a project, decision-makers feel compelled to continue, even when abandoning it would be wiser. A firm may pour additional funds into a failing product line simply because it has already spent heavily on development. Psychologists attribute this behavior partly to loss aversion, the tendency to weigh losses more heavily than equivalent gains, and partly to a desire to avoid appearing wasteful. Admitting that earlier resources were misspent can feel like conceding failure, so individuals persist in order to justify their prior commitment. The consequences can be severe, ranging from personal financial harm to large-scale corporate disasters. Recognizing the fallacy is the first step toward avoiding it; effective managers learn to evaluate ongoing projects solely on their expected future returns, treating money already spent as gone and therefore irrelevant.

questions.q: According to the passage, a sunk cost is:
options: a future expense that can still be avoided
an expense already incurred that cannot be recovered
the profit expected from a new product
a tax imposed on failing firms
questions.why: 短文定義沉沒成本為已發生且無法回收的支出。

questions.q: The word 'prospective' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: prospective 指未來的、預期的,故最接近 related to the future。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a rational decision-maker would:
options: continue a project mainly because much was already invested
always abandon any project that requires more funding
weigh past losses more heavily than future gains
disregard money already spent when deciding what to do next
questions.why: 理性決策者應忽略已花費的沉沒成本,只依未來效益判斷。

questions.q: The author mentions loss aversion in order to:
options: explain one psychological cause of the fallacy
prove that the fallacy does not really exist
recommend spending more on failing projects
define what legally counts as a sunk cost
questions.why: 提到損失趨避是為了解釋沉沒成本謬誤的心理成因之一。

questions.q: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: celebrating the success of well-managed firms
listing the tax advantages of new products
describing a common reasoning error and how to avoid it
questions.why: 全文主要在描述沉沒成本謬誤這個常見推理錯誤及其避免之道。

academic.title: Language and Thought
academic.passage: Does the language we speak shape the way we think? This question lies at the heart of the hypothesis of linguistic relativity, often associated with the linguist Benjamin Whorf. In its strongest form, the hypothesis claims that language determines thought, so that speakers of different languages perceive reality in fundamentally different ways. Most contemporary scholars reject this extreme version, since people can clearly conceive of ideas for which their language has no single word. A weaker version, however, has gained empirical support. It proposes that language influences, rather than dictates, habitual patterns of thought. Studies of color perception offer a well-known example. Languages divide the color spectrum differently; some draw a boundary between blue and green where others do not. Experiments suggest that speakers more readily distinguish colors that their language names separately, though they remain capable of seeing all shades. Similar effects appear in how languages describe space, time, and number. Researchers must be cautious, however, for it is difficult to separate the influence of language from that of culture, since the two are deeply intertwined. The debate remains active, but the emerging consensus is that language is one factor among many that subtly guides cognition, without imprisoning the mind within rigid linguistic boundaries.

questions.q: According to the passage, the strong form of linguistic relativity claims that:
options: language has no effect on thought at all
culture is always more important than language
language determines how speakers perceive reality
all languages divide colors in the same way
questions.why: 短文指出強式假說主張語言決定思維與對現實的知覺。

questions.q: The word 'intertwined' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: intertwined 指緊密交織,最接近 closely connected。

questions.q: The example of color perception suggests that language:
options: makes some colors physically invisible to speakers
can influence which distinctions people notice readily
has no relationship to how people see color
determines the total number of colors that exist
questions.why: 顏色知覺的例子顯示語言會影響人們較易察覺哪些區別。

questions.q: Why does the author note that language and culture are intertwined?
options: to prove the strong hypothesis is entirely correct
to suggest that culture should be ignored entirely
to argue that all languages are essentially identical
to explain why the evidence is difficult to interpret
questions.why: 指出語言與文化交織,是要說明為何相關證據難以解讀。

questions.q: The main purpose of the passage is to:
options: present a balanced overview of linguistic relativity
prove that language completely controls thought
teach readers how to learn a new language
list every color that human languages can name
questions.why: 全文旨在對語言相對論提供一個平衡的概觀。

academic.title: Charging Different Prices
academic.passage: Why does the same airline seat cost one traveler far more than another? The answer often lies in price discrimination, the practice of charging different customers different prices for essentially the same good or service. Contrary to what the term might suggest, this behavior is usually legal and can even benefit some consumers. Economists identify three degrees of price discrimination. First-degree, or perfect, discrimination involves charging each buyer the maximum they are willing to pay; it is rare because sellers seldom know precisely what each customer values the good at. Second-degree discrimination charges different prices based on quantity or version purchased, as when bulk buyers receive discounts. Third-degree discrimination divides customers into groups, such as students or senior citizens, and sets a distinct price for each. For any of these strategies to succeed, three conditions must hold. The seller must possess some market power, must be able to separate customers according to their willingness to pay, and must prevent resale between groups, since otherwise low-price buyers could undercut the seller by reselling to others. When these conditions are met, price discrimination allows firms to capture more of the value they create, and it can extend access to customers who would not purchase at a single uniform price.

questions.q: According to the passage, third-degree price discrimination:
options: charges each buyer the maximum they will pay
divides customers into groups with distinct prices
is generally illegal in most countries
requires customers to resell the product
questions.why: 短文說明三級差別定價將顧客分組並對各組設不同價格。

questions.q: The word 'undercut' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: undercut 指以更低價格搶生意,故最接近 sell below the seller's price。

questions.q: It can be inferred that preventing resale matters because:
options: resale would directly raise the seller's costs
students never resell any of their purchases
resale could let low-price buyers compete with the seller
resale is required for a firm to have market power
questions.why: 防止轉售之所以重要,是因為低價買家可能轉售而與賣方競爭。

questions.q: The author mentions airline seats at the beginning to:
options: argue that flying is far too expensive
introduce the topic with a familiar example
prove that price discrimination is illegal
questions.why: 開頭以機票座位為例,是用熟悉情境引入主題。

questions.q: The passage mainly serves to:
options: condemn firms that charge different prices
advise consumers on how to avoid paying any fees
compare airlines with other industries in detail
explain the types and conditions of price discrimination
questions.why: 全文主要在說明差別定價的類型與成立條件。

academic.title: The Value of Social Capital
academic.passage: Social capital refers to the networks of relationships, shared norms, and mutual trust that enable people to act together effectively. Unlike physical capital, which consists of tangible tools and machines, or human capital, which resides in the skills of individuals, social capital exists in the connections between people. Sociologists distinguish two broad forms. Bonding social capital links people who are similar to one another, such as members of a close-knit family or a tight community; it provides emotional support and reinforces shared identity. Bridging social capital, by contrast, connects people across social divides, linking different groups and granting access to wider resources and information. Both forms carry benefits, but they serve different functions. Communities rich in bonding ties may enjoy strong solidarity yet remain insular, whereas bridging ties tend to foster tolerance and opportunity. Researchers have linked high levels of social capital to numerous positive outcomes, including better public health, lower crime, and more responsive government. However, social capital is not uniformly benign. The same tight bonds that support a group can also exclude outsiders or sustain harmful practices. Moreover, some scholars worry that in many societies civic participation has declined, weakening the trust and cooperation on which democratic institutions ultimately depend.

questions.q: According to the passage, bridging social capital:
options: links people who are very similar to one another
connects different groups across social divides
consists mainly of tools and machines
always reduces tolerance within a community
questions.why: 短文指出橋接型社會資本連結跨越社會分界的不同群體。

questions.q: The word 'insular' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: insular 指封閉、與外界隔絕,最接近 inward-looking and closed off。

questions.q: It can be inferred that bonding social capital can be harmful when:
options: it connects too many different groups at once
it produces an excessive amount of tolerance
it excludes outsiders or sustains bad practices
it improves the public health of a community
questions.why: 可推論凝聚型社會資本在排斥外人或維持有害習慣時會造成傷害。

questions.q: Why does the author contrast social capital with physical and human capital?
options: to argue that social capital is worthless
to clarify what makes social capital distinctive
to explain in detail how machines are built
to prove that individual skills do not matter
questions.why: 將社會資本與實體、人力資本對比,是為了釐清社會資本的獨特之處。

questions.q: The author's primary aim is to:
options: explain social capital, its forms, and its mixed effects
argue that all communities are essentially identical
discourage people from forming any relationships
provide detailed statistics on national economies
questions.why: 作者主旨在說明社會資本的概念、類型及其正負兼具的影響。

academic.title: The Tragedy of the Commons
academic.passage: In 1968, the ecologist Garrett Hardin popularized a parable that has since shaped debates in economics and environmental policy. Imagine a pasture open to all, where herders graze their cattle. Each herder gains the full benefit of adding another animal, yet the cost of overgrazing is shared among everyone. Acting in rational self-interest, each herder adds more cattle, and the pasture is eventually destroyed. This is the tragedy of the commons: individually rational choices producing a collectively disastrous outcome. The logic applies to many shared resources, from fisheries and forests to clean air and water, all of which can be depleted when no one bears the full cost of their use. Traditional solutions fall into two camps. Some economists advocate privatization, assigning ownership so that a single owner internalizes both the benefits and the costs. Others favor government regulation, using quotas or taxes to limit exploitation. Yet the political scientist Elinor Ostrom challenged this stark choice. Studying real communities, she documented numerous cases in which people governing a shared resource devised their own rules, monitored one another, and sustained the commons for generations without either privatization or external control. Her work demonstrated that collective self-governance, though not guaranteed, is a genuine and often overlooked possibility.

questions.q: According to the passage, the tragedy of the commons describes:
options: rational individual choices leading to collective disaster
governments that always protect shared resources
herders who cooperate perfectly from the very start
pastures that can never be depleted at all
questions.why: 短文指出公地悲劇是個體理性選擇導致集體災難。

questions.q: The word 'depleted' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: depleted 指耗盡、枯竭,最接近 exhausted or used up。

questions.q: Elinor Ostrom's research implies that:
options: privatization is always the single best solution
shared resources inevitably collapse in every case
communities can sometimes manage commons themselves
government control is the only possible alternative
questions.why: Ostrom 的研究顯示社群有時能自行治理公共資源。

questions.q: The author mentions fisheries and forests in order to:
options: prove that Hardin was completely mistaken
show that the logic applies beyond pastures
argue that all resources are privately owned
describe the daily diet of grazing cattle
questions.why: 提到漁場與森林是為了說明公地悲劇的邏輯不限於牧場。

questions.q: The passage is primarily intended to:
options: present the commons problem and differing responses to it
prove that self-interest is always harmful to society
recount the detailed biography of Garrett Hardin
promote cattle farming as a profitable livelihood
questions.why: 全文旨在呈現公地問題及對此的不同解決方案。

academic.title: Ladders and Barriers: Social Mobility
academic.passage: Social mobility describes the movement of individuals or groups between different positions in a society's hierarchy of wealth, status, and power. Sociologists distinguish several types. Vertical mobility involves moving up or down the social ladder, while horizontal mobility means shifting between positions of roughly equal rank. Intergenerational mobility compares a person's standing with that of their parents, whereas intragenerational mobility tracks changes across a single individual's lifetime. A society is often called open if talent and effort largely determine one's position, and closed if birth and inherited status dominate. In reality, no society is perfectly open, but the degree of mobility varies considerably. Researchers measure it by asking, for example, how likely a child born into the poorest families is to reach the wealthiest ranks as an adult. Contrary to popular belief, countries that celebrate opportunity most loudly do not always exhibit the highest mobility. Education is frequently described as the great equalizer, yet unequal access to quality schooling can instead reproduce existing advantages across generations. Wealthy families transmit not only money but also social connections, cultural knowledge, and expectations that ease their children's ascent. Understanding these mechanisms helps explain why inequality can prove remarkably persistent even in societies formally committed to equal opportunity.

questions.q: According to the passage, intergenerational mobility compares:
options: two positions of roughly equal social rank
a person's standing with that of their parents
changes occurring over one person's lifetime
a nation's wealth with its political power
questions.why: 短文定義代際流動為比較個人與其父母的社會地位。

questions.q: The word 'ascent' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: ascent 指上升、攀升,最接近 rise upward。

questions.q: The passage implies that education can:
options: either reduce or reproduce existing inequality
prevent any child from ever advancing
questions.why: 短文暗示教育既可能縮小也可能複製不平等。

questions.q: Why does the author mention countries that celebrate opportunity?
options: to show belief in mobility does not ensure high mobility
to prove those countries have the most mobility
to argue that opportunity is entirely unimportant
to list the world's very wealthiest nations
questions.why: 提到高唱機會的國家,是要說明對流動的信念未必等同高流動性。

questions.q: The main purpose of the passage is to:
options: rank countries purely by their total wealth
argue that individual effort never matters at all
describe in detail how public schools are funded
explain social mobility and why inequality can persist
questions.why: 全文旨在解釋社會流動及不平等為何持續存在。

academic.title: Owners, Managers, and the Agency Problem
academic.passage: When the people who own a company are not the same as those who run it, a fundamental tension can arise. Shareholders own modern corporations, but professional managers make the daily decisions. This separation of ownership and control gives rise to what economists call the principal-agent problem. The principals, in this case the shareholders, want the agents, the managers, to act in the owners' best interests. Managers, however, may pursue their own goals, such as higher salaries, greater prestige, or an easier life, which do not always align with maximizing shareholder value. Because owners cannot observe every action a manager takes, information is distributed unequally, and monitoring is costly. Corporate governance refers to the system of rules, practices, and incentives designed to address this problem. Boards of directors are meant to supervise management on behalf of shareholders. Compensation packages that tie a manager's pay to the firm's performance, such as stock options, attempt to align the interests of both parties. External forces, including the threat of takeover and the scrutiny of financial markets, add further discipline. Yet none of these mechanisms is perfect. Well-designed governance reduces the agency problem but rarely eliminates it, and poorly designed incentives can even encourage the reckless behavior they were meant to prevent.

questions.q: According to the passage, the principal-agent problem arises because:
options: owners and managers may have differing goals
shareholders always run the company themselves
managers usually own most of the corporation
questions.why: 短文指出委託代理問題源於所有者與經理人目標可能不一致。

questions.q: The word 'scrutiny' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: scrutiny 指仔細審視,最接近 close examination。

questions.q: It can be inferred that stock options are intended to:
options: increase managers' salaries regardless of performance
make managers' interests match those of owners
remove the board of directors entirely
prevent shareholders from owning the firm
questions.why: 可推論股票選擇權旨在使經理人利益與所有者利益一致。

questions.q: The author mentions the threat of takeover to:
options: argue that takeovers always harm shareholders
prove that corporate governance is unnecessary
give an example of external discipline on managers
describe the process by which managers are hired
questions.why: 提到收購威脅是舉例說明對經理人的外部約束。

questions.q: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: praising managers for their tireless hard work
explaining the steps for buying company shares
proving that shareholders are always right
explaining the agency problem and how governance addresses it
questions.why: 全文主要在說明代理問題及公司治理如何加以因應。

academic.title: The Discomfort of Conflicting Beliefs
academic.passage: In 1957, the psychologist Leon Festinger proposed a theory that transformed the study of attitudes. He argued that people experience psychological discomfort, which he termed cognitive dissonance, when they hold two or more beliefs that contradict one another, or when their actions clash with their beliefs. Because this discomfort is unpleasant, individuals are motivated to reduce it, restoring a sense of internal consistency. Festinger identified several strategies for doing so. A person might change one of the conflicting beliefs, acquire new information that supports one side, or downplay the importance of the inconsistency altogether. A classic illustration involves smokers who know that smoking damages health. Rather than quitting, some convince themselves that the risks are exaggerated, or that the pleasure outweighs the danger. Perhaps the most striking finding concerns effort. When people invest great effort to achieve something that turns out to be disappointing, they often persuade themselves that it was worthwhile, since admitting otherwise would create dissonance. This helps explain why difficult initiations can intensify loyalty to a group. The theory has proven remarkably influential, illuminating behavior in fields ranging from marketing to politics. It reminds us that human beings are not merely rational processors of information, but active managers of their own psychological comfort.

questions.q: According to the passage, cognitive dissonance is:
options: a feeling of joy at learning new facts
the process of making purely rational decisions
a deliberate strategy for improving one's health
questions.why: 短文定義認知失調為持有相互矛盾信念時產生的不適感。

questions.q: The word 'downplay' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: downplay 指淡化、輕描淡寫,最接近 treat as unimportant。

questions.q: The example of smokers suggests that people may reduce dissonance by:
options: immediately changing their behavior for good
seeking professional medical treatment at once
openly sharing their worries with others
convincing themselves the risks are small
questions.why: 吸菸者的例子顯示人們可能藉由說服自己風險不大來減輕失調。

questions.q: Why does the author mention difficult initiations?
options: to argue that groups should be easy to join
to illustrate how effort can intensify loyalty
to prove that dissonance cannot be reduced
to describe a common medical procedure
questions.why: 提到艱難的入會儀式是為了說明付出的努力如何強化忠誠。

questions.q: The main purpose of the passage is to:
options: advise readers on how best to quit smoking
explain cognitive dissonance and its effects
prove that humans are perfectly rational beings
questions.why: 全文主旨在解釋認知失調及其影響。

academic.title: How New Ideas Spread
academic.passage: Why do some innovations sweep through a society while others languish? The sociologist Everett Rogers offered an influential answer in his theory of the diffusion of innovations. Rogers observed that the adoption of a new product or practice typically follows a predictable pattern over time, resembling an S-shaped curve. At first, only a small number of adventurous individuals, whom Rogers called innovators, take up the novelty. They are followed by early adopters, respected members of a community who help legitimize the innovation for others. As confidence grows, the early and late majority join in, until finally the laggards, the most cautious members, adopt it, if they ever do. Rogers also identified characteristics that make an innovation more likely to spread. It must offer a clear relative advantage over existing options, be compatible with people's values and needs, and be reasonably simple to understand. The ability to try an innovation on a limited basis, and to observe others using it, further accelerates adoption. These insights carry practical weight. Companies launching new technologies often target early adopters first, trusting them to influence the broader market. Public health campaigns likewise rely on trusted community members to promote beneficial practices, recognizing that who endorses an idea can matter as much as the idea itself.

questions.q: According to the passage, early adopters are:
options: the most cautious members of a community
respected members who help legitimize an innovation
the very first individuals to try anything new
people who never adopt any innovations
questions.why: 短文指出早期採用者是能為創新賦予正當性的受尊敬成員。

questions.q: The word 'languish' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: languish 指停滯不前、萎靡,最接近 fail to progress。

questions.q: It can be inferred that an innovation spreads more slowly if it:
options: offers a clear advantage over old options
is easy to understand and to try out
is difficult to understand and complex
can be readily observed in use by others
questions.why: 可推論若創新難以理解且複雜,擴散會較慢。

questions.q: Why do companies often target early adopters first?
options: because early adopters are the cheapest customers
because early adopters never influence anyone else
because they strongly dislike new technologies
because early adopters can influence the broader market
questions.why: 企業先鎖定早期採用者,是因為他們能影響更廣大的市場。

questions.q: The passage is mainly intended to:
options: explain how innovations spread and what aids adoption
prove that all innovations eventually fail
criticize people who resist any new ideas
describe the detailed biography of Everett Rogers
questions.why: 全文主要在解釋創新如何擴散以及有助於採用的因素。

academic.title: Newcomers and the Process of Assimilation
academic.passage: When people migrate to a new society, they and their descendants often undergo a gradual process of change known as assimilation, through which they come to resemble the surrounding population in language, customs, and outlook. For much of the twentieth century, social scientists pictured this process as a straight line: each generation of immigrants was expected to move steadily toward full absorption into the mainstream, shedding the culture of origin along the way. Later scholarship complicated this tidy picture. Researchers noticed that assimilation does not always proceed smoothly or in a single direction. Some immigrant groups advance economically while retaining strong ethnic identities, and others may assimilate into disadvantaged segments of society rather than a uniform mainstream. This more nuanced view, sometimes called segmented assimilation, holds that outcomes depend on the resources a group brings, the reception it encounters, and the opportunities available to it. Discrimination, for instance, can block the very integration that a straight-line model predicts. Moreover, assimilation is not merely something that happens to newcomers; the host society itself changes as it absorbs new foods, words, and traditions. Understanding these dynamics matters greatly, for how a society incorporates its immigrants shapes not only their prospects but the character of the community as a whole.

questions.q: According to the passage, assimilation is a process by which newcomers:
options: come to resemble the surrounding population
completely reject the entire host society
force the mainstream to change entirely
return permanently to their country of origin
questions.why: 短文定義同化為新移民逐漸與周遭人口在語言與習俗上趨於相似。

questions.q: The word 'nuanced' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: nuanced 指細緻、有細微差別,最接近 subtle and detailed。

questions.q: The concept of segmented assimilation implies that:
options: all immigrants follow one identical path
ethnic identity must be abandoned quickly
different groups can have very different outcomes
questions.why: 分段同化的概念暗示不同群體可能有截然不同的結果。

questions.q: Why does the author mention discrimination?
options: to prove the straight-line model is always correct
to argue that immigrants never face any obstacles
to show a factor that can block integration
to describe the foods a host society adopts
questions.why: 提到歧視是為了指出一個可能阻礙融入的因素。

questions.q: The passage primarily aims to:
options: prove that host societies never change
rank immigrant groups by their success
trace how thinking about assimilation has evolved
questions.why: 全文主旨在梳理關於同化的思考如何演變。

academic.title: The Logic of Comparative Advantage
academic.passage: Few ideas in economics are as counterintuitive, or as powerful, as the principle of comparative advantage. Developed by David Ricardo in the early nineteenth century, it explains why trade can benefit two parties even when one of them is more efficient at producing everything. The key lies in distinguishing absolute advantage from comparative advantage. A country has an absolute advantage when it can produce a good using fewer resources than another. It has a comparative advantage, however, when it can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost, that is, by giving up less of some other good. Ricardo's insight was that mutually beneficial trade depends on comparative, not absolute, advantage. Suppose one nation is better at producing both cloth and wine, but its edge in wine is far greater. It should specialize in wine and import cloth, while its partner specializes in cloth. By each concentrating on what it sacrifices least to produce, both nations can consume more than they could in isolation. This principle underlies the modern case for international trade and specialization. Critics rightly note that the theory abstracts away from real complications, such as unemployment during transitions and unequal bargaining power. Even so, comparative advantage remains a cornerstone of economic reasoning about why nations trade.

questions.q: According to the passage, a country has a comparative advantage when it can produce a good:
options: using far more resources than its partner
that no other country happens to want
while holding an absolute advantage in everything
at a lower opportunity cost than others
questions.why: 短文定義比較利益為以較低機會成本生產某商品。

questions.q: The word 'counterintuitive' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: counterintuitive 指違反直覺,最接近 contrary to common expectation。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a nation should specialize in the good for which it:
options: holds no absolute advantage at all
uses the greatest amount of resources
questions.why: 可推論一國應專門生產其犧牲最少的商品。

questions.q: The author mentions unemployment during transitions in order to:
options: prove that trade is always harmful
show that Ricardo was completely wrong
argue that nations should never trade
acknowledge a real-world limitation of the theory
questions.why: 提到轉型期失業是為了承認該理論在現實中的一項侷限。

questions.q: The passage is primarily written to:
options: discourage all forms of international trade
explain the principle of comparative advantage
list the goods that every nation produces
questions.why: 全文主旨在解釋比較利益原理。

academic.title: How Social Norms Emerge
academic.passage: Social norms are the unwritten rules that govern behavior within a group, specifying what its members ought to do and what they may expect from others. Unlike formal laws, norms are rarely written down and are enforced not by courts but by social approval and disapproval. A person who violates a norm may face nothing more than a disapproving glance, yet such informal sanctions can powerfully shape conduct. How do norms arise in the first place? Some emerge to solve recurring problems of coordination. When people must act in concert, such as deciding which side of the road to drive on, any consistent rule serves better than none, and one convention becomes established through repeated interaction. Other norms address conflicts between individual and collective interests, encouraging people to cooperate rather than exploit one another. Once a norm takes hold, it tends to persist, partly because individuals internalize it, coming to feel that following it is simply right. Norms also spread through imitation, as people copy the behavior of those they admire or wish to resemble. Yet norms are not permanent. When enough members quietly disagree with a prevailing rule, a norm can unravel surprisingly quickly, and a new standard may take its place, demonstrating that even deeply held conventions rest on ongoing mutual expectations.

questions.q: According to the passage, social norms are enforced mainly by:
questions.why: 短文指出社會規範主要靠社會的贊許與不贊許來強制執行。

questions.q: The word 'sanctions' in the passage is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: 此處 sanctions 指針對行為的懲罰性反應,故選最接近者。

questions.q: The example of driving on one side of the road illustrates a norm that:
options: resolves a conflict between selfish individuals
is enforced strictly by formal courts
questions.why: 靠道路一側行駛的例子說明一種解決協調問題的規範。

questions.q: Why does the author note that people internalize norms?
options: to prove norms are written into formal law
to explain why norms tend to persist
to argue that norms never spread at all
to show that norms are always harmful
questions.why: 提到人們內化規範,是為了解釋規範為何傾向持續存在。

questions.q: The main purpose of the passage is to:
options: argue that norms should be replaced by laws
prove that social norms never change
list every social norm found in a society
explain how social norms emerge and persist
questions.why: 全文主旨在解釋社會規範如何形成並延續。

academic.title: Nudging Better Choices
academic.passage: Traditional economics assumes that people make decisions rationally, weighing costs and benefits to maximize their well-being. Behavioral economics challenges this assumption, showing that human choices are systematically influenced by context, emotion, and mental shortcuts. Building on these findings, scholars have advanced the idea of the nudge, a small change in the way choices are presented that steers people toward better decisions without forbidding any option or altering economic incentives. The arrangement of choices, known as choice architecture, turns out to matter enormously. Consider retirement savings. When employees must actively sign up for a pension plan, many never get around to it. But when enrollment is automatic, with the option to opt out, participation rises dramatically, even though the available choices are identical. The default option exerts a quiet but strong pull, because people tend to stick with whatever requires no effort. Nudges have been applied to encourage healthier eating, reduce energy consumption, and increase organ donation. Supporters praise them as inexpensive and respectful of freedom, since no choice is removed. Critics, however, question who decides what counts as a better outcome, and worry that nudges can manipulate people without their awareness. The debate highlights a deeper tension between guiding behavior and preserving genuine autonomy in an age of sophisticated persuasion.

questions.q: According to the passage, a nudge:
options: forbids people from choosing certain options
changes the economic incentives people face
alters how choices are presented without removing any
forces people to save for their retirement
questions.why: 短文定義推力為改變選項呈現方式,但不禁止任何選項。

questions.q: The word 'steers' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: steers 指引導、導向,最接近 guides。

questions.q: The retirement example suggests that automatic enrollment works because:
options: it removes the pension option entirely
it changes the financial rewards of saving
people tend to stay with the default
questions.why: 退休例子顯示自動加入之所以有效,是因為人們傾向維持預設選項。

questions.q: Why does the author mention critics of nudging?
options: to prove that nudges never work in practice
to argue that all choices should be removed
to present concerns about manipulation and autonomy
to describe how pension funds are financed
questions.why: 提到批評者是為了呈現關於操控與自主的疑慮。

questions.q: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: proving that people are always perfectly rational
explaining nudges and the debate surrounding them
instructing readers on how to invest wisely
condemning behavioral economics as entirely false
questions.why: 全文主要在說明推力概念及圍繞它的爭論。

academic.title: Kinship and Social Organization
academic.passage: Among the earliest concerns of anthropology was kinship, the web of relationships based on descent, marriage, and shared ancestry that organizes social life in every human society. Kinship does far more than describe who is related to whom. In many societies, it determines where people live, whom they may marry, how property passes between generations, and where authority resides. Anthropologists have documented a striking variety of kinship systems. Descent may be traced through the father's line, through the mother's line, or through both. In patrilineal societies, membership and inheritance pass through male ancestors, whereas in matrilineal societies they pass through female ancestors, though this does not necessarily mean that women hold political power. The terms people use for relatives also vary widely. Some languages employ the same word for one's father and one's father's brother, reflecting a social system in which these men play similar roles. Such terminology is not a quirk of vocabulary but a window into how a society classifies relationships and assigns obligations. Early anthropologists sometimes assumed that unfamiliar kinship arrangements were primitive stages on a path toward the family forms they knew. Modern scholars reject this assumption, recognizing instead that each system represents a coherent solution to the universal challenge of organizing human relationships.

questions.q: According to the passage, in matrilineal societies, membership and inheritance pass through:
options: both parents equally in all cases
the father's line of male ancestors
questions.why: 短文指出母系社會的成員身分與繼承是透過女性祖先傳遞。

questions.q: The word 'quirk' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: quirk 指奇特之處、怪癖,最接近 odd feature。

questions.q: The shared word for father and father's brother suggests that:
options: the two men are biologically identical
the language simply has too few words
the two men play similar social roles
kinship has no effect on daily life
questions.why: 父親與父親兄弟共用一詞,暗示這兩位男性扮演相似的社會角色。

questions.q: Why does the author mention early anthropologists' assumptions?
options: to firmly endorse the idea of primitive stages
to prove all kinship systems are the same
to explain how property is inherited
to contrast them with the modern view
questions.why: 提到早期人類學家的假設,是為了與現代觀點形成對比。

questions.q: The passage is primarily intended to:
options: show the variety and importance of kinship systems
prove that one kinship system is clearly best
argue that kinship no longer matters today
teach readers the words for their own relatives
questions.why: 全文主旨在呈現親屬制度的多樣性與重要性。

academic.title: The Fragility of Efficient Supply Chains
academic.passage: For decades, manufacturers pursued efficiency by minimizing the inventory they held. Under the just-in-time system, pioneered in Japanese automobile factories, parts arrive precisely when they are needed, rather than sitting in expensive warehouses. This approach reduces storage costs and frees up capital, and it long served as a model of lean, disciplined production. Yet the same leanness that makes such supply chains efficient also makes them fragile. Because little inventory buffers the system, any disruption, whether a natural disaster, a factory closure, or a sudden surge in demand, can quickly halt production across an entire network. Modern supply chains compound this vulnerability through their global reach. A single component may cross several countries before reaching final assembly, so a problem in one region can ripple around the world. Recent global crises exposed these weaknesses vividly, as shortages of parts idled factories far from the original disruption. In response, some firms have begun to rethink the single-minded pursuit of efficiency. They are building resilience by holding larger reserves of critical parts, cultivating multiple suppliers rather than relying on one, and locating some production closer to home. Such measures raise costs, illustrating a fundamental trade-off: a supply chain optimized purely for efficiency may prove dangerously brittle when the unexpected occurs.

questions.q: According to the passage, under the just-in-time system, parts:
options: are stored in large warehouses for months
arrive precisely when they are needed
are produced entirely within one country
are never affected by any disruptions
questions.why: 短文指出及時生產制度下,零件在需要時才恰好送達。

questions.q: The word 'brittle' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: brittle 指脆弱易斷,最接近 easily broken。

questions.q: It can be inferred that holding larger reserves of parts:
options: makes supply chains even more fragile
has no real effect on production
eliminates the need for any suppliers
increases resilience but also raises costs
questions.why: 可推論持有較多零件庫存能提升韌性,但也增加成本。

questions.q: The author mentions components crossing several countries in order to:
options: explain why disruptions can spread worldwide
prove that global trade is always safe
argue that warehouses are entirely unnecessary
questions.why: 提到零件跨越多國,是為了說明干擾為何會蔓延至全球。

questions.q: The main purpose of the passage is to:
options: praise just-in-time production without any reservation
explain the steps for building a warehouse
examine the trade-off between efficiency and resilience
prove that global supply chains are entirely harmless
questions.why: 全文主旨在探討效率與韌性之間的取捨。
