academic.title: How Societies Remember
academic.passage: Memory is often imagined as a private possession, locked within an individual mind. Yet the sociologist Maurice Halbwachs argued that memory is also profoundly social. He introduced the concept of collective memory to describe the shared recollections that a group holds about its past. According to Halbwachs, even our personal memories are shaped by the social groups to which we belong, for we recall events using frameworks that our families, communities, and nations provide. Collective memory is not simply the sum of individual memories, nor is it identical to formal history. Whereas historians strive for a critical and documented account, collective memory serves the present needs of a group, emphasizing events that reinforce its identity and quietly forgetting those that do not. Monuments, commemorations, textbooks, and rituals all work to sustain a shared version of the past. Because such memory is bound up with identity, it can become fiercely contested. Different groups may remember the same event in incompatible ways, and struggles over how the past should be represented are often struggles over power in the present. What a society chooses to memorialize, and what it allows to fade, reveals much about its values. Collective memory, in this sense, is less a fixed record than an ongoing act of interpretation.

questions.q: According to the passage, collective memory is:
options: exactly identical to formal written history
simply the sum of all individual memories
the shared recollection a group holds of its past
questions.why: 短文定義集體記憶為群體對其過去所共享的記憶。

questions.q: The word 'contested' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: contested 指有爭議的,最接近 disputed。

questions.q: It can be inferred that collective memory differs from history because it:
options: serves the present needs of a group
never forgets any past event at all
is written only by professional historians
questions.why: 可推論集體記憶不同於歷史,因它服務於群體當下的需求。

questions.q: Why does the author mention monuments and commemorations?
options: to argue that they distort all of history
to prove that memory is purely individual
to describe how museums are funded
to give examples of how shared memory is sustained
questions.why: 提到紀念碑與紀念活動,是舉例說明共享記憶如何被維繫。

questions.q: The passage is primarily concerned with:
options: proving that personal memory is unreliable
ranking historical events by their importance
explaining the concept of collective memory
describing how the brain physically stores information
questions.why: 全文主要在說明集體記憶的概念。

academic.title: Creative Destruction and Economic Change
academic.passage: The economist Joseph Schumpeter argued that capitalism is, by its very nature, a process of ceaseless transformation. He coined the phrase creative destruction to capture the way in which new innovations continually displace older products, technologies, and firms. For Schumpeter, this was not a flaw in the system but its central engine. Entrepreneurs introduce novel goods, methods, and forms of organization, and in doing so they overturn established ways of doing business. The gramophone gives way to newer means of recording sound; the horse-drawn carriage yields to the automobile. Each wave of innovation creates new industries and fortunes even as it renders others obsolete. This perspective carries important implications. It suggests that economic progress is inseparable from disruption, and that attempts to protect existing firms from competition may preserve jobs in the short run while slowing the innovation that raises living standards over time. The process is rarely painless, for workers and communities tied to declining industries bear real costs, and these losses are often concentrated while the gains are diffuse. Schumpeter's vision thus poses an enduring dilemma for policymakers: how to encourage the dynamism that drives prosperity while cushioning those whom it displaces. His concept remains a compelling lens for understanding why economies must change to grow.

questions.q: According to the passage, the phrase 'creative destruction' describes how:
options: new innovations displace older products and firms
economies remain permanently stable over time
entrepreneurs avoid taking any risks at all
questions.why: 短文指出創造性破壞描述新創新如何取代舊有產品與企業。

questions.q: The word 'obsolete' is closest in meaning to:
questions.why: obsolete 指過時、被淘汰,最接近 no longer useful。

questions.q: It can be inferred that protecting existing firms from competition may:
options: always benefit the economy in the long run
slow the innovation that raises living standards
have no effect on innovation whatsoever
questions.why: 可推論保護既有企業免於競爭,可能拖慢提升生活水準的創新。

questions.q: The author mentions the horse-drawn carriage and the automobile to:
options: argue that older technologies are always better
prove that innovation never causes any harm
illustrate how new innovations replace old ones
explain how carriages were once manufactured
questions.why: 提到馬車與汽車是為了說明新創新如何取代舊事物。

questions.q: The passage is primarily intended to:
options: argue that economies should never change
explain how to manufacture new products
explain Schumpeter's idea of creative destruction and its implications
questions.why: 全文主旨在說明熊彼得的創造性破壞概念及其意涵。

academic.title: The Resilient Tardigrade
academic.passage:
Tardigrades, often called water bears, are microscopic invertebrates renowned for surviving conditions that would kill nearly any other animal. When their watery habitat dries out, tardigrades enter a dormant state known as cryptobiosis. During this process, an individual expels most of the water from its body and curls into a compact, barrel-shaped form called a tun. In the tun state, metabolic activity slows almost to zero, and the creature can persist for years without food or moisture.

Scientists once believed that ordinary sugars protected the animals during desiccation, but recent research points to a different mechanism. Tardigrades produce specialized proteins that, as the body dries, transform into a glass-like matrix. This matrix encases delicate cellular structures, holding proteins and membranes in place so they do not collapse. When water returns, the glassy material dissolves and the animal revives within minutes.

This tolerance extends beyond drought. Tun-state tardigrades have endured temperatures near absolute zero, extreme pressure, and the vacuum of space during orbital experiments. Understanding how their proteins stabilize living tissue could help researchers preserve vaccines, cells, and other biological materials without refrigeration, a possibility that has attracted considerable attention from medical laboratories.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to a tardigrade's body when it forms a tun?
options: It expels most of its internal water and curls into a compact shape.
It absorbs extra moisture to swell into a barrel form.
It increases its metabolic rate to conserve energy.
It produces sugars that replace its cell membranes.
questions.why: 第一段說明水熊蟲排出體內大部分水分並蜷曲成緊實形狀，故選第一項。

questions.q: The word 'desiccation' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: desiccation 指乾燥脫水，與文中乾涸情境一致，故選第二項。

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that the older explanation involving sugars was
options: replaced by an account based on protective proteins
the reason tardigrades survive in space
questions.why: 文中說近期研究指向不同機制（蛋白質），暗示舊有糖分解釋已被取代，故選第三項。

questions.q: Which detail best supports the claim that tardigrade tolerance extends beyond drought?
options: They curl into a barrel-shaped tun.
Their glassy matrix dissolves in water.
They revive within minutes of rehydration.
They have survived near absolute zero, extreme pressure, and the vacuum of space.
questions.why: 耐受力超出乾旱的證據是能撐過近絕對零度、高壓與太空真空，故選第四項。

questions.q: Why does the author mention vaccines and cells at the end of the passage?
options: To illustrate a practical application of understanding tardigrade proteins.
To argue that tardigrades are dangerous to medical labs.
To prove that sugars preserve biological material.
To explain why tardigrades enter cryptobiosis.
questions.why: 結尾提到疫苗與細胞是為了說明研究水熊蟲蛋白質的實際應用，故選第一項。

academic.title: The Puzzle of Superconductivity
academic.passage:
When certain metals are cooled to extremely low temperatures, they suddenly lose all resistance to electric current. This phenomenon, called superconductivity, allows electricity to flow through a material indefinitely without losing energy as heat. It was first observed in 1911, when a Dutch physicist chilled mercury with liquid helium and watched its resistance vanish completely.

For decades the effect defied explanation. Ordinary conductors resist current because moving electrons collide with vibrating atoms in the metal lattice, scattering their energy. In a superconductor, however, electrons somehow travel in coordinated pairs that glide through the lattice without scattering. These pairs form only when thermal vibrations are weak enough, which is why the material must be kept intensely cold.

A striking consequence is that superconductors expel magnetic fields from their interiors. Because of this property, a small magnet placed above a superconducting disk will hover in midair, held aloft by the excluded field. Engineers have exploited such behavior to build powerful electromagnets for medical scanners and experimental trains that float above their tracks.

The great obstacle remains temperature. Cooling equipment is costly and cumbersome, so laboratories worldwide are searching for materials that superconduct closer to room temperature, a discovery that could transform how societies generate and transmit power.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did the Dutch physicist observe in 1911?
options: The resistance of mercury vanished when it was chilled with liquid helium.
Electrons stopped moving inside a metal lattice.
A magnet floated above a warm metal disk.
questions.why: 文中提到 1911 年荷蘭物理學家用液氦冷卻水銀，觀察到電阻完全消失，故選第二項。

questions.q: The word 'expel' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: expel 指將磁場排出體外，與 push out 意思相符，故選第三項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that superconductivity fails at higher temperatures because
options: electrons move too slowly to pair up
stronger thermal vibrations break apart the electron pairs
questions.why: 文中說電子對只在熱振動夠弱時形成，可推論高溫下較強振動會拆散電子對，故選第四項。

questions.q: Which statement best explains why a magnet can hover above a superconductor?
options: The superconductor excludes the magnetic field, creating a force that supports the magnet.
The superconductor heats the magnet until it rises.
The magnet loses its own field near the cold disk.
Electrons in the magnet begin to travel in pairs.
questions.why: 磁鐵懸浮是因超導體排斥磁場而產生支撐力，故選第一項。

questions.q: The author mentions medical scanners and floating trains mainly to
options: warn about the dangers of strong magnets
give examples of how superconductors are put to use
questions.why: 提到醫療掃描儀與懸浮列車是舉例說明超導體的應用，故選第二項。

academic.title: How Cephalopods Change Color
academic.passage:
Octopuses, squid, and cuttlefish can transform their appearance in a fraction of a second, shifting from mottled brown to vivid stripes almost instantly. This talent depends on tiny organs in the skin called chromatophores. Each chromatophore is a sac of pigment surrounded by a ring of muscles. When the muscles contract, the sac stretches wide and its color becomes visible; when they relax, the sac shrinks to a nearly invisible dot.

The animal's nervous system controls these muscles directly, which is why the changes are so rapid. Beneath the chromatophores lie other cells that reflect light, producing shimmering blues and greens that pigments alone cannot create. Together these layers give cephalopods an enormous palette.

Curiously, most cephalopods are colorblind, yet they match the hues of their surroundings with remarkable precision. Researchers suspect the skin itself may detect light, or that the animals judge brightness and contrast rather than color. Whatever the method, the disguise serves two purposes: hiding from predators and startling them with sudden flashes.

Beyond camouflage, color patterns function as signals. Male cuttlefish, for instance, can display an aggressive pattern toward a rival on one side of the body while showing a courtship pattern to a female on the other, effectively sending two messages at once.

questions.q: According to the passage, what makes a chromatophore's color become visible?
options: Light reflecting from cells beneath the skin.
The pigment sac shrinking to a small dot.
Surrounding muscles contracting to stretch the sac wide.
The animal detecting color with its eyes.
questions.why: 文中說肌肉收縮使色素囊撐大而顯色，故選第三項。

questions.q: The word 'mottled' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: mottled 指帶有斑駁色塊，與 marked with patches 相符，故選第四項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that cephalopods' ability to match their surroundings is surprising because
options: they are mostly colorblind yet still match colors accurately
they have no muscles in their skin
they cannot move their chromatophores quickly
their pigments can produce blue and green
questions.why: 文中指出多數頭足類色盲卻能精準配色，這正是令人驚訝之處，故選第一項。

questions.q: Which example best supports the idea that color patterns can serve as signals?
options: A chromatophore shrinking to an invisible dot.
A male cuttlefish showing rival and courtship patterns on opposite sides.
The nervous system controlling skin muscles.
questions.why: 雄性烏賊兩側同時展示挑釁與求偶圖案，最能支持色彩作為訊號，故選第二項。

questions.q: The author discusses reflective cells beneath the chromatophores in order to
options: prove that cephalopods can see color
explain why cephalopods are slow to change
explain how colors like blue and green are produced
questions.why: 提到反光細胞是為解釋藍綠等色彩如何產生，故選第三項。

academic.title: Reading the Ocean Floor
academic.passage:
For most of history, the deep ocean floor was imagined as an ancient, unchanging plain. In the twentieth century, however, surveys of the seabed revealed a vast mountain range winding through every ocean, split along its crest by a deep valley. This discovery reshaped scientists' understanding of the planet's surface.

At these underwater ridges, molten rock rises from below and hardens into new crust. As fresh material is added, the older crust is pushed outward in both directions, widening the ocean basin over millions of years. The process, called seafloor spreading, means the ocean floor is continually manufactured at the ridges and eventually consumed where it plunges back into the interior at deep trenches.

The most convincing evidence came from magnetism. As molten rock cools, iron-bearing minerals align with Earth's magnetic field, which reverses direction at irregular intervals. The seafloor therefore preserves a series of magnetic stripes, arranged symmetrically on either side of a ridge like a mirror image. These matching bands record the steady outward march of the crust.

Seafloor spreading became a cornerstone of plate tectonics, the theory that Earth's outer shell is broken into rigid plates. The idea explained why continents drift, why earthquakes cluster in belts, and why volcanoes rise where plates meet.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens at underwater ridges?
options: Old crust is pulled down into deep trenches.
The ocean basin narrows over time.
Molten rock rises and hardens into new crust.
questions.why: 文中說中洋脊處熔岩上升並凝固成新地殼，故選第四項。

questions.q: The word 'consumed' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: consumed 指地殼在海溝被吞回消滅，與 taken back in 相符，故選第一項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the magnetic stripes are symmetrical because
options: new crust forms at the ridge and spreads equally to both sides
questions.why: 對稱磁條紋是因新地殼在脊處生成並向兩側等量擴張，故選第二項。

questions.q: Which finding most strongly supported the idea of seafloor spreading?
options: The ocean floor is a flat, ancient plain.
Magnetic stripes form matching mirror-image bands across a ridge.
Continents are made of lighter rock.
questions.why: 對稱如鏡像的磁條紋是最有力的支持證據，故選第三項。

questions.q: In the last paragraph, the author lists earthquakes and volcanoes mainly to
options: argue that plate tectonics is still unproven
show what the theory of plate tectonics successfully explains
questions.why: 列舉地震與火山是為展示板塊構造理論能成功解釋的現象，故選第四項。

academic.title: The Hidden Network Beneath Forests
academic.passage:
Beneath the floor of a healthy forest lies a web of connections invisible to the casual observer. Most plants form partnerships with fungi that wrap around or penetrate their roots. These fungal partners, called mycorrhizae, extend thread-like filaments far into the soil, reaching water and minerals that roots alone could never gather.

The relationship benefits both sides. The fungus delivers nutrients such as phosphorus to the plant, and in return the plant supplies sugars produced above ground. Because a single fungus can link the roots of many separate plants, an entire stand of trees may be joined into one interconnected system, sometimes described as a shared underground economy.

Experiments using labeled carbon have shown that resources can travel through these fungal threads from one tree to another. A large tree growing in sunlight may pass surplus sugar to a shaded seedling struggling on the forest floor. Some scientists argue that older trees can even nourish their own offspring through the network.

Not every exchange is cooperative, however. Certain plants tap into the network without contributing, stealing nutrients from their neighbors. The system is therefore best understood not as pure generosity but as a marketplace, where resources flow according to supply, demand, and the differing interests of the organisms involved.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do plants provide to their fungal partners?
questions.why: 文中說植物供給地上製造的糖分給真菌，故選第一項。

questions.q: The word 'stand' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: a group of trees growing together
questions.why: 此處 stand 指一片生長在一起的樹群，故選第二項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a shaded seedling connected to the network is
options: unable to form any fungal partnership
cut off from all nearby trees
more likely to survive because it may receive sugar from larger trees
the main supplier of nutrients to older trees
questions.why: 文中說大樹會把多餘糖分傳給遮蔭幼苗，可推論其存活機會較高，故選第三項。

questions.q: Which point best supports the author's claim that the network is not pure generosity?
options: Fungi extend filaments into the soil.
Older trees may nourish their offspring.
Some plants take nutrients without giving anything in return.
questions.why: 有些植物只取不予，最能支持並非純粹慷慨的說法，故選第四項。

questions.q: The author describes the system as a marketplace in order to
options: convey that resources flow according to supply and demand rather than kindness
prove that trees can think like merchants
argue that forests should be protected by law
questions.why: 以市場比喻是要表達資源依供需流動而非出於善意，故選第一項。

academic.title: Pulsars: Cosmic Lighthouses
academic.passage:
In 1967, a graduate student examining radio signals noticed a source in the sky that pulsed with astonishing regularity, flashing every 1.3 seconds. The precision was so exact that the team briefly wondered whether the signal came from an intelligent civilization. The true explanation proved even stranger: the pulses came from a neutron star.

A neutron star is the collapsed core left behind when a massive star explodes. Gravity crushes its matter so tightly that a mass greater than the Sun's is packed into a sphere only about twenty kilometers across. A single spoonful of this material would weigh billions of tons on Earth.

Such stars spin rapidly and possess intense magnetic fields. Charged particles trapped by these fields are flung outward along two narrow beams of radiation streaming from the magnetic poles. If a beam happens to sweep across Earth as the star rotates, observers detect a brief pulse, much as a lighthouse appears to blink when its rotating beam passes an onlooker. The star itself shines steadily; only its rotation creates the flashing.

Because their spin is so steady, pulsars serve as remarkably accurate natural clocks. Astronomers use them to test theories of gravity and even to detect ripples in space produced by distant colliding objects.

questions.q: According to the passage, how large is a typical neutron star?
options: About the same size as the Sun.
A sphere only about twenty kilometers across.
questions.why: 文中說中子星直徑約二十公里，故選第二項。

questions.q: The word 'flung' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: flung 指被強力拋出，與 thrown with force 相符，故選第三項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that we see a pulsar blink only when
options: one of its radiation beams sweeps across Earth
questions.why: 文中把脈衝比作燈塔，只有輻射束掃過地球時才見閃爍，故選第四項。

questions.q: Which detail best explains why researchers first suspected an intelligent source?
options: The signal pulsed with extraordinary regularity.
The beams came from the magnetic poles.
questions.why: 訊號極其規律才使研究者一度懷疑是文明所發，故選第一項。

questions.q: The comparison to a lighthouse is used mainly to
options: suggest pulsars guide ships at sea
help readers picture how a steady star produces flashing pulses
questions.why: 燈塔比喻是幫助讀者理解穩定發光的星如何產生閃爍脈衝，故選第二項。

academic.title: How Migrating Birds Find Their Way
academic.passage:
Each year, countless birds travel thousands of kilometers between breeding and wintering grounds, often returning to the very same patch of forest. How they navigate across featureless oceans and unfamiliar terrain has long puzzled biologists. Evidence now suggests that many species can sense Earth's magnetic field and use it as a compass.

Unlike a simple needle compass, a bird's magnetic sense appears to read the angle at which field lines dip toward the ground rather than distinguishing north from south. This angle grows steeper as a bird approaches the poles, providing a rough sense of latitude. Experiments in which the surrounding magnetic field is artificially tilted cause captive birds to orient in the wrong direction, confirming that they rely on this cue.

The mechanism behind the sense remains debated. One leading idea holds that light striking special molecules in the bird's eye triggers reactions sensitive to magnetic fields, allowing the bird to perceive direction as a pattern overlaid on its vision. Another proposes that tiny crystals of iron in the beak act as detectors.

Magnetism is not the birds' only tool. They also read the position of the setting sun and the rotation of the stars, combining several cues. When one source of information conflicts with another, birds recalibrate, weighing each signal against the others.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does a bird's magnetic sense primarily detect?
options: The difference between north and south.
The angle at which field lines dip toward the ground.
The color of the setting sun.
questions.why: 文中說鳥類感測磁力線向地面傾斜的角度，故選第三項。

questions.q: The word 'featureless' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: featureless 指缺乏明顯地標的，故選第四項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that tilting the magnetic field around captive birds
options: misled them into orienting the wrong way
had no effect on their behavior
caused them to stop migrating forever
questions.why: 文中說人工傾斜磁場使鳥定向錯誤，可推論它們被誤導，故選第一項。

questions.q: Which statement best supports the idea that birds rely on more than one navigational cue?
options: They can return to the same patch of forest.
They read the sun and stars in addition to magnetism, and recalibrate when cues conflict.
Their magnetic sense reads the dip of field lines.
Iron crystals may sit in their beaks.
questions.why: 鳥同時利用太陽、星象並在衝突時重新校準，最能支持多重線索，故選第二項。

questions.q: The author mentions iron crystals in the beak mainly to
options: prove that birds cannot see magnetic fields
explain how birds read the stars
present a second proposed explanation for the magnetic sense
describe how birds build their nests
questions.why: 提到喙中鐵晶體是提出磁感的第二種可能解釋，故選第三項。

academic.title: The Origin of the Auroras
academic.passage:
The shimmering curtains of light known as auroras, visible near the poles, are the product of a distant connection between Earth and the Sun. The Sun constantly releases a stream of charged particles called the solar wind. When this wind reaches Earth, most of it is deflected by the planet's magnetic field, which acts as a protective shield surrounding the globe.

Some particles, however, are funneled along magnetic field lines toward the polar regions. There they collide with atoms of gas high in the atmosphere. Each collision transfers energy to an atom, briefly exciting it. As the atom returns to its normal state, it releases that energy as a flash of light. Multiplied across countless collisions, these flashes create the glowing displays.

The color of an aurora depends on which gas is struck and at what altitude. Oxygen high in the atmosphere tends to glow red, while oxygen lower down produces the familiar green. Nitrogen can add fringes of blue and purple.

Auroras intensify after violent eruptions on the Sun hurl extra particles toward Earth. During such storms the lights may appear far from the poles, occasionally reaching latitudes where they are almost never seen. The same storms can disturb radio communication and disrupt the electrical grids on which modern life depends.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to most of the solar wind when it reaches Earth?
options: It collides with oxygen to glow red.
It is funneled toward the equator.
It is deflected by Earth's magnetic field.
questions.why: 文中說大部分太陽風被地球磁場偏轉，故選第四項。

questions.q: The word 'funneled' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: funneled 指沿磁力線被導引集中，與 channeled 相符，故選第一項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that when an atom returns to its normal state, it
options: gives off the energy it had gained as light
questions.why: 文中說原子回到常態時釋放能量為光，可推論其放出先前獲得的能量，故選第二項。

questions.q: Which fact best explains why auroras can appear in different colors?
options: The solar wind travels at high speed.
Earth's magnetic field surrounds the globe.
Different gases at different altitudes emit different colors.
questions.why: 不同高度的不同氣體發出不同顏色，最能解釋極光色彩差異，故選第三項。

questions.q: The author mentions radio communication and electrical grids in order to
options: explain how auroras produce green light
describe the structure of the magnetic field
prove that the Sun is shrinking
point out that solar storms can affect human technology
questions.why: 提到無線電與電網是指出太陽風暴會影響人類科技，故選第四項。

academic.title: Light in the Deep Sea
academic.passage:
Below a few hundred meters, sunlight fades and the ocean sinks into permanent darkness. Yet this vast realm is far from lightless. A great many deep-sea creatures generate their own glow through a chemical process called bioluminescence, in which a molecule reacts with oxygen to release energy as light rather than heat. The light is often blue, the color that travels farthest through seawater.

Animals put this ability to varied uses. Some anglerfish dangle a glowing lure to draw curious prey within reach of their jaws. Certain shrimp do the opposite, spewing a cloud of luminous fluid to dazzle an attacker while they escape into the gloom. Many small organisms flash briefly to startle or confuse whatever threatens them.

A subtler trick is counterillumination. Seen from below, an animal would normally appear as a dark silhouette against the faint light filtering from above. To erase this outline, some fish and squid produce a soft glow on their undersides that matches the dimness overhead, rendering them nearly invisible to predators lurking beneath.

Not all light is made by the animals themselves. Some fish house colonies of luminous bacteria in special organs, feeding and sheltering the microbes in exchange for a dependable source of light. The partnership benefits both, an arrangement common in the deep.

questions.q: According to the passage, what color is deep-sea bioluminescence most often?
options: Blue, because it travels farthest through seawater.
Red, because it is easiest to produce.
questions.why: 文中說生物發光多為藍色，因藍光在海水中傳得最遠，故選第一項。

questions.q: The word 'dazzle' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: dazzle 指以強光使對方目眩，故選第二項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that counterillumination works by
options: making an animal glow brighter than the surface
matching the dim light from above so no dark outline shows
attracting prey toward a glowing lure
questions.why: 反照明使腹部發光配合上方微光，消除暗色輪廓，故選第三項。

questions.q: Which example best supports the claim that animals use light in varied ways?
options: Blue light travels farthest in seawater.
Sunlight fades below a few hundred meters.
Light is produced without much heat.
Anglerfish lure prey while shrimp blind attackers and flee.
questions.why: 鮟鱇引誘獵物、蝦以光逃脫，展現多樣用途，故選第四項。

questions.q: The author describes fish that house luminous bacteria in order to
options: show that some animals obtain light through partnership rather than making it themselves
prove that bacteria are harmful to fish
explain why blue light travels far
argue that the deep sea is completely dark
questions.why: 描述寄居發光細菌的魚是說明有些動物靠合作而非自體取得光，故選第一項。

academic.title: Dating the Past with Carbon
academic.passage:
Archaeologists often need to know the age of ancient wood, bone, or cloth. One of their most powerful tools is radiocarbon dating, a method that relies on a rare, unstable form of carbon known as carbon-14. This isotope forms continuously in the upper atmosphere when cosmic rays strike nitrogen atoms.

Carbon-14 mixes with ordinary carbon and is absorbed by plants during their growth. Animals then take it in by eating plants. As long as an organism is alive, it keeps replacing the carbon-14 that steadily decays, so its proportion of the isotope stays roughly constant. Once the organism dies, no new carbon enters, and the carbon-14 it contains begins to disappear at a fixed, predictable rate.

That rate is described by a half-life: about every 5,700 years, half of the remaining carbon-14 breaks down. By measuring how much carbon-14 is left in a sample and comparing it with the amount expected in living tissue, scientists can estimate how long ago the organism died.

The method has limits. After roughly 50,000 years, so little carbon-14 remains that measurement becomes unreliable. Results must also be adjusted, because the amount of carbon-14 in the atmosphere has varied over time. Even so, radiocarbon dating transformed archaeology by giving it a dependable clock.

questions.q: According to the passage, how is carbon-14 formed?
options: When cosmic rays strike nitrogen atoms in the upper atmosphere.
questions.why: 文中說碳十四是宇宙射線撞擊高層大氣中氮原子時形成，故選第二項。

questions.q: The word 'decays' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: decays 指碳十四隨時間衰變分解，故選第三項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a living organism's proportion of carbon-14 stays constant because
options: carbon-14 does not decay in living tissue
it continually takes in new carbon-14 to replace what decays
questions.why: 活體不斷攝入新碳十四以補充衰變的部分，故比例維持穩定，選第四項。

questions.q: Why does radiocarbon dating become unreliable for very old samples?
options: So little carbon-14 remains that it can no longer be measured accurately.
questions.why: 極古老樣本殘餘碳十四太少而難以準確測量，故選第一項。

questions.q: The author explains the concept of half-life mainly to
options: show how the remaining carbon-14 reveals the time since death
questions.why: 解釋半衰期是為說明殘餘碳十四如何揭示死亡至今的時間，故選第二項。

academic.title: The Dance Language of Honeybees
academic.passage:
When a foraging honeybee discovers a rich patch of flowers, it returns to the hive and shares the news through an elaborate movement known as the waggle dance. Performed on the vertical surface of the comb in darkness, the dance conveys both the direction and the distance of the food to the bee's nestmates, who follow closely to read the message.

The dancer runs forward in a straight line while rapidly shaking its body, then loops back to repeat the run, tracing a figure-eight pattern. The direction of the straight run encodes the bearing to the flowers. Because the bees cannot see the sun inside the hive, they use gravity as a reference: a run pointing straight up means fly toward the sun, while an angle to one side means fly at that same angle relative to the sun.

Distance is signaled by the duration of the waggling portion. A longer run indicates a more distant source, and the vigor of the shaking adds further information about the quality of the find.

Remarkably, the bees adjust the dance as the sun moves across the sky, so the same location may be described differently at different times of day. This built-in correction shows that the insects track the sun's changing position even while working in total darkness inside the hive.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does the direction of the straight run indicate?
options: The number of bees in the hive.
The bearing toward the food source.
questions.why: 文中說直線奔跑的方向編碼通往花朵的方位，故選第三項。

questions.q: The word 'vigor' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: vigor 指搖擺的力度與活力，故選第四項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a longer waggle run tells other bees that the food is
questions.why: 文中說搖擺時間越長代表來源越遠，可推論食物較遠，故選第一項。

questions.q: Which detail best shows that bees track the sun's movement?
options: They dance on a vertical comb.
They adjust the dance as the sun moves, describing the same spot differently over the day.
questions.why: 蜜蜂隨太陽移動調整舞蹈，最能顯示它們追蹤太陽，故選第二項。

questions.q: The author mentions that the dance occurs in darkness mainly to
options: explain why flowers are hard to find
emphasize that bees use gravity, not sight, as a reference
describe the shape of the comb
questions.why: 強調在黑暗中進行是為說明蜜蜂以重力而非視覺為參照，故選第三項。

academic.title: The Ocean's Global Conveyor
academic.passage:
The world's oceans are not still basins of water but a vast, slowly moving system that redistributes heat around the planet. Near the surface, warm currents driven partly by wind carry heat from the tropics toward the poles. But a deeper, quieter circulation is driven by differences in the density of seawater, a flow scientists call thermohaline circulation, named for the roles of heat and salt.

Seawater grows denser as it becomes colder and saltier. In the far North Atlantic, surface water chilled by frigid air sinks because it has turned dense and heavy. As this water descends, more surface water flows in to replace it, and the sinking water spreads slowly through the deep ocean toward other basins. Eventually it rises again in distant regions, completing a loop that may take a thousand years.

This circulation carries enormous consequences. The northward flow of warm surface water keeps parts of Europe far milder than their latitude would suggest. Nutrients stirred up where deep water rises support blooms of tiny organisms that feed marine food webs.

Scientists warn that the system could weaken. As polar ice melts, fresh water dilutes the salty surface layer, making it less dense and less able to sink. A slowdown could reshape climate patterns across whole continents.

questions.q: According to the passage, what causes surface water in the North Atlantic to sink?
options: Nutrients rising from the deep ocean.
Becoming cold and salty, and therefore dense.
questions.why: 文中說北大西洋表層水變冷變鹹而密度增大下沉，故選第四項。

questions.q: The word 'frigid' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: frigid 指極為寒冷，故選第一項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that parts of Europe are milder than expected because
options: warm surface water flows northward to them
deep water rises along their coasts
questions.why: 溫暖表層水北流使歐洲部分地區較溫和，故選第二項。

questions.q: Which statement best explains why melting polar ice could weaken the circulation?
options: It raises the temperature of tropical water.
It increases the salt content of the ocean.
Fresh water makes the surface layer less dense, so it sinks less easily.
It stirs up nutrients from the deep.
questions.why: 融冰帶來淡水降低表層密度，使其不易下沉，故選第三項。

questions.q: In the final paragraph, the author discusses melting ice mainly to
options: explain how salt dissolves in water
describe the feeding of marine organisms
prove that the ocean is warming everywhere
warn that the circulation system could slow and alter climate
questions.why: 討論融冰是警告該循環可能減弱並改變氣候，故選第四項。

academic.title: The Community Within
academic.passage:
The human digestive tract is home to trillions of microorganisms, mostly bacteria, that together form what is called the gut microbiome. Far from being mere passengers, these microbes perform tasks the body cannot manage alone. They break down certain fibers that human enzymes cannot digest, releasing compounds that nourish the cells lining the intestine and supplying a portion of the body's daily energy.

The composition of this community varies from person to person and shifts with diet. A meal rich in plant fiber tends to favor microbes that produce beneficial substances, whereas a diet heavy in fat and sugar can encourage a different, less helpful mix. Because the balance can change within days, what a person eats has surprisingly rapid effects on the population inside them.

The microbiome also influences the immune system. Early in life, exposure to a diverse range of microbes appears to train the body to distinguish genuine threats from harmless substances. A poorly developed community has been linked to allergies and other disorders in which the immune response goes awry.

Researchers are still untangling cause from effect. It is often unclear whether an unusual microbiome triggers a disease or simply results from it. Nonetheless, the gut community has emerged as a promising frontier in the study of human health.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do gut microbes do that the body cannot do alone?
options: Break down certain fibers that human enzymes cannot digest.
Replace the cells lining the intestine.
Store fat and sugar for later use.
questions.why: 文中說腸道微生物能分解人體酵素無法消化的纖維，故選第一項。

questions.q: The word 'composition' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: the makeup or mix of parts
questions.why: 此處 composition 指群落的組成與比例，故選第二項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a person's diet affects the microbiome
questions.why: 文中說平衡可在數天內改變，可推論飲食影響相當迅速，故選第三項。

questions.q: Which finding best supports the claim that the microbiome influences the immune system?
options: Microbes supply part of the body's energy.
Diet can change the community within days.
The community varies from person to person.
A poorly developed community has been linked to allergies and immune disorders.
questions.why: 發育不良的菌群與過敏及免疫失調有關，最能支持其影響免疫系統，故選第四項。

questions.q: The author mentions that cause and effect are hard to untangle in order to
options: acknowledge uncertainty about whether the microbiome causes disease or results from it
prove that the microbiome has no effect on health
argue that diet cannot change the microbiome
questions.why: 提到難分因果是承認尚不確定菌群是致病還是疾病造成，故選第一項。

academic.title: Passing Through Walls: Quantum Tunneling
academic.passage:
In everyday life, an object stopped by a barrier stays on one side unless it has enough energy to get over the top. A ball rolled at a hill will roll back if it cannot reach the crest. In the world of the very small, however, particles obey different rules, and one of the strangest is quantum tunneling.

According to quantum theory, a particle such as an electron does not have a single definite position but is described by a wave that gives the probability of finding it in various places. When this wave meets a barrier, it does not stop abruptly. A small part of it extends into and beyond the barrier, meaning there is a real chance the particle will appear on the far side even without enough energy to climb over. It has, in effect, passed through the wall.

The thinner the barrier, the greater the chance of tunneling. This sensitivity has been put to practical use. In one type of microscope, a sharp tip is moved just above a surface, and the tiny tunneling current that flows across the gap changes sharply with distance, allowing individual atoms to be mapped.

Tunneling is not a laboratory curiosity alone. It allows the nuclear reactions that power the Sun to proceed at temperatures that classical physics would deem far too low.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does quantum theory describe a particle's position?
options: As a wave giving the probability of finding it in various places.
As a ball rolling down a hill.
questions.why: 文中說量子理論以波描述粒子在各處出現的機率，故選第二項。

questions.q: The word 'abruptly' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: abruptly 指突然地，波不會在障礙處突然停止，故選第三項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that tunneling is more likely when a barrier is
questions.why: 文中說障礙越薄，穿隧機率越大，故選第四項。

questions.q: Which fact best supports the claim that tunneling has practical uses?
options: A microscope maps atoms using a tunneling current that varies with distance.
A ball rolls back down a hill.
questions.why: 顯微鏡利用隨距離變化的穿隧電流繪製原子，最能支持實際用途，故選第一項。

questions.q: The author mentions the Sun's nuclear reactions in order to
options: prove that the Sun is cooler than expected
show that tunneling matters in nature, not only in laboratories
describe how a ball rolls over a hill
questions.why: 提到太陽核反應是說明穿隧在自然界而非僅實驗室中也重要，故選第二項。

academic.title: How Bacteria Outwit Antibiotics
academic.passage:
Antibiotics rank among the most valuable discoveries in medicine, yet the bacteria they target are steadily learning to survive them. This growing problem, known as antibiotic resistance, arises through ordinary evolution acting at remarkable speed.

Within any large population of bacteria, random genetic differences mean a few individuals may happen to carry a trait that blunts a drug's effect. When an antibiotic is applied, it kills the vulnerable majority but spares these resistant few. The survivors then multiply freely, and before long the entire population descends from the resistant cells. Because bacteria reproduce in minutes and exist in astronomical numbers, this shift can occur with alarming rapidity.

Bacteria have an additional advantage. They can pass genes not only to their offspring but also sideways, handing fragments of DNA directly to unrelated neighbors. In this way a resistance gene that appears in one species can spread to others, assembling strains that shrug off several drugs at once.

Human behavior accelerates the trend. Taking antibiotics when they are not needed, or stopping a course too early, exposes bacteria to the drugs without wiping them out, precisely the conditions that favor resistant survivors. Doctors now urge more careful use, warning that without it, once-routine infections could again become difficult, and sometimes impossible, to treat.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens when an antibiotic is applied to a bacterial population?
options: It makes all bacteria reproduce faster.
It kills the vulnerable majority but spares resistant individuals.
questions.why: 文中說抗生素殺死脆弱多數，卻放過具抗性的少數，故選第三項。

questions.q: The word 'blunts' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: blunts 指削弱藥物效果，故選第四項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that bacteria evolve resistance quickly partly because
options: they reproduce in minutes and exist in enormous numbers
they never pass genes to their offspring
they live only in isolated groups
questions.why: 細菌數分鐘繁殖且數量龐大，故抗性演化迅速，選第一項。

questions.q: Which fact best explains how resistance can spread between different bacterial species?
options: Bacteria can hand DNA fragments directly to unrelated neighbors.
Doctors urge careful use of drugs.
questions.why: 細菌能把 DNA 片段直接交給不相關的鄰居，故抗性可跨物種傳播，選第二項。

questions.q: The author discusses stopping a course of antibiotics too early in order to
options: recommend taking antibiotics for every illness
show how human behavior can favor resistant bacteria
questions.why: 提到過早停藥是說明人類行為如何助長抗性細菌，故選第三項。

academic.title: Land That Rises After the Ice
academic.passage:
In parts of northern Scandinavia and Canada, the land is slowly rising out of the sea, lifting old harbors above the water and stranding former shorelines far inland. This gradual uplift is a delayed response to events that ended thousands of years ago, when great ice sheets covered these regions.

During the last ice age, sheets of ice several kilometers thick pressed down on the crust. The rock of Earth's outer shell is rigid, but the hotter layer beneath it behaves over long spans like an extremely thick fluid. Under the tremendous weight of the ice, this deep material slowly flowed sideways, and the crust sank, much as a cushion compresses under a heavy load.

When the ice melted, the burden was removed, but the deep material cannot flow back instantly. Over thousands of years it creeps into place, and the crust rebounds upward. In some areas the land is still rising by roughly a centimeter each year, a process geologists call isostatic rebound.

The effect complicates measurements of sea level, since a coastline can appear to fall simply because the land beneath it is climbing. By studying old beaches now stranded high above the tide, scientists reconstruct how much ice once lay on the land and how the crust has responded since.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happened to the crust during the last ice age?
options: It rose by a centimeter each year.
It melted along with the ice.
It flowed sideways like a fluid.
It sank under the weight of thick ice sheets.
questions.why: 文中說冰河期厚冰層的重量使地殼下沉，故選第四項。

questions.q: The word 'burden' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: burden 指冰的沉重負荷，與 heavy load 相符，故選第一項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the land rebounds slowly because
options: the deep material beneath the crust flows back only gradually
the crust is not rigid at all
questions.why: 地殼下方的深層物質只能緩慢回流，故回彈緩慢，選第二項。

questions.q: Which detail best supports the idea that the layer beneath the crust behaves like a thick fluid?
options: Old harbors are lifted above the water.
Under the weight of ice, the deep material slowly flowed sideways.
questions.why: 深層物質在冰重下緩慢橫向流動，最能支持其如厚流體，選第三項。

questions.q: The author mentions stranded old beaches mainly to
options: prove that sea level never changes
show how scientists reconstruct past ice and crustal movement
questions.why: 提到被抬高的古海灘是說明科學家如何重建過去的冰量與地殼運動，故選第四項。

academic.title: The Electric Sense of Fishes
academic.passage:
Many aquatic animals possess a sense that humans lack entirely: the ability to detect electric fields in the water around them. This capacity, called electroreception, is especially well developed in sharks, rays, and certain freshwater fishes, and it opens a channel of information hidden from our own senses.

Every living animal generates weak electric fields, produced by the activity of its muscles and nerves. A hidden fish or buried prey cannot conceal these faint signals. Sharks carry hundreds of tiny jelly-filled pores on their snouts that are exquisitely sensitive to such fields, enabling a hunter to locate a flatfish lying motionless beneath the sand, guided by nothing but the animal's own electrical leakage.

Some fishes go further and generate electric fields of their own. Species living in murky rivers, where vision is nearly useless, emit a steady stream of weak pulses. Objects nearby distort this self-made field, and the fish reads the distortions to sense its surroundings, in effect feeling its way through the dark by electricity rather than light.

A few fishes wield electricity as a weapon. The electric eel can discharge a powerful jolt strong enough to stun prey or deter an attacker. The same basic machinery, refined in different directions, thus serves for sensing, for navigation, and occasionally for defense.

questions.q: According to the passage, what produces the weak electric fields that all animals generate?
options: The activity of their muscles and nerves.
questions.why: 文中說所有動物的微弱電場由肌肉與神經活動產生，故選第一項。

questions.q: The word 'exquisitely' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: exquisitely 指極為敏感的程度，故選第二項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that fishes in murky rivers rely on electric fields because
options: their prey is always buried in sand
vision is nearly useless in cloudy water
questions.why: 混濁河水中視覺幾乎無用，故魚類依賴電場，選第三項。

questions.q: Which example best supports the claim that electroreception reveals hidden prey?
options: The electric eel can stun an attacker.
Fishes emit a steady stream of pulses.
All animals generate weak electric fields.
A shark locates a flatfish buried motionless under the sand.
questions.why: 鯊魚憑電場找到埋在沙下的比目魚，最能支持偵測隱藏獵物，選第四項。

questions.q: The final paragraph about the electric eel is included mainly to
options: show that the same electrical machinery can also be used as a weapon
prove that all fishes can stun prey
explain how sharks find buried fish
questions.why: 電鰻段落是說明同一套電力機制也能作為武器，故選第一項。

academic.title: Turning Pressure into Electricity
academic.passage:
Certain crystals have a remarkable property: when squeezed or stretched, they generate a small electric voltage, and when a voltage is applied to them, they change shape in return. This two-way behavior, called piezoelectricity, was first observed in the 1880s and has since become woven into countless everyday devices.

The effect arises from the arrangement of charges within the crystal. In an unstressed piezoelectric crystal, positive and negative charges are distributed so that their centers coincide and no overall voltage appears. Pressing on the crystal shifts the atoms slightly, separating the centers of positive and negative charge and producing a measurable voltage across its faces. Release the pressure, and the charges realign.

Because the effect works in both directions, piezoelectric materials serve as both sensors and sources of motion. In one familiar application, a crystal deforms when an electric signal is applied, vibrating thousands of times per second. These steady vibrations keep time in quartz watches with impressive accuracy.

The same principle drives ultrasound. A piezoelectric element vibrates to send sound waves into the body, then converts the returning echoes back into electric signals that form an image. From lighters that spark when pressed to sensitive microphones, piezoelectric crystals quietly translate between the worlds of motion and electricity.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens when a piezoelectric crystal is squeezed?
options: It generates a small electric voltage.
questions.why: 文中說壓電晶體受擠壓會產生微小電壓，故選第二項。

questions.q: The word 'coincide' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: coincide 指正負電荷中心重合於同一位置，故選第三項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that no voltage appears in an unstressed crystal because
options: the centers of positive and negative charge coincide
questions.why: 未受力時正負電荷中心重合，故不出現電壓，選第四項。

questions.q: Which detail best illustrates that the effect works in both directions?
options: A crystal deforms when a voltage is applied, and produces voltage when pressed.
The effect was discovered in the 1880s.
Charges realign when pressure is released.
questions.why: 施加電壓晶體變形、受壓又產生電壓，最能說明雙向效應，選第一項。

questions.q: The author mentions ultrasound mainly to
options: explain how quartz watches were invented
give an example of piezoelectricity converting between motion and electric signals
questions.why: 提到超音波是舉例說明壓電在運動與電訊號間的轉換，故選第二項。

academic.title: Surviving Winter by Slowing Down
academic.passage:
As winter approaches and food grows scarce, some mammals escape the hardship not by fleeing but by shutting much of their bodies down. This state, called hibernation, is far more profound than ordinary sleep. A hibernating ground squirrel lets its body temperature fall close to freezing, its heartbeat slow from hundreds of beats per minute to only a few, and its breathing become so faint as to be barely detectable.

By lowering its metabolism to a fraction of the normal rate, the animal drastically cuts how much energy it needs, allowing it to live for months on fat reserves built up during autumn. Without this economy, no store of fat could carry it through a long winter.

Hibernation is not a single unbroken sleep, however. Every week or two the animal briefly warms back to its normal temperature before cooling again. These arousals are puzzling, since rewarming burns a large share of the precious energy the animal is trying to conserve. Researchers suspect the periods serve essential functions, perhaps allowing the immune system to operate or letting the brain carry out maintenance that the cold state prevents.

Studying how hibernators protect their tissues through such extremes may one day guide medicine, from preserving organs for transplant to shielding patients during long operations.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to a hibernating ground squirrel's heartbeat?
options: It rises to hundreds of beats per minute.
It slows from hundreds of beats per minute to only a few.
It stays exactly the same as when awake.
questions.why: 文中說地松鼠冬眠時心跳從每分鐘數百次降到只有幾次，故選第三項。

questions.q: The word 'scarce' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: scarce 指稀少、難以取得，故選第四項。

questions.q: It can be inferred that lowering its metabolism helps the animal because
options: it needs much less energy and can live on stored fat
it can keep hunting through the winter
it no longer needs any fat reserves
questions.why: 降低代謝使耗能大減，可靠儲存脂肪度冬，選第一項。

questions.q: Why do the periodic arousals puzzle researchers?
options: They occur only once each winter.
Rewarming burns much of the energy the animal is trying to save.
They keep the animal's temperature near freezing.
They happen without any change in metabolism.
questions.why: 回暖會消耗大量本欲節省的能量，故令研究者困惑，選第二項。

questions.q: The author mentions organ transplants and long operations in order to
options: suggest possible medical applications of studying hibernators
describe how the immune system fails in the cold
questions.why: 提到器官移植與長時間手術是暗示研究冬眠動物可能的醫療應用，故選第三項。

academic.title: The Accidental Republic of Cospaia
academic.passage: In 1440, a clerical error created one of history's most improbable states. When Pope Eugene IV sold a stretch of Italian land to Florence to repay a loan, the two parties measured the boundary using different streams that happened to share the same name, the Rio. The narrow strip of territory between them, containing the village of Cospaia, belonged to neither buyer nor seller. Its roughly 300 inhabitants soon realized they answered to no external authority. Rather than petition for annexation, the villagers embraced their accidental independence and declared a republic with no government, no taxes, no written laws, and no army. Order was maintained instead by a council of family heads and by the church. Freed from taxation, Cospaia flourished as a haven for smugglers and, crucially, for tobacco cultivation, which neighboring states had banned or heavily taxed. The tiny republic prospered on this trade for nearly four centuries. Its unusual liberty finally ended in 1826, when the surrounding Papal States and Tuscany, tired of the smuggling, jointly absorbed the territory. As compensation, each Cospaian household received a silver coin. The republic's motto, inscribed above a church door, captured its ethos with disarming candor: perpetual and firm liberty.

questions.q: According to the passage, what caused the creation of the Republic of Cospaia?
options: A boundary was measured using two different streams that shared the same name.
The villagers rebelled against both Florence and the Papacy.
Pope Eugene IV deliberately granted the village its independence.
A war left the territory without any recognized ruler.
questions.why: 文中指出雙方以同名的兩條溪流劃界,才使中間地帶不屬於任何一方。

questions.q: The word 'embraced' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: embrace 在此指欣然接受,與 willingly accepted 意思最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that Cospaia's tobacco trade was profitable mainly because
options: the village had exceptionally fertile volcanic soil
the Pope secretly subsidized its tobacco farmers
neighboring states banned or taxed tobacco, giving Cospaia an advantage
tobacco could physically grow only in that region
questions.why: 由鄰國禁菸或重稅可推知,免稅的Cospaia因此在菸草貿易上佔有優勢。

questions.q: Which conclusion about Cospaia's long independence is best supported by the passage?
options: It maintained a powerful army that deterred invaders.
Its written constitution was respected by its neighbors.
Florence formally protected it for four centuries.
Neither neighbor had a clear legal claim, and its trade served outside smugglers.
questions.why: 因雙方皆無明確法理主權,加上走私貿易的利益,可合理推論其得以長久獨立。

questions.q: Why does the author quote the republic's motto at the end of the passage?
options: To capture the spirit of Cospaia's accidental and cherished freedom
To prove the republic possessed a formal written constitution
To criticize the villagers for their lawlessness
To explain the method by which the republic collected taxes
questions.why: 作者引用格言,是為呼應並凸顯Cospaia珍視意外得來的自由。

academic.title: The Vanished Voices of the Castrati
academic.passage: For roughly two centuries, the most celebrated voices in European opera belonged to the castrati, male singers castrated before puberty to preserve their high vocal range. The practice emerged in late sixteenth-century Italy, partly because women were forbidden to sing in many churches. By halting the hormonal changes of adolescence, the operation kept the larynx small while the rest of the body continued to grow. The result was a rare combination: a boyish, soprano-like pitch produced by an adult's large chest and powerful lungs. Trained for years in demanding conservatories, the finest castrati commanded extraordinary breath control and agility, and audiences prized their almost superhuman sustained notes. The most famous, Farinelli, earned vast sums and reportedly soothed a melancholic Spanish king by singing the same arias to him nightly for years. Yet the glamour concealed a grim reality. Thousands of boys were mutilated in hopes of stardom, but only a handful ever achieved fame; the rest faced disfigurement and social stigma with no compensating career. As tastes shifted toward naturalism and reformers condemned the cruelty, the practice declined during the nineteenth century. The last known castrato, Alessandro Moreschi, died in 1922, leaving only scratchy early recordings that faintly hint at a vanished sound.

questions.q: According to the passage, why did the practice of creating castrati emerge in Italy?
options: Because male singers were universally considered more talented than women.
Partly because women were forbidden to sing in many churches.
Because kings demanded ever higher voices for their court music.
Because the operation was believed to improve overall health.
questions.why: 文中明言此風氣部分源於許多教堂禁止女性歌唱。

questions.q: The word 'prized' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: prize 作動詞意為珍視、看重,與 highly valued 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that a castrato's unusual voice resulted from
options: years of breathing exercises alone, without surgery
a naturally occurring and very rare genetic condition
an operation performed only in adulthood
a small larynx combined with an adult body's lung power
questions.why: 文中描述小喉部搭配成人胸腔與肺活量,可推知其嗓音成因。

questions.q: Which statement best reflects the contrast the author draws about the castrati?
options: A few achieved fame while thousands suffered mutilation without reward.
Their voices were admired yet technically rather weak.
They were popular in churches but banned from the opera house.
They earned very little money despite their enormous fame.
questions.why: 作者對比少數成名者與數千名受害卻無回報者,凸顯光鮮背後的殘酷。

questions.q: Why does the author mention Alessandro Moreschi at the end of the passage?
options: To prove that castrati still perform in opera today
To show the practice has ended, leaving only faint recorded traces
To argue that early recordings increased the castrati's fame
To identify the single most talented castrato in history
questions.why: 提到最後一位閹伶及其模糊錄音,是要說明此傳統已消亡、僅存微弱痕跡。

academic.title: The Stone Money of Yap
academic.passage: On the Micronesian island of Yap, one of the world's most unusual currencies still commands respect: the rai, enormous doughnut-shaped disks of limestone that can exceed three meters across and weigh several tons. Because Yap lacks suitable stone, islanders once quarried the limestone on distant Palau, hundreds of kilometers away, and ferried the heavy disks home on fragile canoes and rafts. The peril of the voyage, not merely the size of the stone, determined a rai's value; a disk whose transport had cost lives was especially prized. What makes the rai remarkable to economists is that ownership, rather than physical possession, defined wealth. A stone was rarely moved after arrival. When it changed hands in a transaction, the community simply acknowledged the new owner, while the disk itself stayed in place, sometimes leaning against a neighbor's house. In one celebrated case, a large rai sank to the sea floor during a storm, yet everyone agreed the drowned stone was just as valid, and its owner continued to trade with it. This system depended entirely on shared memory and collective consensus about who owned what. Modern economists often cite the rai as a striking illustration that money is fundamentally a social ledger of agreed claims, not the physical object itself.

questions.q: According to the passage, what primarily determined the value of a rai stone?
options: Its color and the smoothness of its polished surface
The number of times it had already changed owners
The danger and cost of transporting it from Palau
Its exact weight as measured upon arrival
questions.why: 文中指出航程的危險程度,而非僅大小,決定石幣的價值。

questions.q: The word 'peril' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: peril 意為危險,與 danger 意思相同。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the sunken rai retained its value because
options: the community still agreed on who owned it, even unseen
skilled divers could recover it again whenever needed
a replacement stone was quickly carved to match it
the seawater actually improved the limestone's quality
questions.why: 沉沒石幣仍有效,可推知價值來自社群對所有權的共識,而非實體持有。

questions.q: The example of the sunken stone functions in the passage to support the claim that
options: sea storms were the main threat to Yap's economy
ownership, not physical possession, defined wealth
rai stones were poorly made and often fragile
islanders came to prefer metal coins to stone money
questions.why: 沉石案例用來佐證「所有權而非實體佔有決定財富」的論點。

questions.q: Why do modern economists cite the rai, according to the passage?
options: To recommend a return to stone-based currencies
To demonstrate that heavy forms of money are impractical
To illustrate that money is a social ledger of agreed claims
To explain how limestone forms in nature over time
questions.why: 經濟學家引用石幣,是為說明貨幣本質是被認可的社會帳本。

academic.title: How Tardigrades Cheat Death
academic.passage: Tardigrades, often called water bears, are microscopic invertebrates renowned for surviving conditions that would kill almost any other animal. Barely half a millimeter long, they inhabit mosses, soils, and sediments worldwide. Their fame rests on an extraordinary ability called cryptobiosis, a reversible state in which their metabolism slows to less than one thousandth of normal, appearing almost completely halted. When their surroundings dry out, tardigrades expel most of their body water, retract their legs, and curl into a shriveled husk known as a tun. In this dormant form they can endure temperatures near absolute zero, the vacuum and radiation of open space, and pressures far greater than those in the deepest ocean. Researchers believe a special sugar and unique proteins replace the lost water, forming a glassy matrix that cushions delicate cellular structures until moisture returns. Once rehydrated, a tun can revive within hours and resume feeding and reproducing as though nothing had happened, sometimes after years or even decades of dormancy. Importantly, cryptobiosis is not the same as ordinary sleep or hibernation, which still require active metabolism. By essentially suspending life itself, tardigrades challenge our very definitions of what it means to be alive, and their resilience now inspires research into preserving vaccines, crops, and human cells without refrigeration.

questions.q: According to the passage, what happens to tardigrades when their environment dries out?
options: They swim away to find a wetter location.
They lay tough eggs that survive the drought.
They increase their metabolism to fight the heat.
They expel most of their water and curl into a tun.
questions.why: 文中說乾旱時牠們排出大部分水分並蜷縮成桶狀。

questions.q: The word 'dormant' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: dormant 意為休眠、不活動,與 inactive 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the special sugar and proteins are important because they
options: provide a food supply during the dormant period
protect cellular structures in place of the lost water
attract moisture directly from the surrounding air
allow the tardigrade to crawl much faster
questions.why: 糖與蛋白質形成玻璃態基質保護細胞,可推知其代替水分保護結構的作用。

questions.q: Why does the author distinguish cryptobiosis from sleep and hibernation?
options: To show that cryptobiosis lasts a much shorter time
To prove that tardigrades never truly wake up again
Because sleep and hibernation still require active metabolism, unlike cryptobiosis
Because hibernation is far more dangerous than cryptobiosis
questions.why: 作者區分二者,因睡眠與冬眠仍需活躍代謝,而隱生幾近停止代謝。

questions.q: The final sentence about vaccines and crops mainly serves to
options: warn readers about the dangers posed by tardigrades
explain in detail how tardigrades reproduce
describe the natural habitat of the tardigrade
suggest practical applications inspired by tardigrade resilience
questions.why: 末句提到疫苗與作物,是點出水熊蟲韌性所啟發的實際應用。

academic.title: The Strange World of Superfluid Helium
academic.passage: When helium is cooled to about two degrees above absolute zero, it undergoes a transformation unlike anything in everyday experience: it becomes a superfluid, a liquid with essentially zero viscosity. Ordinary fluids resist flow because their molecules drag against one another and against container walls. Superfluid helium, by contrast, flows without any friction at all. This bizarre behavior arises from quantum mechanics operating on a visible scale. Below the so-called lambda point, a large fraction of the helium atoms condense into a single shared quantum state, moving in perfect unison as though they were one entity. The consequences border on the surreal. A superfluid can creep up the inside wall of a beaker, form a thin invisible film, and flow over the rim to escape, defying gravity in slow motion. If stirred, it can rotate endlessly without slowing, since there is no friction to dissipate its motion. It also conducts heat so efficiently that it never bubbles the way a boiling pot does. Physicists study superfluidity not merely for its strangeness but because it reveals how quantum rules, usually confined to individual atoms, can govern matter we can actually see and pour. The same underlying principle, known as Bose-Einstein condensation, links superfluids to superconductors, in which electricity likewise flows without any resistance.

questions.q: According to the passage, what defining property does superfluid helium have?
options: It flows with essentially zero viscosity, meaning no friction.
It hardens into a transparent solid crystal.
It glows with a faint visible light.
It expands violently the moment it is cooled.
questions.why: 文中指超流體氦的定義特性是幾乎零黏滯、無摩擦地流動。

questions.q: The word 'dissipate' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: dissipate 指能量逐漸消散,與 gradually lose 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a superfluid can climb up a beaker's wall because
options: it is somehow lighter than the surrounding air
the beaker is being strongly heated from below
it experiences no friction to hold it back
it is magnetically attracted to the glass
questions.why: 因無摩擦阻力,可推知超流體能沿杯壁攀爬並越過杯緣。

questions.q: The mention of superconductors at the end mainly serves to
options: contrast superfluids with ordinary everyday liquids
prove that superfluids can themselves carry electricity
explain the reason helium must first be cooled
show that the same quantum principle governs another frictionless phenomenon
questions.why: 提到超導體,是說明同一量子原理也支配另一種無阻力現象。

questions.q: Why do physicists study superfluidity, according to the passage?
options: Because it reveals how quantum rules can govern visible, pourable matter
Because it offers a cheap and abundant source of energy
Because it helps cool ordinary household refrigerators
Because it is the only known liquid form of helium
questions.why: 物理學家研究它,是因它揭示量子規則能支配肉眼可見、可傾倒的物質。

academic.title: The Limits of Human Friendship
academic.passage: In the early 1990s, the anthropologist Robin Dunbar noticed a striking correlation across primate species: the larger a species' neocortex, the bigger the social groups its members maintained. Reasoning that the brain's capacity for processing relationships sets a ceiling on group size, he extrapolated from human brain measurements to predict a limit for our own species. The figure he arrived at, roughly 150, has become known as Dunbar's number. It represents not the total people one can recognize, but the maximum number of stable social relationships an individual can actively maintain, meaning connections in which one knows each person and how they relate to everyone else. Dunbar supported the estimate with an eclectic range of evidence, from the typical size of hunter-gatherer bands and early farming villages to the basic units of professional armies and the average number of holiday cards a household sends. Beyond about 150, he argued, groups tend to require formal rules, hierarchies, and enforcement to stay cohesive, because informal bonds alone no longer suffice. The idea has proved influential yet contentious. Critics note that the correlation is loose and the human figure highly uncertain, and that modern communication may stretch our networks. Still, Dunbar's number endures as a provocative reminder that human sociality may have measurable cognitive limits.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does Dunbar's number of about 150 represent?
options: The total number of faces a person can ever recognize
The maximum number of stable relationships one can actively maintain
The average population of a modern city neighborhood
The number of primates found in a typical troop
questions.why: 文中明言此數指個人能主動維繫的穩定社會關係上限。

questions.q: The word 'eclectic' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: eclectic 指取材多元、來源廣泛,與 drawn from diverse sources 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that groups larger than 150 tend to need formal rules because
options: their members generally come to dislike one another
national governments require such rules by law
larger groups always possess much greater wealth
informal personal bonds alone can no longer hold them together
questions.why: 超過150需正式規則,可推知因非正式的人際連結已不足以維繫凝聚。

questions.q: The evidence about holiday cards and army units is presented mainly to
options: provide varied real-world support for the proposed limit
prove that Dunbar's number is exactly 150
show that armies are always larger than villages
criticize the weakness of Dunbar's methodology
questions.why: 賀卡與軍隊等例子,是為此上限提供多樣的現實佐證。

questions.q: Why does the author mention the critics near the end of the passage?
options: To reject Dunbar's number entirely as worthless
To acknowledge that the idea is influential but disputed
To prove that modern technology has no effect at all
To show that Dunbar later abandoned his own theory
questions.why: 提及批評者,是要平衡指出此概念雖具影響力卻仍有爭議。

academic.title: The Hidden Waterworks of Petra
academic.passage: The rose-colored city of Petra, carved into the cliffs of southern Jordan, is celebrated for its dramatic facades, yet its true genius lay hidden underground. The Nabataeans, the once-nomadic Arab people who built Petra, transformed an arid desert canyon into a thriving metropolis of perhaps twenty thousand inhabitants by mastering the one resource the region lacked: water. Their homeland received scarcely fifteen centimeters of rain a year, most of it in brief, violent bursts that could trigger deadly flash floods. Rather than fear this water, the Nabataeans captured it. They cut networks of channels, pipes, and cisterns into the rock to harvest every drop of seasonal runoff and store it for the long dry months. Ceramic pipes were engineered with a precise gradient that kept water moving at an ideal speed, fast enough to prevent stagnation yet slow enough to avoid bursting the joints. Dams and diversion tunnels redirected floodwater away from the city center, protecting inhabitants while replenishing reservoirs. This hidden infrastructure allowed Petra to flourish as a wealthy hub of the caravan trade in incense and spices. When the trade routes later shifted and Roman power absorbed the kingdom, the city gradually declined, and its ingenious waterworks fell silent, leaving later visitors to marvel only at the empty tombs carved above.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did the Nabataeans make Petra habitable?
options: By importing fresh water from distant rivers
By carving elaborate tombs into the cliffs
By capturing and storing seasonal rainfall and runoff
By relocating the city entirely during the dry season
questions.why: 文中指出他們透過收集並儲存季節性降雨與逕流,使佩特拉宜居。

questions.q: The word 'arid' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: arid 意為乾旱,與 very dry 意思相同。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the precise gradient of the ceramic pipes was important because
options: it balanced the flow to prevent both stagnation and bursting
it made the ceramic pipes cheaper to manufacture
it allowed small boats to travel through them
it kept the stored water permanently frozen
questions.why: 管道精準坡度兼防淤積與爆裂,可推知其在調節流速上的重要性。

questions.q: According to the passage, what was the relationship between Petra's water system and its prosperity?
options: The costly water system steadily drained the city's wealth.
The hidden waterworks enabled Petra to thrive as a trade hub.
Prosperity arrived only after the water system had failed.
The two developments were entirely unrelated to each other.
questions.why: 隱藏的水利系統使佩特拉得以繁榮為貿易樞紐,兩者為因果關係。

questions.q: Why does the author contrast the dramatic facades with what lay hidden underground?
options: To argue that the famous facades were poorly built
To suggest that the tombs concealed great treasure
To emphasize that Petra's real achievement was its unseen engineering
To explain why modern tourists tend to avoid the city
questions.why: 對比華麗立面與地下設施,是強調佩特拉真正的成就在於看不見的工程。

academic.title: One Throat, Two Voices
academic.passage: In the grasslands of Tuva, a small republic in southern Siberia, herders practice an astonishing vocal art known as khoomei, or throat singing. A single performer produces two or more pitches simultaneously: a low, sustained drone and, floating above it, one or more clear whistling tones that seem to belong to a separate instrument. The effect can sound eerily like wind, flowing water, or a distant flute, and first-time listeners often struggle to believe it comes from one human throat. The technique works by manipulating harmonics, the faint higher frequencies naturally present in any sung note. By precisely shaping the tongue, lips, and throat, a skilled singer amplifies a chosen harmonic until it stands out as a distinct melody above the fundamental drone. Tuvan tradition holds that the style arose from an intimate relationship with the natural landscape; singers historically imitated the sounds of their environment as a form of communion with it. Different styles evoke different textures, from a deep growl to a bright, birdlike shimmer. Long dismissed by outsiders as a mere curiosity, khoomei gained international recognition in the late twentieth century, when recordings and touring ensembles introduced it to global audiences. Today it is studied by acousticians and celebrated as an intangible cultural heritage, a living link between the human voice and the open steppe.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is remarkable about khoomei?
options: It requires several singers performing closely together.
It can only be performed alongside instruments.
It is always sung at an extremely high pitch.
A single singer produces two or more pitches at once.
questions.why: 文中指出一名歌者能同時發出兩個以上的音高,這正是其驚人之處。

questions.q: The word 'eerily' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: with a great deal of joy
questions.why: eerily 指以怪異、令人不安的方式,與 strange, unsettling 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the whistling tones in khoomei are produced by
options: a small wind instrument hidden by the singer
amplifying particular harmonics already present in the voice
two singers rapidly alternating their notes
questions.why: 由放大既有泛音的說明,可推知哨音來自對嗓音中泛音的強化。

questions.q: The Tuvan belief that the style imitates nature is presented in order to
options: prove that khoomei is scientifically inaccurate
show that singers actually disliked their environment
explain the cultural origin and meaning of the practice
argue that natural sounds are superior to singing
questions.why: 圖瓦人模仿自然的說法,是用來解釋此藝術的文化起源與意義。

questions.q: Why does the author mention the late twentieth century?
options: To show that khoomei was first invented at that time
To explain why khoomei was officially banned
To prove that throat singing is easy to learn
To mark when khoomei gained wider international recognition
questions.why: 提到二十世紀晚期,是標示喉音唱法獲得更廣泛國際認識的時點。

academic.title: The Merchant Network That Ruled the North
academic.passage: Long before nation-states dominated Europe, a loose confederation of merchant towns quietly controlled the trade of the northern seas. The Hanseatic League, which flourished from the thirteenth to the fifteenth century, began as informal agreements among traders seeking mutual protection along the Baltic and North Sea coasts. Over time it grew into a powerful network linking as many as two hundred towns, from Novgorod in the east to London and Bruges in the west. The League possessed no king, capital, or standing army, and no written constitution bound its members. Instead, it operated through shared commercial interest, periodic assemblies, and a common body of trading customs. Its merchants secured exclusive privileges in foreign ports, established fortified trading posts called kontors, and enforced their will through collective bargaining and, when necessary, trade embargoes that could cripple an uncooperative city. This economic solidarity gave the League remarkable leverage; it even waged and won a war against the king of Denmark, dictating the peace terms. Yet the same looseness that made it flexible eventually undermined it. As centralized monarchies grew stronger and Atlantic trade routes drew commerce westward, individual towns increasingly pursued their own advantage. Lacking any binding political structure, the League slowly dissolved, a reminder that economic power without formal institutions can prove impressively effective yet ultimately fragile.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did the Hanseatic League primarily operate?
options: Through shared commercial interest, assemblies, and common trading customs
Through a single powerful king and a written constitution
Through a large standing army based in a fixed capital city
