Through direct control by the Danish monarchy
questions.why: 文中說聯盟靠共同商業利益、集會與貿易慣例運作,而非國王或憲法。

questions.q: The word 'leverage' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: leverage 在此指影響力或談判籌碼,與 bargaining power 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the League's trade embargoes were effective because
options: they were always ordered directly by the Pope
each member town maintained a large army
a targeted city depended on League trade and could be economically pressured
they physically destroyed the rival city's ports
questions.why: 禁運有效,可推知因目標城市依賴聯盟貿易,故能承受經濟施壓。

questions.q: According to the passage, what ultimately caused the League's decline?
options: A single sudden military defeat by Denmark
The invention of entirely new trading customs
The loss of all its member towns to disease
Stronger monarchies and shifting trade routes, with no binding structure to hold it together
questions.why: 衰落肇因於中央集權王國崛起、貿易路線西移,加上缺乏約束性結構。

questions.q: The final sentence of the passage mainly serves to
options: draw a broader lesson about economic power without formal institutions
predict that the League will soon be revived
list all of the towns belonging to the League
explain the method by which kontors were built
questions.why: 末句旨在提煉出「缺乏正式制度的經濟力量終究脆弱」的普遍教訓。

academic.title: The Wood Wide Web
academic.passage: Beneath the floor of a healthy forest lies a hidden communication system so intricate that scientists have nicknamed it the wood wide web. It is built not from cables but from fungi. The threadlike filaments of mycorrhizal fungi wrap around and penetrate plant roots, forming a partnership hundreds of millions of years old. In this exchange, the fungi absorb water and mineral nutrients such as phosphorus from the soil and deliver them to the plant, while the plant repays them with sugars produced through photosynthesis. Because a single fungal network can connect many trees at once, it effectively links separate plants into one interwoven system. Researchers using radioactive and chemical tracers have shown that carbon, nutrients, and even chemical warning signals can travel through these fungal threads from one tree to another. A large, well-established tree may channel surplus sugars to shaded seedlings struggling in the understory, and a plant under insect attack may trigger neighboring plants to raise their chemical defenses before the pests arrive. Some scientists interpret this as evidence of cooperation among trees, while others caution that the fungi may be acting in their own interest, steering resources for their own benefit. Whatever the correct interpretation, the discovery has overturned the old image of a forest as merely a crowd of solitary, competing individuals.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do the fungi provide to the plant in the partnership?
options: Water and mineral nutrients such as phosphorus
Complete protection from all insect attacks
questions.why: 文中說真菌提供水分與磷等礦物養分給植物。

questions.q: The word 'intricate' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: intricate 意為錯綜複雜,與 highly complex 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a shaded seedling may benefit from the network because
options: the fungi block sunlight from reaching taller trees
seedlings produce far more sugar than adult trees do
the network keeps all insects away completely
a large tree can channel surplus sugars to it through the fungi
questions.why: 大樹可經真菌輸送多餘糖分,可推知遮蔭幼苗因此受益。

questions.q: The passage presents two interpretations of the network mainly to
options: show that scientists disagree about whether it reflects true cooperation
prove that trees are entirely selfish organisms
argue that the fungi are harmful to the forest
demonstrate that the network does not really exist
questions.why: 並陳兩種詮釋,是顯示科學家對其是否代表合作仍有分歧。

questions.q: The final sentence of the passage mainly serves to
options: warn readers that the world's forests are dying
show that the discovery has changed how we view forests
explain the chemical steps of photosynthesis
describe the exact color of the fungal threads
questions.why: 末句旨在說明此發現改變了我們把森林視為孤立個體的舊觀念。

academic.title: The Reactor That Ran Itself
academic.passage: In 1972, analysts at a French nuclear facility noticed something apparently impossible in a shipment of uranium ore from Oklo, in the West African nation of Gabon. The ore contained slightly less of the fissile isotope uranium-235 than every other uranium sample on Earth, as though some had already been consumed in a reactor. Suspecting theft of enriched material, investigators traced the ore to its source, only to uncover a far stranger explanation. Roughly two billion years ago, the deposits at Oklo had operated as natural nuclear reactors, sustaining chain reactions entirely without human involvement. The conditions were extraordinarily precise. At that ancient time, the proportion of uranium-235 in natural uranium was much higher than today, high enough to support a self-sustaining reaction. Groundwater seeping into the rich ore acted as a moderator, slowing neutrons so they could split more uranium atoms. As the reaction heated the rock, the water boiled away, halting the process; when the rock cooled, water returned and the reaction resumed. This self-regulating cycle pulsed on and off for hundreds of thousands of years. The Oklo discovery is more than a curiosity. Because the ancient reactors trapped their radioactive waste in place for two billion years, scientists study the site closely for lessons about safely storing nuclear waste today.

questions.q: According to the passage, what first alerted analysts to something unusual about the Oklo ore?
options: It was far more radioactive than any normal ore.
It held less uranium-235 than any other sample on Earth.
It was found mixed together with enriched fuel.
questions.why: 文中說礦石中的鈾-235比地球上任何樣本都少,這才引起注意。

questions.q: The word 'moderator' in the passage refers to something that
options: mines the uranium from the rock
slows neutrons so they split more atoms
questions.why: 文中的 moderator 指減慢中子、使其更能分裂鈾原子的物質。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the reaction stopped periodically because
options: the heat boiled away the water needed to slow the neutrons
the uranium ran out completely each time
humans manually switched the reactor off
the rock became too cold to hold any uranium
questions.why: 熱使水沸乾,而水是減速中子所需,故可推知反應因此週期性停止。

questions.q: Why was the higher ancient proportion of uranium-235 essential to the Oklo reactors?
options: It made the ore much easier to mine.
Without it, a self-sustaining chain reaction could not occur.
It prevented groundwater from entering the rock.
It kept the radioactive waste from ever spreading.
questions.why: 古代較高的鈾-235比例是自持鏈式反應的必要條件,否則反應無法發生。

questions.q: Why does the author mention nuclear waste storage at the end of the passage?
options: To warn that Oklo is still dangerously active today
To explain in detail how uranium is enriched
To show that the site offers practical lessons for storing modern waste
To argue that natural reactors should be deliberately rebuilt
questions.why: 提到核廢料儲存,是說明該遺址對現今安全封存廢料具實用啟示。

academic.title: Rethinking the Tragedy of the Commons
academic.passage: For decades, economists warned of an apparently inescapable trap called the tragedy of the commons. The argument held that any resource shared by many users, such as a communal pasture, fishery, or forest, would inevitably be overexploited, because each user gains the full benefit of taking more while the cost of depletion is spread across everyone. The standard conclusion was stark: only private ownership or top-down government control could prevent ruin. The political scientist Elinor Ostrom challenged this pessimism with painstaking fieldwork. Studying communities around the world, from Swiss alpine pastures to Japanese forests and Philippine irrigation systems, she documented numerous cases in which ordinary people had sustainably managed shared resources for centuries without either privatization or state control. What these successful communities had in common, she found, were certain design principles. Members clearly defined who had rights to the resource, crafted rules suited to local conditions, monitored one another, and imposed graduated penalties on those who overused it. Crucially, the users themselves participated in making and revising the rules, which gave the arrangements both legitimacy and flexibility. Ostrom's work, which earned her a Nobel Prize in 2009, did not claim that communities always succeed; many do collapse. Rather, it demonstrated that self-governance is a genuine third option, dissolving the false choice between the market and the state.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did the tragedy of the commons argument predict?
options: That shared resources would always be well managed
That governments would refuse to regulate resources
That private owners would neglect their own land
That shared resources would inevitably be overexploited
questions.why: 文中說該論點預測共享資源必然遭到過度開發。

questions.q: The word 'painstaking' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: painstaking 指細心且徹底的,與 careful and thorough 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that user participation in rule-making mattered because it
options: reduced the number of users allowed in
gave the rules both legitimacy and flexibility
eliminated the need for any monitoring at all
transferred final control to the government
questions.why: 使用者參與訂規賦予規則正當性與彈性,可由文中推知其重要性。

questions.q: Ostrom's field studies function in the passage to
options: confirm that only privatization can ever work
prove that every community manages resources well
provide counterexamples that challenge the standard pessimistic conclusion
show that government control is always the best option
questions.why: 她的田野研究提供反例,用以挑戰傳統悲觀結論。

questions.q: Why does the author note that many communities do collapse?
options: To argue that self-governance never actually works
To prove that Ostrom's theory was rejected
To clarify that Ostrom did not claim communities always succeed
questions.why: 指出許多社群仍會失敗,是澄清歐斯壯並未主張自治必然成功。

academic.title: The Dancing Plague of 1518
academic.passage: In July 1518, in the city of Strasbourg, a woman known as Frau Troffea stepped into a narrow street and began to dance. She did not stop for days. Within a week dozens had joined her, and within a month the afflicted reportedly numbered around four hundred, dancing uncontrollably in the summer heat. Contemporary records suggest that some collapsed from exhaustion, and a number may have died from strokes or heart failure brought on by relentless exertion. The city's alarmed authorities consulted physicians, who concluded, in keeping with the medical theories of the day, that the mania was caused by overheated blood. Their prescribed cure was strikingly counterintuitive: they decided the sufferers should dance the affliction out of their systems. Officials cleared guild halls, hired musicians, and even paid dancers to accompany the victims, unintentionally worsening the spectacle. Historians still debate the cause. Some have proposed poisoning by ergot, a toxic mold on damp rye that can produce convulsions, though ergot rarely permits sustained, coordinated movement. Many scholars now favor a psychological explanation: a mass stress-induced disorder, triggered by a year of famine, disease, and dread, spreading through suggestion in a population steeped in fears of divine punishment. Whatever its origin, the episode remains one of history's most bizarre documented outbreaks of collective behavior.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did the Strasbourg authorities initially respond to the dancing?
options: They arranged for musicians and dancers to help sufferers dance it out.
They immediately banned all dancing throughout the city.
They ignored the strange outbreak entirely.
They ordered the execution of those who danced.
questions.why: 文中說當局雇樂師與舞者,想讓患者把病跳出來。

questions.q: The word 'counterintuitive' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: contrary to what one would expect
questions.why: counterintuitive 指與直覺或預期相反,與 contrary to what one would expect 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that many scholars doubt the ergot explanation because
options: ergot did not exist anywhere in that region
no rye at all was grown near Strasbourg
ergot poisoning rarely allows sustained, coordinated movement
the dancers were later proven to be merely pretending
questions.why: 麥角中毒難以維持協調動作,可推知學者因此質疑該說法。

questions.q: The mention of a year of famine and disease supports the idea that the outbreak was
options: caused entirely by contaminated drinking water
a deliberate and organized political protest
a religious festival that had gone badly wrong
possibly a stress-induced mass psychological disorder
questions.why: 提到饑荒與疾病之年,是支持此事件可能是壓力引發的集體心理失調。

questions.q: Why does the author present multiple explanations for the event?
options: To show that its cause remains genuinely debated
To prove that ergot poisoning was the certain cause
To argue that the event never actually happened
questions.why: 並列多種解釋,是為呈現其成因至今仍真正存有爭論。

academic.title: The Monk Who Made Music Readable
academic.passage: Before the eleventh century, Western church music was passed down almost entirely by ear. A singer might spend a decade memorizing the vast repertoire of chants, since no reliable method existed to record precise pitch on the page. Early notational marks, called neumes, could remind a singer of a melody already known but could not teach a new one. This laborious system was transformed by Guido of Arezzo, an Italian monk and music teacher. Frustrated by the years his pupils wasted, Guido refined the idea of the musical staff, using horizontal lines and the spaces between them to fix the exact pitch of each note according to its vertical position. A note's height on the staff now told a singer precisely how high or low to sing, allowing a musician to read and perform an unfamiliar melody accurately at first sight. Guido also devised a system of syllables to name the notes of a scale, assigning each a memorable name drawn from a well-known hymn. These syllables, later adjusted, survive today as the familiar do, re, mi that music students still learn. His innovations spread rapidly through European monasteries, drastically shortening the time needed to train singers. By making music legible, Guido laid the groundwork for the complex compositions of later centuries, when writing down elaborate multi-voice works finally became possible at all.

questions.q: According to the passage, how was church music mainly transmitted before the eleventh century?
options: Almost entirely by ear and memorization
Through Guido's newly invented staff notation
By means of the do, re, mi syllable system
questions.why: 文中說十一世紀前教會音樂幾乎全靠口耳相傳與背誦。

questions.q: The word 'laborious' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: laborious 指費力耗時的,與 requiring much effort 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that neumes were inadequate because they
options: were far too expensive to print
could only be read by senior monks
could remind singers of known tunes but not teach new ones
questions.why: 紐姆譜只能提醒已知旋律,無法教授新曲,可推知其不足之處。

questions.q: How does the musical staff solve the central problem described in the passage?
options: A note's vertical position fixes its exact pitch, so unfamiliar melodies can be sung at sight.
It makes each chant shorter and thus easier to memorize.
It replaces human singers entirely with instruments.
It removes the need for any training whatsoever.
questions.why: 五線譜以音符的垂直位置固定音高,故能視譜唱出陌生旋律,正解決核心難題。

questions.q: Why does the author mention do, re, mi at the end of the passage?
options: To prove that Guido himself could not read music
To show that Guido's influence still survives in modern music teaching
To argue that syllables completely replaced the staff
To explain why the old chants were eventually forgotten
questions.why: 提到 do, re, mi,是顯示圭多的影響至今仍存於現代音樂教學中。

academic.title: The Fairs That Financed Europe
academic.passage: During the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, the rural county of Champagne in northeastern France became, for a few weeks at a time, the beating heart of the European economy. A rotating cycle of six great fairs, held in four towns across the year, drew merchants from Flanders, Italy, Spain, and beyond. Flemish cloth, Italian silks and spices, and goods from across the continent changed hands in a carefully organized sequence of trading and settlement periods. The fairs owed much of their success to the local counts, who understood that protection was more profitable than plunder. They guaranteed the safety of merchants traveling to and from the fairs, enforced contracts through special fair courts, and standardized weights and measures, reducing the risks that otherwise plagued long-distance trade. Perhaps most importantly, the fairs became centers of finance as well as of goods. Italian merchants developed instruments of credit that allowed a debt incurred at one fair to be settled at another, so that traders no longer had to haul dangerous quantities of coin across bandit-ridden roads. In effect, the fairs helped pioneer the practices of international banking. Their decline in the fourteenth century came as sea routes opened between Italy and the north and as war disrupted the overland roads, but the commercial techniques nurtured there long outlived the fairs themselves.

questions.q: According to the passage, what did the counts of Champagne do to support the fairs?
options: They banned all foreign merchants from attending.
They minted a single unified European coin.
They guaranteed merchants' safety and enforced contracts.
They built the ships used by the Italian traders.
questions.why: 文中說伯爵保障商人安全並透過特別法庭執行契約。

questions.q: The word 'plagued' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: plague 作動詞意為困擾、折磨,與 troubled or afflicted 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the credit instruments were valuable because they
options: let merchants avoid transporting large amounts of coin on dangerous roads
eliminated the need for any trade in goods at all
made the fairs last much longer each year
replaced cloth as the main product being sold
questions.why: 信用工具讓商人免於在危險道路上運送大量錢幣,可推知其價值所在。

questions.q: What was the relationship between the counts' policies and the fairs' prosperity?
options: The policies had essentially no effect on the fairs.
By reducing trade risks, the policies helped the fairs flourish.
The policies drove most merchants away from Champagne.
Prosperity forced the counts to abandon their policies.
questions.why: 伯爵的政策降低貿易風險,從而促成市集繁榮,兩者為因果關係。

questions.q: The final sentence of the passage mainly emphasizes that
options: the fairs were never truly important to Europe
sea routes were more dangerous than the overland roads
the financial techniques outlasted the fairs that produced them
the wars of the fourteenth century had no real effect
questions.why: 末句強調市集孕育的金融技術比市集本身更為長久。

academic.title: The Snapping Weapon of the Pistol Shrimp
academic.passage: Few creatures pack as much power into so small a body as the pistol shrimp, a crustacean rarely longer than a few centimeters. Its secret is a grossly oversized claw that can account for half the animal's total length. When the shrimp snaps this claw shut, it does not strike its prey directly. Instead, the claw closes so violently that it ejects a jet of water at astonishing speed. The water moves so fast that the pressure within it drops sharply, and a tiny bubble of vapor forms, a phenomenon known as cavitation. An instant later, the surrounding pressure crushes the bubble, which collapses with tremendous force. This collapse unleashes a shockwave powerful enough to stun or kill small prey nearby, and it briefly generates a flash of light and a spot of heat estimated to approach the temperature of the sun's surface, though only for a fleeting moment in a microscopic space. The accompanying snap is one of the loudest sounds in the ocean; colonies of these shrimp can produce a crackle so constant that it once interfered with naval sonar. Engineers now study the shrimp's claw for insights into managing cavitation, which ordinarily damages ship propellers and pumps. In this tiny animal, a destructive force that engineers struggle to control has been refined by evolution into a precise weapon.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does the pistol shrimp stun its prey?
options: By biting it repeatedly with sharp teeth
By injecting venom directly through its claw
By striking the prey directly with its heavy claw
By snapping its claw to create a collapsing bubble that produces a shockwave
questions.why: 文中說蝦以巨螯瞬閉噴出水流,形成並崩解氣泡,產生震波擊昏獵物。

questions.q: The word 'grossly' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: grossly 在此作副詞強調程度,意為極其,與 extremely 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that cavitation forms in the water jet because
options: the water is heated before it leaves the claw
the water's high speed sharply lowers its pressure
the shrimp exhales air into the surrounding water
the frightened prey releases gas into the water
questions.why: 水流高速使壓力驟降而生成氣泡,可推知空穴現象的成因。

questions.q: The mention of naval sonar mainly serves to
options: prove that pistol shrimp deliberately attack submarines
explain in detail how sonar equipment is built
illustrate how loud the shrimp's snap can be
show that the shrimp live only near naval bases
questions.why: 提到海軍聲納,是用以說明蝦的爆響有多麼響亮。

questions.q: Why does the author mention engineers at the end of the passage?
options: To warn that pistol shrimp seriously damage ships
To explain how the shrimp are caught for food
To argue that cavitation is completely harmless
To show that the shrimp's natural control of cavitation interests human technology
questions.why: 提到工程師,是顯示蝦天然駕馭空穴現象的能力引起人類科技的興趣。

academic.title: Turning Sound into Light
academic.passage: One of the more mysterious phenomena in physics can be produced in an ordinary flask of water using nothing but sound. If a small bubble of gas is trapped in a liquid and bathed in an intense sound field of the right frequency, the bubble will pulse in rhythm with the sound waves, expanding and then collapsing violently thousands of times per second. At the instant of each collapse, the bubble emits a minuscule flash of light. This astonishing conversion of sound into light is called sonoluminescence. The flashes are extraordinarily brief, lasting mere trillionths of a second, yet they recur with such regularity that a single trapped bubble can be made to blink steadily for long periods. What puzzles physicists most is the source of the light. During the rapid collapse, the gas inside the bubble is compressed so suddenly that its temperature soars to tens of thousands of degrees, hotter than the surface of the sun, concentrated in a space smaller than a speck of dust. Exactly what happens in that extreme, fleeting instant remains incompletely understood, and competing theories invoke shockwaves, ionized gas, or other mechanisms. Because the collapse briefly creates such concentrated energy, some researchers have investigated whether the phenomenon could ever drive chemical reactions or other processes that demand extraordinary local conditions.

questions.q: According to the passage, what produces sonoluminescence?
options: A trapped gas bubble collapsing under an intense sound field emits light.
A powerful laser heating water until it begins to glow.
Two separate bubbles merging into one larger bubble.
Sound waves reflecting off a polished mirror.
questions.why: 文中說被困氣泡在強聲場下劇烈崩解時發出光,即聲致發光。

questions.q: The word 'minuscule' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: minuscule 意為極小的,與 extremely small 意思相同。

questions.q: It can be inferred that the gas inside the bubble becomes extremely hot because
options: the surrounding water is already boiling
the sound waves carry heat into it directly
it is compressed very suddenly during the collapse
light from the flash warms it from within
questions.why: 氣體因崩解時被瞬間壓縮而升溫,可由文中推知其高溫成因。

questions.q: The statement that the cause of the light remains incompletely understood is supported by
options: the claim that the bubble never actually collapses
the fact that the flashes are brightly colored
the great regularity of the bubble's blinking
the mention of competing theories about the mechanism
questions.why: 並存的多種相互競爭理論,支持了發光機制仍未完全理解的說法。

questions.q: Why does the author mention driving chemical reactions at the end of the passage?
options: To suggest a possible practical use of the concentrated energy
To prove that sonoluminescence is highly dangerous
To explain the reason bubbles form in water
To argue that the phenomenon has no applications at all
questions.why: 提到驅動化學反應,是為指出這股集中能量可能的實際用途。

academic.title: The Gift Economy of the Kula Ring
academic.passage: Among the Trobriand Islands off the coast of New Guinea, islanders undertake perilous ocean voyages by canoe to exchange two kinds of objects that have no practical use whatsoever. In a vast ceremonial circuit known as the Kula ring, red shell necklaces travel clockwise from island to island, while white shell armbands travel counterclockwise. A man receives a valuable, keeps it for a time, and then passes it onward to a distant partner, who will eventually pass it on again. No one owns these treasures permanently; their worth lies entirely in their movement and their history. A famous necklace, enriched by the reputations of the celebrated men who once held it, confers great prestige on its temporary keeper. When the anthropologist Bronislaw Malinowski documented the practice in the early twentieth century, he argued that it overturned the assumption that so-called primitive peoples engaged only in crude barter driven by immediate need. The Kula was plainly not about material profit, since the objects themselves were useless. Instead, it wove scattered island communities into a durable web of obligation, trust, and alliance. Under the cover of these ceremonial exchanges, ordinary trade in practical goods could also take place safely between partners. The Kula thus revealed how an economy of gifts, rather than markets, can bind a society together across great distances.

questions.q: According to the passage, in which direction do the shell armbands travel in the Kula ring?
options: They do not move at all
questions.why: 文中明言白色貝殼臂環以逆時針方向流動。

questions.q: The word 'confers' in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.why: confer 在此意為賦予、授予,與 grants or bestows 最接近。

questions.q: It can be inferred that a Kula valuable's worth comes mainly from
options: the practical usefulness of the shells
the amount of money it can be sold for
its permanent ownership by a single chief
its history and continuous movement among partners
questions.why: 貝飾價值來自其歷史與不斷流轉,可由文中推知,而非實用或永久佔有。

questions.q: Malinowski used the Kula ring to argue against the idea that
options: so-called primitive peoples engaged only in crude, need-driven barter
gift exchange can help bind societies together
the Trobriand Islanders were highly skilled sailors
shell objects could carry great social prestige
questions.why: 馬林諾斯基以庫拉圈反駁原始民族僅從事粗糙、需求驅動的以物易物之說。

questions.q: Why does the author note that ordinary trade occurred under the cover of the ceremonial exchanges?
options: To prove the Kula was secretly all about profit
To show the ceremonial system also enabled safe practical trade
To argue that practical goods were valued more than the shells
To explain why the ocean voyages were so dangerous
questions.why: 指出實用貿易伴隨儀式進行,是說明該儀式體系也讓實際交易得以安全進行。
